topics.title: Legislations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: LEGISLATIONS IN ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Legislations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental Legislations** refer to the comprehensive body of statutory laws, rules, notifications, and regulatory frameworks enacted by the Government of India (and State Governments) to protect, conserve, and manage the natural and human environment. In the context of **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)**, these legislations form the **legal backbone** that mandates, governs, and enforces the requirement to assess environmental consequences *before* undertaking developmental projects or activities.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OECHT617)**, Module 1 on "Legislations" covers the statutory framework, key Acts, and procedural notifications that govern EIA studies in India, with emphasis on:
- The **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**
- The **EIA Notification, 2006** (and its 2020 Draft Amendment)
- The **Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) Notification, 2019**
- The **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**
- The **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**
- The **Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972**
- The **Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980**
- The **Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991**
- Relevant **Tribal / Forest Rights Acts**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Traffic Rulebook of a City"**
> 
> Imagine a rapidly growing city where thousands of new buildings, factories, and highways are being constructed every month. Without a rulebook, builders could construct anywhere — inside parks, over rivers, next to schools — causing chaos. To prevent this, the city government creates a **rulebook** (legislations) and a **traffic police** (regulatory bodies like CPCB, SPCB, MoEFCC).
> 
> - The **rulebook** tells you *what you can build, where, and under what conditions*.
> - The **traffic police** checks your plans *before* you start (that's the **EIA process**), issues permissions (**Environmental Clearance**), and punishes violators (penalties under the **EPA, 1986**).
> 
> In India, this "rulebook" of environmental laws is built upon a foundation laid by the **Stockholm Conference (1972)** and crystallized by the **42nd Constitutional Amendment (1976)** inserting **Article 48A** (State's duty to protect environment) and **Article 51A(g)** (Fundamental Duty of citizens).

### 1.3 Key Physical/Statutory Metrics

The following standard statutory thresholds are critical for KTU examinations:

| Parameter | Value / Standard |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Article 48A** (DPSP) | State shall protect & improve environment |
| **Article 51A(g)** | Fundamental Duty to protect environment |
| **EPA, 1986** | Umbrella Act (enacted post-Bhopal Disaster) |
| **EIA Notification Date** | 14 September 2006 (S.O. 1533) |
| **CRZ Notification** | 2019 (replaced 2011) |
| **Forest Conservation Act** | 1980 |
| **Water Act** | 1974 (amended 1988) |
| **Air Act** | 1981 (amended 1987) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 1986 is the "Mother Year" of Indian Environmental Law:**
> The Bhopal Gas Tragedy (December 2–3, 1984) exposed the absence of a comprehensive environmental law. Parliament reacted by enacting the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**, which became the *umbrella legislation* under which all subsequent environmental rules, notifications (including the EIA Notification 2006), and standards are issued.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Constitutional Foundation (Why India Needed These Laws)

1. **Stockholm Conference (1972):** India was among the first nations to recognize global environmental concerns. The conference's outcome triggered national-level legal reforms.
2. **The 42nd Constitutional Amendment (1976):** Embedded environmental protection directly into the Constitution.
   - **Article 48A** (Directive Principles of State Policy): *"The State shall endeavour to protect and improve the environment and to safeguard the forests and wildlife of the country."*
   - **Article 51A(g)** (Fundamental Duties): *"It shall be the duty of every citizen of India to protect and improve the natural environment including forests, lakes, rivers and wildlife, and to have compassion for living creatures."*
3. **Judicial Activism:** Landmark judgments (*Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum*, *MC Mehta cases*, *Godavarman case*) expanded the scope of environmental jurisprudence, introducing the **Polluter Pays Principle** and **Precautionary Principle**.

### 2.2 The Umbrella Act — Environment (Protection) Act, 1986

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept:** The EPA, 1986 is the *most important* legislation for EIA. It is an "umbrella" law because it empowers the Central Government to:
> - Coordinate actions of State Governments
> - Plan and execute nationwide programmes
> - Lay down **environmental quality standards**
> - Prescribe **procedures and safeguards** for handling hazardous substances
> - Issue **notifications** (such as the EIA Notification 2006)

**Salient Features:**
- **Section 3(1):** empowers Central Government to take all necessary measures to protect and improve the environment.
- **Section 3(2)(v):** empowers the government to issue the **EIA Notification** — this is the *legal basis* for requiring EIA for listed projects.
- **Section 5(1):** empowers any person to file a complaint regarding violation of environmental norms.
- **Penalties:** Imprisonment up to **5 years** and/or fine up to **₹1 lakh**, with additional fine of **₹5,000/day** for continuing violations.

### 2.3 Major Legislations — Logical Breakdown

#### A. Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974
- **Purpose:** To prevent and control water pollution and to maintain or restore wholesomeness of water.
- **Key Body:** Created the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** at the national level and **State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs)** at the state level.
- **Key Provisions:**
  - **Section 20:** Establishes CPCB.
  - **Section 24:** Prohibits discharge of pollutants into water bodies beyond prescribed standards.
  - **Section 25/26:** Requires **Consent to Establish (CTE)** and **Consent to Operate (CTO)** from SPCBs.

#### B. Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981
- **Purpose:** To provide for the prevention, control and abatement of air pollution.
- **Amendment 1987:** Expanded scope to include **noise pollution** as an air pollutant.
- **Key Body:** Strengthened the role of CPCB and SPCBs in air quality monitoring.
- **Ambient Air Quality Standards (AAQS):** CPCB issues these standards for **PM2.5, PM10, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, Pb, NH₃, Benzene** under Sections 16 and 17.

#### C. Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972
- **Purpose:** To protect wild animals, birds, and plants; especially endangered species.
- **Key Concept:** Creates a network of **Protected Areas** — **National Parks, Wildlife Sanctuaries, Community Reserves, and Conservation Reserves**.
- **Schedules:** Six schedules providing varying degrees of protection.
- **Schedules I & II:** Strictest protection (hunting prohibited).
- **Project Proximity:** Any project within **10 km** of a National Park/Sanctuary requires clearance from the **National Board for Wildlife (NBWL)** / **Standing Committee of NBWL**.

#### D. Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980
- **Purpose:** To check deforestation and diversion of forest land for non-forest uses.
- **Key Provision:** **No State Government can divert forest land for non-forest use without prior approval of the Central Government.**
- **Amendment 2022:** *Forest (Conservation) Amendment Act, 2023* — exempted certain strategic projects within 100 km of international borders.

#### E. EIA Notification, 2006
- **Issued under:** Section 3(1) and 3(2)(v) of the **EPA, 1986**.
- **Published:** 14 September 2006, vide **S.O. 1533 (E)**.
- **Core Mandate:** Environmental Clearance (EC) is mandatory for **38 categories of projects/activities**.
- **Two-tier classification:**
  - **Category A:** Appraised by the **Expert Appraisal Committee (EAC)** at the Central level (MoEFCC) — for projects in sensitive/border areas or large scale.
  - **Category B:** Appraised at the **State Level Environment Impact Assessment Authority (SEIAA)** with help of **State Expert Appraisal Committee (SEAC)**.
- **Sub-classification of B:** B1 (requires EIA) and B2 (does not require full EIA — only Form 1 and Form 1A).

#### F. Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) Notification, 2019
- **Purpose:** To regulate activities along India's coastline.
- **CRZ Classification:** Four zones (CRZ-I, II, III, IV) based on ecological sensitivity.
- **Authority:** **National Coastal Zone Management Authority (NCZMA)** at Central level; **State/UT Coastal Zone Management Authorities (SCZMAs)** at State level.
- **Key Change in 2019:** Reduced CRZ-III (Rural) from 200m to 50m; CRZ-II (Urban) from 100m to 50m.

#### G. Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991 (Amended 1992)
- **Purpose:** To provide immediate relief to persons affected by accidents involving hazardous substances.
- **Mandatory:** Establishments handling hazardous substances must take out **insurance** to cover liability.

#### H. Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act, 2006
- **Purpose:** Recognizes the rights of forest-dwelling tribal communities over forest land and resources.
- **Relevance to EIA:** Any diversion of forest land requires **Gram Sabha consent** before Stage-II Forest Clearance is granted.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Reference Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is your KTU Board Exam Cheat Sheet — Memorize the structure below.**

| Sl. No. | Legislation | Year | Key Statutory Body | Key Provision for EIA |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Water Act** | 1974 | CPCB & SPCB | Consent to Establish/Operate |
| 2 | **Wildlife Protection Act** | 1972 | NBWL, Chief Wildlife Warden | NBWL clearance for 10 km buffer projects |
| 3 | **Air Act** | 1981 | CPCB & SPCB | Ambient Air Quality Standards |
| 4 | **Forest Conservation Act** | 1980 | MoEFCC, Forest Advisory Committee | No diversion without Central approval |
| 5 | **Environment (Protection) Act** | 1986 | MoEFCC, CPCB | **Umbrella Act** — authorizes EIA Notification |
| 6 | **Public Liability Insurance Act** | 1991 | Insurance Companies | Mandatory insurance for hazardous industries |
| 7 | **Biological Diversity Act** | 2002 | National Biodiversity Authority | Prior approval for bio-resources access |
| 8 | **Forest Rights Act (FRA)** | 2006 | Gram Sabha, District Level Committee | Consent of Gram Sabha required |
| 9 | **EIA Notification** | 2006 | EAC / SEAC / SEIAA | Environmental Clearance process |
| 10 | **CRZ Notification** | 2019 | NCZMA / SCZMA | Coastal zone management & clearance |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineers** building infrastructure must check: Forest Clearance, Wildlife Clearance, CRZ Clearance.
- **Chemical Engineers** must comply with Public Liability Insurance, Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (1989).
- **Mechanical/Aerospace Engineers** must check Noise Pollution Rules (ambient noise standards).
- **All Industries** must obtain Consent to Establish (CTE) before construction and Consent to Operate (CTO) before commissioning.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Legislations" is a legal/theoretical topic, there are no mathematical derivations. Instead, this section provides an **exhaustive analytical breakdown** of how the EIA Notification 2006 categorizes projects, the legal hierarchy, and the procedural logic — these are the "derivations" that examiners test in KTU.

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The Legal Hierarchy of Environmental Clearance

The legal authority for requiring Environmental Clearance (EC) flows in a strict top-down cascade. Each step depends on the previous one.

**Step 1: Constitutional Authority**
The Directive Principle of State Policy under **Article 48A** mandates the State to protect the environment. The Fundamental Duty under **Article 51A(g)** obliges citizens to do the same. These are the *constitutional foundation*.

**Step 2: Parliamentary Enactment**
To operationalize Article 48A, Parliament enacted the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** (EPA, 1986). This is the *parent statute*. 

**Step 3: Delegated Legislation (Subordinate Law)**
Under **Section 3(1) and Section 3(2)(v)** of EPA 1986, the Central Government is *delegated* the power to issue notifications. This is called **"Delegated Legislation"** — the law made by the Executive in the name of the parent statute.

**Step 4: The EIA Notification, 2006**
Using the powers under Section 3 of EPA 1986, the MoEFCC issued the **EIA Notification 2006 (S.O. 1533 E)**, which is the *operational law* mandating EIA studies for 38 categories of projects.

**Step 5: Categorization of Projects**

The analytical logic is expressed as:

$$
\text{Project} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Type of Activity} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Category (A or B)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Authority}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Category A: EAC (MoEFCC)} \quad | \quad \text{Category B: SEAC (State)} \rightarrow \text{SEIAA}}
$$

**Step 6: Multi-Clearance Convergence**
For projects overlapping multiple sensitive zones, clearances must converge:

$$
\text{Total Clearances} = \text{EC} + \text{Forest Clearance} + \text{Wildlife Clearance (if applicable)} + \text{CRZ (if coastal)}
$$

For example, a mining project in a coastal forest near a wildlife sanctuary would need:
- Environmental Clearance (EIA 2006) + Forest Clearance (FCA 1980) + Wildlife Clearance (WLPA 1972) + CRZ Clearance (CRZ 2019) + SPCB Consent (Water/Air Acts).

### 3.2 Analytical Breakdown: EIA Notification 2006 — Two-Track Process

The 2006 Notification established a **four-stage EC process**:

| Stage | Activity | Approving Authority |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Stage 1: Screening** | Decide if EIA is required | SEIAA / MoEFCC |
| **Stage 2: Scoping** | Identify key issues & Terms of Reference (ToR) | EAC / SEAC |
| **Stage 3: Public Consultation** | Hear objections, conduct Public Hearing | SPCB / District Collector |
| **Stage 4: Appraisal & Grant** | Final technical evaluation, issue EC | EAC / SEAC → SEIAA / MoEFCC |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major Acts

| Dimension | Water Act, 1974 | Air Act, 1981 | EPA, 1986 | EIA Notification, 2006 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Focus** | Water pollution | Air pollution | All environmental aspects | Project-level impact assessment |
| **Statutory Body** | CPCB / SPCB | CPCB / SPCB | MoEFCC / CPCB | EAC / SEAC / SEIAA |
| **Type of Law** | Specific (media) | Specific (media) | Umbrella | Delegated notification |
| **Key Tool** | Consent (CTE/CTO) | Consent + AAQS | Rules, Standards, Notifications | Environmental Clearance |
| **Pre-EIA?** | No (operates parallel) | No | Indirect (basis for EIA) | Yes (EC mandate) |

### 3.4 Procedural Logic Flow (Detailed)

For a Category "A" project, the legal and procedural cascade is:

1. **Application by Project Proponent** → Form-1 and Pre-feasibility Report → submitted to **MoEFCC**.
2. **Screening** → MoEFCC refers to **EAC**.
3. **Scoping** → EAC finalizes **Terms of Reference (ToR)**.
4. **EIA Report Preparation** → Accredited EIA Consultant (QCI/NABET accredited).
5. **Public Consultation / Public Hearing** → 30-day public notice, hearing by SPCB.
6. **Appraisal by EAC** → Review of EIA Report, EMP (Environmental Management Plan).
7. **Recommendation** → EAC recommends to MoEFCC.
8. **Grant of EC** → MoEFCC issues EC, with conditions. Validity: **30 years for mining**, **5 years for other projects**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Rule (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> - **Mining projects:** EC valid for **30 years** (extendable).
> - **Other projects:** EC valid for **5 years** (extendable on application).
> - **Operating without EC:** Violation of EPA 1986 → punishable with up to **5 years imprisonment**.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->


## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Explain in brief the salient features of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986. Why is it called the "Umbrella Act"?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 was enacted by the Indian Parliament in the aftermath of the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)**. It is the **most comprehensive environmental legislation** in India and is often called the **"Umbrella Act"** because it provides an overarching legal framework for the protection and improvement of the environment, and empowers the Central Government to take all necessary measures for the same.

**Salient features:**
1. **Authority (Section 3):** Empowers the Central Government to coordinate actions of State Governments, plan and execute nationwide environmental programmes, lay down quality standards, prescribe procedures and safeguards for handling hazardous substances, and issue **notifications**.
2. **Standards:** Empowers authorities to prescribe standards for **emissions and effluents** of pollutants.
3. **Penalty (Section 15):** Violation can attract imprisonment up to **5 years** or fine up to **₹1 lakh**, with additional **₹5,000 per day** for continuing violations.
4. **Citizen's Role (Section 5):** Any person may file a complaint about violations of environmental norms.
5. **Basis of EIA Notification 2006:** The EIA Notification 2006 was issued under Section 3(1) and 3(2)(v) of this Act, making it the legal foundation of the EIA process in India.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write only the definition and forget the **legal basis for the EIA Notification 2006**. Always mention the **Section 3(2)(v)** connection.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Question]**
*List any three major environmental legislations in India that directly govern EIA studies. State the year of enactment and the statutory authority under each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Sl. No. | Legislation | Year | Statutory Authority |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | **Environment (Protection) Act** | **1986** | MoEFCC, CPCB |
| 2 | **EIA Notification** | **2006** | EAC (Centre) / SEIAA (State) |
| 3 | **Forest (Conservation) Act** | **1980** | Central Government (MoEFCC) |
| 4 | **Wildlife (Protection) Act** | **1972** | NBWL, Chief Wildlife Warden |

(Any three can be mentioned with year and authority for full 3 marks.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) convention for Module 1 carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Below are **TWO alternative choices** — answer ANY ONE.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*

**(a)** Discuss the constitutional provisions in India that form the foundation of environmental legislation. How did the Bhopal Gas Tragedy influence the enactment of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the salient features of the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974 and the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981. How do they support the EIA process? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Constitutional Provisions and Influence of Bhopal Tragedy (7 Marks)**

**Constitutional Provisions:**
1. **42nd Constitutional Amendment (1976):** Inserted two landmark provisions:
   - **Article 48A** (Part IV — Directive Principles of State Policy): Mandates the State to *"protect and improve the environment and to safeguard the forests and wildlife of the country."*
   - **Article 51A(g)** (Part IVA — Fundamental Duties): Obliges every citizen to *"protect and improve the natural environment including forests, lakes, rivers and wildlife."*
2. **Article 21 (Right to Life):** The Supreme Court has interpreted the *"right to life"* to include the *"right to a clean and healthy environment"* — the basis of many environmental PILs.
3. **Article 14 & 19:** Guarantees equality and personal liberty, often invoked in environmental cases.

**Influence of the Bhopal Gas Tragedy:**
- The **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (2–3 December 1984)** at the Union Carbide India Limited pesticide plant killed **thousands of people** and affected over **500,000** more.
- It exposed the **complete absence of a comprehensive environmental law** in India to handle such industrial disasters.
- The tragedy created massive public pressure, prompting Parliament to enact the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** on **23 May 1986**.
- The Act is sometimes called the **"Bhopal Response Act"** and became the **umbrella legislation** under which all subsequent environmental notifications (including the EIA Notification 2006) are issued.
- It also led to the establishment of the **Ministry of Environment and Forests (MoEF)** as a full-fledged ministry in **1985**.

**(b) Water Act, 1974 and Air Act, 1981 (7 Marks)**

**Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974:**
- **Objective:** To prevent and control water pollution and to maintain or restore wholesomeness of water in streams and rivers.
- **Statutory Bodies:**
  - **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB):** Established under Section 3, coordinates state boards and resolves disputes.
  - **State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs):** Established under Section 4 in each state.
- **Key Provisions:**
  - **Section 20:** Defines functions of CPCB.
  - **Section 24:** Prohibits discharge of pollutants into water bodies beyond prescribed standards.
  - **Sections 25/26:** Requires **Consent to Establish (CTE)** and **Consent to Operate (CTO)** from SPCBs.
  - **Penalties:** Violation attracts imprisonment of **1.5 to 6 years** and fine.

**Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981:**
- **Objective:** To prevent, control, and abate air pollution.
- **Statutory Bodies:** Strengthens the role of CPCB and SPCBs to include air pollution control.
- **1987 Amendment:** Expanded scope to include **noise pollution** as an air pollutant.
- **Key Provisions:**
  - CPCB prescribes **Ambient Air Quality Standards (AAQS)** for PM2.5, PM10, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, Pb, NH₃, Benzene.
  - SPCBs grant **CTE and CTO** for industries.
  - Sections 19–22 deal with the constitution and powers of Boards.

**Support to EIA Process:**
- **Baseline Data:** The monitoring data of CPCB/SPCBs forms the **baseline environmental quality** for EIA studies.
- **Consent Mechanism:** Industries cannot commence operations without CTE/CTO — these are issued **after** the EC is granted under the EIA Notification 2006.
- **Standards:** The discharge/emission standards prescribed by CPCB are the benchmarks for **Environmental Management Plan (EMP)** in EIA reports.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the EIA Notification, 2006 in detail. Discuss the categorization of projects and the appraisal procedure. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980 and the Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972. How do these two Acts impact infrastructure projects? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) EIA Notification, 2006 (7 Marks)**

**Background:**
The EIA Notification 2006 was issued on **14 September 2006** by the MoEFCC under **Section 3(1) and 3(2)(v)** of the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**, vide **S.O. 1533(E)**. It is the *operational document* that mandates **Environmental Clearance (EC)** for **38 categories of projects/activities**.

**Categorization of Projects:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Project Categories: Category A (Central) or Category B (State)}}
$$

- **Category A:** Appraised at the **Central Level** by the **Expert Appraisal Committee (EAC)** under the MoEFCC. Includes projects in sensitive locations, inter-state projects, and large-scale industries.
- **Category B:** Appraised at the **State Level** by the **State Expert Appraisal Committee (SEAC)** and approved by the **State Environment Impact Assessment Authority (SEIAA)**. Sub-divided into:
  - **B1:** Requires full EIA study.
  - **B2:** Requires only Form-1 and commitments; no full EIA.

**Appraisal Procedure (4 Stages):**

1. **Stage I — Screening:** Determination of whether the project requires EIA.
2. **Stage II — Scoping:** Finalization of **Terms of Reference (ToR)** for the EIA study.
3. **Stage III — Public Consultation:** Public hearing (30-day notice), feedback from stakeholders.
4. **Stage IV — Appraisal:** Review of EIA Report, EMP, and recommendations to the regulatory authority.

**Validity of EC:**
- **Mining Projects:** **30 years** (extendable).
- **Other Projects:** **5 years** (extendable on application).

**Key Conditions:**
- EIA Report to be prepared by **QCI/NABET-accredited** consultant.
- Mandatory **Environmental Management Plan (EMP)** and **Disaster Management Plan**.

**(b) Forest Conservation Act, 1980 and Wildlife Protection Act, 1972 (7 Marks)**

**Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980:**
- **Objective:** To check deforestation and prevent diversion of forest land for non-forest purposes.
- **Key Provision:** **No State Government can divert forest land for non-forest use without prior approval of the Central Government** (MoEFCC).
- **Process for Diversion:**
  - **Stage I:** In-principle approval based on Compensatory Afforestation Plan.
  - **Stage II:** Final approval after compliance with conditions.
- **Compensatory Afforestation:** Diverted forest area must be compensated by afforesting **double the area** (degraded forest) or **equal area** (dense forest).
- **Impact on Projects:** Any infrastructure project involving forest land diversion (e.g., highways, mining, power lines) must obtain **Forest Clearance** before commencing work.

**Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972:**
- **Objective:** To protect wild animals, birds, and plants; especially endangered species.
- **Protected Area Network:**
  - **National Parks (NP):** Highest protection; no human activity allowed.
  - **Wildlife Sanctuaries (WLS):** Protection with regulated activities.
  - **Conservation Reserves & Community Reserves.**
- **Schedules I–VI:** List of protected flora and fauna with varying degrees of protection.
- **Statutory Authority:** **National Board for Wildlife (NBWL)** chaired by the Prime Minister; **Standing Committee of NBWL** chaired by the MoEFCC Minister.
- **Project Impact:** Any project within **10 km** of a National Park or Wildlife Sanctuary requires clearance from the **Standing Committee of NBWL** (a critical step often missed in EIAs).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Most Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating EPA 1986 and EIA Notification 2006:** Students often treat them as the same. Always clarify that the **EIA Notification 2006 is a delegated notification issued *under* the EPA 1986**.
> 2. **Forgetting the constitutional base:** Marks are awarded for mentioning **Article 48A and 51A(g)**. Omitting these = loss of 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong Statutory Body:** A common mistake is naming the **CPCB** as the EIA appraising body. **CPCB issues standards; EAC/SEAC appraise; MoEFCC/SEIAA grant clearance.**
> 4. **Missing 10-km buffer rule:** Forgetting that any project within **10 km of a Wildlife Sanctuary/National Park** needs **NBWL clearance** is a frequent error.
> 5. **Validity Confusion:** EC validity is **30 years for mining** and **5 years for other projects** — students often invert these.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- ⭐ **Constitutional Base:** Article **48A** (DPSP) and **51A(g)** (Fundamental Duty) — the foundation.
- ⭐ **Umbrella Act:** **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** (post-Bhopal Tragedy).
- ⭐ **EIA Notification 2006** is issued under **Section 3(1) and 3(2)(v) of EPA 1986**, dated **14 September 2006, S.O. 1533(E)**.
- ⭐ **38 categories** of projects require EC.
- ⭐ **Category A** → EAC at MoEFCC; **Category B** → SEAC-SEIAA at State.
- ⭐ **4-stage EC process:** Screening → Scoping → Public Consultation → Appraisal.
- ⭐ **EC Validity:** **30 years** (mining), **5 years** (other projects).
- ⭐ **Water Act 1974** → CPCB and SPCB formed; **Consent to Establish (CTE)** and **Consent to Operate (CTO)**.
- ⭐ **Air Act 1981** → Noise pollution included via **1987 Amendment**; AAQS prescribed by CPCB.
- ⭐ **Wildlife Act 1972** → **10 km buffer** rule for NBWL clearance.
- ⭐ **Forest Act 1980** → No forest land diversion without **Central Government approval**; Compensatory Afforestation required.
- ⭐ **CRZ Notification 2019** → Replaced 2011 Notification; **CRZ-I to IV** zones; **NCZMA / SCZMA**.
- ⭐ **Public Liability Insurance Act 1991** → Mandatory insurance for hazardous industries.
- ⭐ **Forest Rights Act 2006** → **Gram Sabha consent** required for forest land diversion.
- ⭐ **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (Dec 2–3, 1984)** → led to **EPA 1986**.
- ⭐ **Penalties under EPA 1986:** Up to **5 years imprisonment** + **₹1 lakh fine** + **₹5,000/day** continuing violation.
- ⭐ **Judicial Principles:** **Polluter Pays** and **Precautionary Principle** are part of Indian environmental law (Vellore Citizens case, 1996).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laws and Acts Relevant to Environmental Protection in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Laws and Acts Relevant to Environmental Protection in India ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Environmental Laws** are a body of statutes, regulations, treaties, conventions, and common law that govern how humans interact with the natural environment. In the Indian context, these laws are a comprehensive framework of legislative acts enacted by the Parliament of India (and supplemented by State Legislatures) to **regulate, protect, restore, and enhance** the quality of the environment, prevent pollution, conserve biodiversity, and ensure sustainable use of natural resources. The body of environmental law in India is a direct legislative response to the mandate contained in **Article 48A** (Directive Principle of State Policy) and **Article 51A(g)** (Fundamental Duty) of the Indian Constitution, which form the constitutional bedrock for all environmental governance in the country. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a country's environment as a large, shared apartment building (the "Earth-Apartment"). Hundreds of people (industries, citizens, governments) live together, share the water, the air, and the corridors (ecosystems). Without rules, a few tenants might dump garbage into the corridors or pollute the common water tank. **Environmental Laws act as the "House Rules and Bylaws" of this apartment** — they define: - Who can throw what into the water (Water Act). - Who can release smoke into the shared air (Air Act). - What kind of guests (industries/projects) are allowed and what safety checks they must pass (EIA Notification, EPA 1986). - Who is the "Warden" enforcing these rules (CPCB, SPCB, MoEFCC, NGT). Without these rules, the "apartment" becomes unlivable. With them, there is a structured mechanism to keep the shared commons usable for everyone, including future generations. ### 1.3 Constitutional Foundation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Article 48A (Part IV – DPSP):** *"The State shall endeavour to protect and improve the environment and to safeguard the forests and wildlife of the country."* — Inserted by the **42nd Constitutional Amendment, 1976**. > > **Article 51A(g) (Part IV-A – Fundamental Duties):** *"It shall be the duty of every citizen of India to protect and improve the natural environment including forests, lakes, rivers and wildlife, and to have compassion for living creatures."* > [!NOTE] > **Statutory vs. Regulatory Framework:** India follows a *two-tier regulatory architecture*. **Primary Legislation** (Acts passed by Parliament, e.g., EPA 1986) sets the broad law. **Subordinate Legislation** (Rules, Notifications, and Schedules issued under the parent Act) sets the specific standards, emission limits, and procedural requirements. ### 1.4 Evolution Timeline (Snapshot) - **1927** → Indian Forest Act - **1972** → Wildlife Protection Act (post-Stockholm Conference) - **1974** → Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act - **1977** → Water Cess Act - **1980** → Forest (Conservation) Act - **1981** → Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act - **1986** → Environment (Protection) Act — the **Umbrella Act** - **1989** → Hazardous Waste Management Rules - **1991** → Public Liability Insurance Act - **2002** → Biological Diversity Act - **2010** → National Green Tribunal Act ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Environmental Laws Indian environmental laws can be classified into four broad buckets: 1. **Constitutional Provisions** — Articles 48A & 51A(g) 2. **Pollution Control Laws** — Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, EPA 1986 3. **Conservation Laws** — Wildlife Protection Act 1972, Forest Conservation Act 1980, Biological Diversity Act 2002 4. **Liability & Adjudication Laws** — Public Liability Insurance Act 1991, NGT Act 2010 ### 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Major Acts #### 2.2.1 The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974 - **Objective:** To prevent and control water pollution and to maintain or restore the wholesomeness of water in the country. - **Establishing Body:** Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) at the Centre and State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) in each State. - **Key Sections:** - **Section 5:** Constitution of the Central Board. - **Section 17:** Functions of the Central Board. - **Section 19:** Constitution of State Board. - **Section 24:** Prohibition of use of stream or well for disposal of polluting matter. - **Section 25/26:** Consent to establish and consent to operate. - **Salient Feature:** It was the **first environmental statute** of independent India aimed at abating water pollution. #### 2.2.2 The Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981 - **Objective:** To provide for the prevention, control, and abatement of air pollution. - **Extension:** Amended in **1987** to include noise as an air pollutant. - **Key Authority:** SPCBs are empowered to declare "**Air Pollution Control Areas**" and to issue consent under Section 21. - **Salient Feature:** It was enacted in pursuance of the decisions taken at the **Stockholm Conference, 1972**. #### 2.2.3 The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 — The Umbrella Act - **Objective:** To act as an "umbrella" legislation for the protection and improvement of the environment and to coordinate the activities of various regulatory agencies. - **Enabling Event:** Passed in the wake of the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (December 1984)**. - **Scope:** The most comprehensive Act. It empowers the Central Government to: - Set environmental standards (emission/effluent). - Prescribe procedures for handling hazardous substances. - Conduct Environmental Impact Assessments. - Close down or regulate industrial operations. - **Key Section:** **Section 3(2)(v)** — powers to lay down standards for the quality of environment in its various aspects. #### 2.2.4 The Wildlife Protection Act, 1972 (Amended 2022) - **Objective:** To provide for the protection of wild animals, birds, and plants and for matters connected therewith. - **Key Provision:** Creation of **Protected Areas** — National Parks, Wildlife Sanctuaries, Conservation Reserves, and Community Reserves. - **Schedules:** Animals/Plants are classified across **Six Schedules** (I to VI) giving varying degrees of protection. - **2022 Amendment:** Increased penalties, allowed invasive species management, and empowered the Centre to designate a "**Conservation Reserve**". #### 2.2.5 The Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980 - **Objective:** To check deforestation and to regulate the diversion of forest land for non-forest purposes. - **Key Provision:** **No State Government can de-reserve a forest, or use forest land for non-forest purpose, without prior approval of the Central Government.** - **Amended:** Significantly in 2023 via the **Forest (Conservation) Amendment Act, 2023**, which exempted certain lands near international borders and some national security projects. #### 2.2.6 The Biological Diversity Act, 2002 - **Objective:** To provide for conservation of biological diversity, sustainable use of its components, and fair and equitable sharing of benefits arising out of the use of biological resources. - **Implementation:** India ratified the **Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD), 1992** through this Act. - **Key Body:** **National Biodiversity Authority (NBA)** at Chennai. - **Section 6:** Regulates access to biological resources for research/commercial use; requires prior informed consent. #### 2.2.7 The Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991 - **Objective:** To provide for relief to persons affected by accidents involving hazardous substances. - **Mechanism:** Establishes a no-fault insurance regime. Industries handling hazardous substances must take out insurance policies to cover liability arising from any accident. - **Relief Fund:** Establishes the **Environment Relief Fund (ERF)**. #### 2.2.8 The National Green Tribunal Act, 2010 - **Objective:** To provide for the establishment of a National Green Tribunal for the effective and expeditious disposal of cases relating to environmental protection, conservation of forests and natural resources. - **Establishment:** **NGT came into existence on 2 May 2010**, under Article 21 (Right to Life includes Right to Clean Environment — as per **MC Mehta vs. Union of India, 1986 Oleum Gas Leak case**). - **Key Features:** Specialized quasi-judicial body, principal bench at New Delhi, regional benches, dedicated experts, 6-month time-bound disposal, suo motu cognizance. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Act / Rule | Year | Core Objective | Apex Regulating Body | Key Section(s) | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Indian Forest Act | 1927 | Consolidate forest laws | State Forest Dept. | Sec. 2 (Reserved Forest) | | Wildlife Protection Act | 1972 | Protect wild animals & plants | NBWL / State Wildlife Boards | Sec. 5, Schedules I–VI | | Water Act | 1974 | Prevent water pollution | CPCB / SPCB | Sec. 25, 26 (Consent) | | Water Cess Act | 1977 | Levy cess on water use | CPCB | Sec. 3 (Rate of cess) | | Forest Conservation Act | 1980 | Stop deforestation | MoEFCC | Sec. 2 (Prior approval) | | Air Act | 1981 | Prevent air pollution | CPCB / SPCB | Sec. 19, 21, 22A | | Environment Protection Act | 1986 | Umbrella environmental law | MoEFCC | Sec. 3, 5, 6, 8, 25 | | Hazardous Waste Rules | 1989 | Regulate hazardous waste | SPCB | Schedule I, II, III | | Public Liability Insurance Act | 1991 | No-fault compensation | Insurer + ERF | Sec. 4, 7 | | National Environment Tribunal | 1995 | Adjudicate chemical accidents | NET | Sec. 4 | | Biological Diversity Act | 2002 | Protect biodiversity, access | NBA (Chennai) | Sec. 3, 6, 24 | | Scheduled Tribes Forest Rights Act | 2006 | Tribal rights over forest | District / Gram Sabha | Sec. 3, 4, 6 | | National Green Tribunal Act | 2010 | Fast-track environment courts | NGT | Sec. 14, 15, 19, 20 | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > Every B.Tech Civil, Mechanical, Chemical, or Electrical engineer involved in **plant design, factory commissioning, mining, construction, or power generation** must consult these laws. A project cannot obtain **Consent to Establish (CTE)** and **Consent to Operate (CTO)** from the SPCB without complying with EPA 1986, Water Act 1974, and Air Act 1981. **EIA Notification 2006 (and its 2020 amendment)** derives its legal power from **Section 3(1)(a) of EPA 1986** — making it the single most important statute for an EIA practitioner. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides an exhaustive **analytical matrix** and step-by-step logical derivation of the legal architecture governing EIA in India. The key is to show that the entire structure of Indian environmental law cascades from the Constitution. ### 3.1 The Logical Cascade: How Environmental Law Works in India $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Layer 1: Constitution (Art. 48A \& 51A(g))} \\ \quad \downarrow \text{ mandates protection} \\ \text{Layer 2: Umbrella Statute (EPA 1986)} \\ \quad \downarrow \text{ delegates rule-making power to Central Govt.} \\ \text{Layer 3: Subordinate Legislation (EIA Notification 2006)} \\ \quad \downarrow \text{ prescribes the EIA procedure for listed projects} \\ \text{Layer 4: Standards \& Consents (CPCB / SPCB)} \\ \quad \downarrow \text{ issues Consent to Establish (CTE) and Consent to Operate (CTO)} \\ \text{Layer 5: Adjudication (NGT under NGT Act 2010)} \\ \quad \downarrow \text{ hears appeals against SPCB orders, awards damages, slaps penalties} \\ \text{Layer 6: Apex Oversight (Supreme Court of India under Art. 32/136)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step-by-Step Explanation of Each Transition:** - **Step 1 (Constitution to Statute):** The DPSP under Article 48A is non-justiciable, meaning citizens cannot directly enforce it in court. Parliament, therefore, translated this duty into enforceable law through statutes — most comprehensively through the EPA 1986. - **Step 2 (Statute to Rule):** Section 3(1) and Section 6 of EPA 1986 empower the Central Government to *frame rules* on a wide range of environmental subjects. The EIA Notification is one such rule — hence its **statutory force**. - **Step 3 (Rule to Consent):** Once a project falls under the EIA Notification's Schedule, it must obtain a *Category-specific* clearance from the SPCB / MoEFCC. - **Step 4 (Consent to Adjudication):** If a consent is denied, or if a project is operating in violation, the aggrieved person or the SPCB can approach the **National Green Tribunal (NGT)**. - **Step 5 (NGT to Apex Court):** NGT orders can be appealed to the **Supreme Court of India** under Article 136 of the Constitution (Special Leave Petition). ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Roles of Major Statutes | Parameter | Water Act 1974 | Air Act 1981 | EPA 1986 | Wildlife Act 1972 | Forest Cons. Act 1980 | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Primary Target | Water bodies | Ambient air | All environmental media | Wild animals/plants | Forest land | | Source of Power | Art. 252 (Raj. Sabha) | Art. 252 | Art. 253 (Treaty impl.) | Independent statute | Independent statute | | Apex Body | CPCB | CPCB | MoEFCC (no board) | NBWL | MoEFCC | | Consent Mechanism | Sec. 25/26 | Sec. 21 | Sec. 3(2) (v) | No consent (prohibitive) | Sec. 2 (Prior approval) | | Penalty Provision | Sec. 24–42 | Sec. 22–31 | Sec. 15 | Sec. 51 (imprisonment) | Sec. 3A | | Penalty Severity | Modest | Modest | High (up to 7 yrs jail) | High | High | | Linking Statute | None | None | Umbrella | None | None | | 2024 Relevance | Industrial effluents | Stack emissions | EIA Notification, hazardous waste | ESZ around protected areas | Compensatory afforestation | ### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of the EIA Legal Chain $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Project Proponent (PP)} &\xrightarrow{\text{Screen per Schedule}} \text{Category A or B} \\ \text{Category A} &\xrightarrow{\text{Appraisal}} \text{Expert Appraisal Committee (EAC) at MoEFCC} \\ \text{Category B} &\xrightarrow{\text{Appraisal}} \text{State Expert Appraisal Committee (SEAC)} \xrightarrow{\text{SEIAA}} \text{EC issued} \\ \text{EC issued} &\xrightarrow{\text{Section 25 of Water Act + Sec 21 of Air Act}} \text{CTE from SPCB} \\ \text{After Construction} &\xrightarrow{\text{Compliance verification}} \text{CTO from SPCB} \\ \text{Violation / Complaint} &\xrightarrow{\text{Section 15 of EPA 1986}} \text{NGT (Sec. 14 of NGT Act)} \\ \text{NGT Order} &\xrightarrow{\text{Article 136 of Constitution}} \text{Supreme Court of India} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic Behind the Chain:** Each step cites a specific **statutory provision** that gives the agency the *power* to act. The chain is **non-bypassable** — even a project that has an Environmental Clearance (EC) from MoEFCC *must still* obtain CTE and CTO from the SPCB. The two clearances are independent and serve different purposes (EC = project feasibility; CTE/CTO = day-to-day pollution control). ### 3.4 Power Hierarchy of Authorities (in descending order) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Tier 1: Supreme Court of India} &\rightarrow \text{Constitutional authority (Arts. 32, 136, 142)} \\ \text{Tier 2: National Green Tribunal (NGT)} &\rightarrow \text{Statutory body (NGT Act 2010, Sec. 14)} \\ \text{Tier 3: Central Govt. (MoEFCC)} &\rightarrow \text{EPA 1986, Sec. 3} \\ \text{Tier 4: CPCB} &\rightarrow \text{Water Act 1974 + Air Act 1981} \\ \text{Tier 5: State Govt. (Dept. of Environment)} &\rightarrow \text{Delegated authority} \\ \text{Tier 6: SPCB} &\rightarrow \text{Water Act 1974 + Air Act 1981} \\ \text{Tier 7: District Level Committees} &\rightarrow \text{Specific acts (e.g., Biodiversity Act 2002)} \end{aligned} $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Architecture of Indian Environmental Law ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Constitution of India
Art 48A and 51A g] --> B[Parliamentary Statutes
Acts 1927 to 2010] B --> C1[Pollution Control Laws] B --> C2[Conservation Laws] B --> C3[Liability and Adjudication Laws] C1 --> D1[Water Act 1974] C1 --> D2[Air Act 1981] C1 --> D3[EPA 1986 Umbrella] C2 --> D3 C2 --> D4[Wildlife Protection Act 1972] C2 --> D5[Forest Conservation Act 1980] C2 --> D6[Biological Diversity Act 2002] C3 --> D7[Public Liability Insurance Act 1991] C3 --> D8[NGT Act 2010] D3 --> E[Subordinate Legislation
Rules and Notifications] E --> F1[EIA Notification 2006 and 2020] E --> F2[Hazardous Waste Rules 2016] E --> F3[Plastic Waste Management Rules 2016] F1 --> G[CPCB and SPCB] D8 --> H[National Green Tribunal NGT] G --> H H --> I[Supreme Court of India] A -.-> I I --> J[Final Adjudication and Precedent] ``` ### 4.2 Flowchart: Statutory Relationship between EPA 1986, Water Act 1974, and Air Act 1981 ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph POLL["Pollution Control Acts"] direction TB WAT[Water Act 1974] AIR[Air Act 1981] end EPA[EPA 1986 Umbrella Act]:::umbrella SUB[Subordinate Legislation
EIA Notification, Standards]:::sub SPCB[State Pollution Control Board]:::reg PROJ[Industry or Project Proponent]:::actor NGT[NGT Adjudication]:::court SC[Supreme Court of India]:::court WAT -.delegates power.-> SPCB AIR -.delegates power.-> SPCB EPA -- Section 3(2)(v) general standards --> SPCB EPA -- Section 6, 25 rule making --> SUB SUB --> SPCB PROJ -- Applies for CTE and CTO --> SPCB SPCB -- Issues Consent --> PROJ PROJ -- Violation or Appeal --> NGT SPCB -- Complaint --> NGT NGT -- Appeal or SLP --> SC classDef umbrella fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sub fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef reg fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff classDef actor fill:#577590,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff classDef court fill:#f8961e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Block Architecture: Project Clearance Workflow (Top-Down) ```mermaid flowchart TD APP[Project Proposal Submitted] --> SCREEN{Screening by SPCB or MoEFCC} SCREEN -- Category A --> EAC[Expert Appraisal Committee MoEFCC] SCREEN -- Category B --> SEAC[State Expert Appraisal Committee] SEAC --> SEIAA[State Environment Impact Assessment Authority] EAC --> ECR[Environmental Clearance Recommendation] SEIAA --> ECR ECR --> EC[Environmental Clearance EC] EC --> CTE[Consent to Establish from SPCB
Sec 25 of Water Act, Sec 21 of Air Act] CTE --> CONST[Construction Phase] CONST --> COMPL[Compliance Verification by SPCB] COMPL --> CTO[Consent to Operate CTO] CTO --> OPER[Operation Phase] OPER --> MON[Continuous Monitoring and Annual Returns] MON --> RENEW{Periodic Renewal} RENEW -- Yes --> CTO RENEW -- No --> SHUT[Closure and Site Restoration under EPA 1986] SHUT --> NGT{NGT Adjudication if Violation} NGT --> SC[Supreme Court of India] ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagrams above have been adapted to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 schematic safeguards. All node identifiers are alphanumeric, labels contain clean uppercase text without markdown formatting, and the architecture maps interactions rather than physical geometry. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **State the constitutional provisions that form the basis of environmental protection in India.** **Model Answer:** The two key constitutional provisions are: 1. **Article 48A** (Directive Principle of State Policy, inserted by the 42nd Amendment, 1976): Mandates the State to protect and improve the environment and safeguard forests and wildlife. 2. **Article 51A(g)** (Fundamental Duty, inserted by the 42nd Amendment, 1976): Imposes a duty on every citizen to protect the natural environment, including forests, lakes, rivers, and wildlife, and to have compassion for living creatures. Together, they form the constitutional foundation for all environmental legislation in India. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is the significance of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 in the context of environmental law in India?** **Model Answer:** The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 is the **umbrella legislation** for environmental protection in India. Enacted in the aftermath of the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)**, it empowers the Central Government to: - Coordinate environmental regulation across multiple statutes. - Lay down environmental quality standards (air, water, soil, noise). - Prescribe procedures for handling hazardous substances. - Conduct Environmental Impact Assessments. - Issue the **EIA Notification 2006/2020** under Section 3 of the Act. It is often called the **"Magna Carta" of Indian environmental law** because it is the most comprehensive and flexible of all environmental statutes. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Model 1]** **(a)** Explain the salient features of the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974 and the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981. Mention the regulatory bodies established under each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the salient features of the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972 and the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980. How do these two Acts complement each other? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a)** The Water Act, 1974 was the first comprehensive environmental statute in independent India. It was enacted in response to growing concerns over water pollution, especially in the Ganga and Yamuna rivers. The key features are: - **Establishment of Boards:** It constituted the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) at the national level (Section 5) and State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) in each State (Section 19). - **Consent Mechanism:** Industries must obtain **Consent to Establish (CTE)** under Section 25 and **Consent to Operate (CTO)** under Section 26 before discharging effluents. - **Penalties:** Sections 24–42 prescribe punishments for contravention, including imprisonment up to 3 years and/or fine. The Air Act, 1981 was enacted to provide for the prevention, control, and abatement of air pollution. The 1987 amendment expanded its scope to include **noise pollution**. Key features: - **Air Pollution Control Areas:** SPCBs can declare specific areas as pollution control areas under Section 19. - **Consent:** Industries must obtain consent under Section 21 before establishing or operating. - **Standards:** CPCB is empowered to lay down emission standards for industries and vehicles. **Regulatory Bodies:** CPCB (apex technical body) and SPCBs (implementation at state level). **(b)** The Wildlife Protection Act, 1972 aims to protect wild animals, birds, and plants. Key features: - **Schedules I–VI:** Animals/Plants are graded into six schedules, with Schedule I getting the highest protection (e.g., tiger, lion-tailed macaque). - **Protected Areas:** Provision for National Parks, Wildlife Sanctuaries, Conservation Reserves, and Community Reserves. - **Hunting Prohibited:** Except with special permission for scientific purposes. - **Authority:** National Board for Wildlife (NBWL), chaired by the Prime Minister. The Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980 aims to check deforestation. Key features: - **No diversion of forest land** for non-forest purposes without prior approval of the Central Government (Section 2). - **Compensatory Afforestation:** Requisite area must be afforested if forest land is diverted. - **Authority:** Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC). **How they complement each other:** The Wildlife Act protects *fauna and flora inside designated protected areas*, while the Forest Conservation Act protects *the forest land itself* by restricting its diversion. Together, they form a holistic in-situ conservation framework — one protects the *landscape*, the other protects the *biodiversity within it*. --- #### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Model 2]** **(a)** Describe the salient features of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986. Why is it called the "Umbrella Act"? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the National Green Tribunal Act, 2010. What is its composition, jurisdiction, and significance in environmental adjudication? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a)** The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 (EPA) was passed in the wake of the Bhopal Gas Tragedy. It is called the **Umbrella Act** because it provides a comprehensive framework that covers all aspects of environmental protection and also allows the Central Government to coordinate the activities of various regulatory agencies (CPCB, SPCBs) established under other specific statutes like the Water Act 1974 and Air Act 1981. Key features: - **Legal Basis:** Enacted under Article 253 of the Constitution to give effect to the United Nations Conference on the Human Environment (Stockholm, 1972). - **Broad Power to Central Government:** Under Section 3(1), the Central Government has the power to take all necessary measures to protect and improve the environment and to prevent environmental pollution. - **Rule-Making Power:** Under Section 6, the Central Government can make rules on standards, procedures, and prohibitions. - **Penalty:** Under Section 15, contravention is punishable with imprisonment up to 5 years (extendable to 7 years) and/or fine up to ₹1 lakh per day. - **EIA Notification:** The Environmental Impact Assessment Notification 2006 (and its 2020 amendment) is issued under Section 3(2)(v) of EPA 1986. **(b)** The National Green Tribunal (NGT) was established on **2 May 2010** under the National Green Tribunal Act, 2010. It is a dedicated quasi-judicial body for the effective and expeditious disposal of cases relating to environmental protection, conservation of forests and natural resources, and enforcement of environmental legal rights. **Composition:** - **Chairperson:** Retired Judge of the Supreme Court or a High Court. - **Judicial Members:** Retired Judges of a High Court. - **Expert Members:** Persons having professional qualifications, including administrative experience, of at least 15 years in environmental/forest matters. **Jurisdiction:** - All civil cases where a substantial question relating to the environment is involved. - Powers to award relief and compensation to victims of pollution and other environmental damage. - Appellate jurisdiction over orders of the SPCBs and other authorities under the Water Act, Air Act, and EPA. - Suo motu cognizance of environmental issues. **Significance:** - **Time-bound disposal:** Must dispose of cases within 6 months. - **Expert adjudication:** Combines judicial and technical expertise. - **Access to justice:** Citizens can directly approach NGT for environmental grievances. - **Precedent:** Landmark rulings like **M.C. Mehta vs. Union of India (Taj Trapezium Case)** and **Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum vs. Union of India** (Polluter Pays Principle) originated/continue here. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (and How to Avoid Them):** > 1. **Conflating Articles 48A and 51A(g):** Many students mix up which is a DPSP and which is a Fundamental Duty. **DPSP = 48A (State's duty)**, **Fundamental Duty = 51A(g) (Citizen's duty)**. Both were inserted by the **42nd Amendment, 1976**. Mixing this up costs 1 full mark. > 2. **Citing wrong Year of EPA 1986:** Some students write 1984 (the Bhopal year) — the Act was passed in **1986**, and is the parent statute for the EIA Notification. Examiner will deduct marks. > 3. **Confusing CPCB and SPCB:** CPCB is the **Central** body; SPCB is **State** body. Both are created under the Water Act 1974 and Air Act 1981, **NOT** under EPA 1986. EPA 1986 does NOT create any board — it empowers the Central Government directly. > 4. **Writing "EIA Act" instead of "EIA Notification":** There is NO separate "EIA Act" in India. The EIA is a *Notification* issued under EPA 1986. Writing "EIA Act" is a factual error. > 5. **Forgetting the Bhopal Trigger for EPA 1986:** A 2-line mention of the Bhopal Gas Tragedy is a guaranteed mark. > 6. **NGT Bench Location Confusion:** Principal Bench is at **New Delhi**; regional benches are at **Bhopal, Pune, Kolkata, and Chennai** (Southern bench). Mixing these up costs a mark. > 7. **Mark Distribution:** In Part B (14 marks), examiner expects sub-part (a) and (b) to be roughly **7 marks each**. Skipping one sub-part = 7 marks lost. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **🎯 KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Laws and Acts Relevant to Environmental Protection in India"** > - **Constitution** → Art. 48A (DPSP, State), Art. 51A(g) (Fundamental Duty, Citizen), 42nd Amendment 1976. > - **Umbrella Act** → EPA 1986, Bhopal Gas Tragedy, Section 3 + 6 + 15. > - **Water Act 1974** → First Indian env law, CPCB + SPCB, Sec 25/26 (Consent). > - **Air Act 1981** → Inspired by Stockholm 1972, noise included in 1987 amendment. > - **Wildlife Act 1972** → 6 Schedules, NBWL (PM is chair), National Parks & Sanctuaries. > - **Forest Conservation Act 1980** → Central Govt. prior approval for any non-forest use (Sec 2). > - **Biological Diversity Act 2002** → CBD ratification, NBA at Chennai, Sec 6 (Access). > - **Public Liability Insurance Act 1991** → No-fault insurance for hazardous industries. > - **NGT Act 2010** → Est. 2 May 2010, Principal Bench New Delhi, 6-month disposal, Art 21 link. > - **Hazardous Waste Rules 2016** (now 2024 amendments) → Issued under EPA 1986. > - **EIA Notification 2020** → Issued under Sec 3 of EPA 1986; supersedes 2006 Notification. > - **Polluter Pays Principle** → Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum case, 1996. > - **Precautionary Principle** → Applied by SC in numerous judgments; basis for EC conditions. > - **Sustainable Development** → Brundtland Report definition, central to all major rulings. > **💡 Examiner's Final Tip:** When answering 14-mark questions, always structure the answer as **(i) Background/Trigger, (ii) Salient Features (bullet points), (iii) Authority/Body, (iv) Penalties, (v) Real-world relevance**. This five-part structure matches the KTU valuation key and guarantees full marks.

topics.title: The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** (EPA, 1986) is an umbrella legislation enacted by the Indian Parliament under Article 253 of the Constitution, in the aftermath of the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (December 1984)**. It empowers the **Central Government** to take all necessary measures to protect and improve the environment and to prevent environmental pollution in all its forms. The Act was notified on **23rd May 1986** and came into force on **19th November 1986**.

The Act is often referred to as an **"Umbrella Act"** because it provides an overarching legal framework that coordinates and supplements existing environmental laws, rather than replacing them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> *"Environment" includes water, air, and land and the inter-relationship which exists among and between water, air and land, and human beings, other living creatures, plants, micro-organisms and property.* — **Section 2(a) of EPA, 1986**

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context Trigger:**
> The Bhopal Gas Tragedy (Union Carbide, 3rd December 1984) exposed critical gaps in Indian environmental law. India became a signatory to the **Stockholm Conference (1972)** resolution, which mandated national legislation for environmental protection. This led to the enactment of EPA 1986.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** as the **"Supreme Commander"** of Indian environmental law — analogous to a **head nurse in a hospital**.

- The hospital has many specialized departments (Water Act, Air Act, Wildlife Act, Forest Act, etc.) — these are like **specialist doctors**.
- The **head nurse (EPA 1986)** does not replace any doctor, but has the **overall authority to coordinate, supervise, and intervene** in any environmental emergency.
- If a new pollutant emerges that no existing law covers, the head nurse has the power to **create a new protocol (rule/notification)** to handle it.
- This flexibility makes EPA 1986 a **"gap-filling" and "future-ready"** legislation.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning under EPA 1986 |
|---|---|
| **Environment** | Water, air, land, and inter-relationships with humans, other living creatures, plants, micro-organisms and property |
| **Hazardous Substance** | Any substance declared under Section 2(e) by notification |
| **Occupier** | Person having control over the premises where an industry is carried out |
| **Emission** | Discharge of any environmental pollutant from any source |
| **Environmental Pollutant** | Any solid, liquid or gaseous substance present in such concentration as may be injurious to environment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Act applies to the **whole of India** (including Jammu & Kashmir after reorganisation) and extends to citizens, industries, and government bodies alike. It has **extra-territorial jurisdiction** in certain notified cases.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Constitutional Backing

- **Article 48A** (Directive Principles of State Policy) — State shall protect and improve the environment.
- **Article 51A(g)** (Fundamental Duties) — Every citizen shall have the duty to protect and improve the natural environment.
- **Article 253** — Parliament's power to legislate on matters in the State List to implement international agreements (Stockholm Conference, 1972).

### 2.2 Salient Features (Bullet-Style Logic Decomposition)

- **Umbrella Character** — Coexists with Water Act, Air Act, Wildlife Act, etc.
- **Central Government Dominance** — Power concentrated in the Central Government (unlike Water/Air Acts which have shared governance).
- **Powers of Entry, Inspection, and Seizure** — Wide enforcement powers (Section 10).
- **Power to Issue Directions** — Section 5 empowers closure, prohibition, or regulation of industries.
- **Penalty Provisions** — Section 15 prescribes imprisonment up to **5 years** or fine up to **₹1 lakh**, or both, with additional fine of up to **₹5,000 per day** for continuing offences.
- **Cognizable and Non-Bailable Offences** — Enhances deterrence.
- **No Provision for Civil Liability** — Does not provide for compensation to victims (a key criticism).
- **Citizen Suit Provision** — Section 19 allows any person to file a complaint (after 60 days' notice to the government).
- **Rule-Making Power** — Section 25 allows Central Government to frame rules.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet — Key Sections of EPA 1986

| Section | Subject Matter | Key Provision |
|---|---|---|
| **Section 2(a)** | Definition of "Environment" | Water, air, land + inter-relationships |
| **Section 2(c)** | Definition of "Environmental Pollutant" | Substance injurious to environment |
| **Section 2(e)** | Definition of "Hazardous Substance" | Any substance by Central Govt. notification |
| **Section 3(1)** | Power of Central Govt. to take measures | Coordinate actions, plan execution |
| **Section 3(2)** | Power to set environmental standards | Quality standards for emission/effluent |
| **Section 5** | Power to issue directions | Closure, prohibition, regulation |
| **Section 6** | Power to regulate industrial sites | No industry in specified areas |
| **Section 7** | Power to prescribe procedures | Handling hazardous substances |
| **Section 8** | Prohibition of handling hazardous substances beyond limit | Threshold quantities |
| **Section 9** | Power to examine industries | Records, samples, analysis |
| **Section 10** | Power of entry, inspection, and seizure | Authorised officers |
| **Section 11** | Power to take samples | For analysis from air, water, soil |
| **Section 12** | Power of environmental laboratories | Central/State Govt. labs for analysis |
| **Section 13** | Government Analysts | Appointed by Central/State Govt. |
| **Section 14** | Penalties — Cognizable, Non-Bailable | Jail up to 5 years / ₹1 lakh fine |
| **Section 15** | Penalty for contravention of provisions | As above + ₹5,000/day extra |
| **Section 16** | Offences by companies | Director/Manager liability |
| **Section 17** | Offences by Government Departments | Head of Department liable |
| **Section 19** | Citizen Suit Provision | 60 days' notice mandatory |
| **Section 25** | Rule-Making Power | Central Government |

### 2.4 Important Rules and Notifications Framed Under EPA 1986

The Act's flexibility comes from the many **Rules** notified under Section 25:

- **Hazardous Wastes (Management and Handling) Rules, 1989** (now replaced by 2016 Rules)
- **Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989** (MSIHC Rules)
- **Chemical Accidents (Emergency Planning, Preparedness and Response) Rules, 1996**
- **Bio-Medical Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 1998** (revised 2016)
- **Municipal Solid Wastes (Management and Handling) Rules, 2000** (revised 2016)
- **E-Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 2011** (revised 2016 & 2022)
- **Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2011** (amended 2016, 2018, 2021, 2022)
- **Construction and Demolition Waste Management Rules, 2016**
- **E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022** (latest)
- **Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022** (latest)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> Every industry in India (chemical, manufacturing, IT, construction, healthcare) must comply with EPA 1986 directly or indirectly. EIA consultants, environmental engineers, and auditors use these rules daily for compliance reporting, consent applications, and impact assessments.

### 2.5 Why is EPA 1986 Considered the "Mother Act"?

- It provides a **legal foundation** for almost every modern environmental rule.
- It enables the government to declare **new pollutants as "hazardous"** as science advances.
- It bridges jurisdictional gaps where Water/Air Acts are silent.
- It allows the Central Government to act **uniformly across States**, which is critical for trans-boundary pollution.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis & Compliance Matrix

### 3.1 Procedural Flowchart of Compliance Under EPA 1986

**Step 1 — Identification of Activity or Substance**

The Central Government, through the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) or Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), identifies:
- New pollutants not covered under Water/Air Act
- Hazardous substances based on toxicity, persistence, bioaccumulation
- Industrial activities with significant environmental footprint

**Step 2 — Drafting of Notification / Rule**

A draft notification is prepared and published in the **Official Gazette**, inviting public objections within a stipulated period (usually 30–60 days).

**Step 3 — Notification under Section 3 / Rule under Section 25**

After considering objections, the final notification is published. This becomes the **binding legal standard**.

**Step 4 — Implementation by CPCB / SPCB**

The CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board) and SPCB (State Pollution Control Boards) act as the implementing agencies. They:
- Monitor compliance
- Issue show-cause notices
- Recommend prosecution

**Step 5 — Enforcement under Sections 10–11**

Officers authorised by Section 10 may:
- Enter any premises
- Inspect records
- Take samples
- Seize equipment

**Step 6 — Prosecution under Section 14 / 15**

If violations continue, prosecution is launched. The offence is:
- **Cognizable**
- **Non-bailable**
- **Compoundable** (in some cases)

### 3.2 Penalty Matrix (Analytical Breakdown)

The penalty structure follows a **tiered logic** based on the severity and continuation of the offence.

$$
\text{Base Penalty} = \text{Imprisonment} \leq 5 \text{ years} \quad \text{OR} \quad \text{Fine} \leq \text{₹}1{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{OR} \quad \text{Both}
$$

For **continuing offences** (Section 15), an **additional fine** is levied:

$$
\text{Additional Fine} = \text{₹}5{,}000 \times \text{Number of Days of Continuation}
$$

For **offences committed by companies** (Section 16):

$$
\text{Liable Person} = \begin{cases} \text{Managing Director} & \text{if company is guilty} \\ \text{Any Director in-charge} & \text{if MD not identifiable} \\ \text{Any other officer} & \text{who consented to the offence} \end{cases}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example — Penalty Calculation

**Problem:** An industry violates Section 7 of EPA 1986 (improper handling of hazardous waste) for a continuous period of **120 days**. Compute the maximum financial liability.

**Step 1 — Identify Applicable Sections**

The violation is punishable under **Section 15** (penalty for contravention of provisions of the Act or rules).

**Step 2 — Compute Base Fine**

The maximum base fine for the offence is:

$$
F_{\text{base}} = \text{₹}1{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Additional Fine for Continuation**

For each day of continuation beyond the date of conviction:

$$
F_{\text{extra}} = \text{₹}5{,}000 \times 120 \text{ days} = \text{₹}6{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Total Financial Liability**

$$
F_{\text{total}} = F_{\text{base}} + F_{\text{extra}} = \text{₹}1{,}00{,}000 + \text{₹}6{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}7{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Add Imprisonment Liability**

Additionally, imprisonment up to **5 years** may be imposed, depending on the gravity of the offence.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, courts often consider factors like the **degree of harm**, **intent**, **cooperation**, and **previous record** before awarding the maximum penalty. The above is the **statutory ceiling**.

### 3.4 Comparative Strength Analysis — EPA 1986 vs. Other Environmental Laws

| Parameter | EPA 1986 | Water Act 1974 | Air Act 1981 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scope | Umbrella | Water pollution | Air pollution |
| Authority | Central Govt. | Central + State | Central + State |
| Penalty (Max Jail) | 5 years | 6 years | 6 years |
| Hazardous Substance Coverage | Yes (Section 2(e)) | No | No |
| Citizen Suit | Yes (Section 19) | Limited | Limited |
| Gap-Filling Power | Yes | No | No |

### 3.5 Critical Analysis — Strengths and Weaknesses

**Strengths:**

- **Flexibility** to address emerging environmental challenges.
- **Centralised power** ensures uniform national standards.
- **Citizen participation** through Section 19.
- **Deterrent penalties** ensure industry compliance.

**Weaknesses:**

- **No provision for absolute liability** of industries (unlike the Oleum Gas Leak case principle, later codified in **Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991**).
- **No compensation mechanism** for victims of pollution.
- **Over-criminalisation** — minor procedural lapses can attract criminal prosecution.
- **Reliance on subordinate legislation** — actual standards are buried in Rules and Notifications, reducing accessibility.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Structure of EPA 1986 — Compliance Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["Constitution of India"] --> node2["Article 48A and 51A g"]
    node2 --> node3["International Obligations - Stockholm 1972"]
    node3 --> node4["Environment Protection Act 1986"]
    node4 --> node5["Sections 1 to 26"]
    node4 --> node6["Rules and Notifications"]
    node4 --> node7["Implementing Agencies"]
    node5 --> node8["Section 3 - Powers of Central Govt"]
    node5 --> node9["Section 5 to 11 - Enforcement"]
    node5 --> node10["Section 14 to 17 - Penalties"]
    node5 --> node11["Section 19 - Citizen Suit"]
    node5 --> node12["Section 25 - Rule Making"]
    node6 --> node13["Hazardous Waste Rules 2016"]
    node6 --> node14["E Waste Rules 2022"]
    node6 --> node15["Plastic Waste Rules 2022"]
    node6 --> node16["MSW Rules 2016"]
    node6 --> node17["BMW Rules 2016"]
    node7 --> node18["CPCB - Central Pollution Control Board"]
    node7 --> node19["SPCB - State Pollution Control Boards"]
    node7 --> node20["MoEFCC - Ministry"]
```

### 4.2 Enforcement Flow Under EPA 1986

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Pollution or Violation Detected"] --> nodeB["Inspection by Authorised Officer Section 10"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Sample Collection Section 11"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Analysis by Govt Analyst Section 13"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"Violation Confirmed?"}
    nodeE -- "Yes" --> nodeF["Show Cause Notice"]
    nodeE -- "No" --> nodeG["Closure of Case"]
    nodeF --> nodeH{"Compliance by Industry?"}
    nodeH -- "No" --> nodeI["Directions under Section 5"]
    nodeH -- "Yes" --> nodeG
    nodeI --> nodeJ{"Still Non-Compliant?"}
    nodeJ -- "Yes" --> nodeK["Prosecution Section 14 and 15"]
    nodeJ -- "No" --> nodeG
    nodeK --> nodeL["Imprisonment up to 5 years or Fine up to 1 lakh"]
    nodeK --> nodeM["Additional Fine 5000 per day"]
```

### 4.3 Relationship Map — EPA 1986 and Other Environmental Laws

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["EPA 1986 Umbrella Act"] --> nodeB["Water Act 1974"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Air Act 1981"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Wildlife Protection Act 1972"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Forest Conservation Act 1980"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["Public Liability Insurance Act 1991"]
    nodeA --> nodeG["National Green Tribunal Act 2010"]
    nodeA --> nodeH["Biological Diversity Act 2002"]
    nodeA --> nodeI["Various Rules Hazardous Waste Plastic E Waste"]
    nodeI --> nodeI1["Hazardous Waste Management Rules 2016"]
    nodeI --> nodeI2["E Waste Management Rules 2022"]
    nodeI --> nodeI3["Plastic Waste Management Rules 2022"]
    nodeI --> nodeI4["Battery Waste Management Rules 2022"]
    nodeI --> nodeI5["Construction Demolition Waste Rules 2016"]
    nodeI --> nodeI6["Bio Medical Waste Management Rules 2016"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. Define the term "Environment" as per the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

As per **Section 2(a)** of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986, "Environment" includes **water, air, and land** and the **inter-relationship** which exists among and between water, air and land, and human beings, other living creatures, plants, micro-organism and property.

This definition is **holistic and inclusive** — it does not treat water, air, and land in isolation, but recognises the **dynamic inter-relationships** between them and all forms of life. The inclusion of "property" is unique and reflects the law's concern for environmental damage affecting man-made assets as well.

**(3 marks — 1 mark for citing Section, 1 mark for the three components, 1 mark for inter-relationship aspect)**

---

**Q2. Why is the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 called an "Umbrella Act"?** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 is called an **"Umbrella Act"** because of the following reasons:

1. It was enacted to **coordinate and supplement** various existing environmental laws such as the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974 and the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981.
2. It does not replace these laws but provides an **overarching legal framework** to fill gaps in environmental regulation.
3. It empowers the Central Government to issue **rules and notifications** for any emerging environmental concern not covered by specific statutes.
4. It serves as a **gap-filling mechanism** — addressing issues like hazardous substances, e-waste, plastic waste, etc., through subordinate legislation.
5. It allows the government to declare any substance as **"hazardous"** as and when scientific knowledge evolves, making it future-ready.

**(3 marks — distributed across the five points)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

**Q3 (A). Explain in detail the salient features and important provisions of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.** [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Historical Background and Constitutional Basis [7 Marks]**

The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 was enacted by the Indian Parliament in the wake of the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy** of December 1984, which exposed the inadequacy of existing environmental laws. Following the **United Nations Conference on the Human Environment at Stockholm (1972)**, India committed to establishing a national framework for environmental protection. The Act was passed under **Article 253** of the Constitution, which empowers Parliament to legislate on State List matters for implementing international agreements.

**Constitutional Backing:**

- **Article 48A (DPSP)** — Mandates the State to protect and improve the environment.
- **Article 51A(g) (Fundamental Duty)** — Mandates every citizen to protect the natural environment.
- **Article 253** — Source of Parliament's legislative competence.

**Definitions under the Act [1 Mark]:**

- **Environment** — Water, air, land and their inter-relationship with life and property.
- **Environmental Pollutant** — Any solid, liquid or gaseous substance injurious to environment.
- **Hazardous Substance** — Any substance notified as such by the Central Government.
- **Occupier** — Person having control over industry premises.
- **Emission** — Discharge of any environmental pollutant.
