**(b) Key Provisions and Enforcement Mechanism [7 Marks]**

**Section 3 — Powers of Central Government [1 Mark]:**
Empowers the Central Government to take all necessary measures to protect and improve the environment, and to set **environmental standards** for emission or discharge of environmental pollutants from any source.

**Sections 5–9 — Regulatory Powers [1 Mark]:**

- **Section 5** — Power to issue directions including closure or prohibition of industries.
- **Section 6** — Power to regulate industrial locations.
- **Section 7** — Prescribes procedures for handling hazardous substances.
- **Section 8** — Restricts handling of hazardous substances beyond prescribed limits.
- **Section 9** — Power to examine industries, access records, conduct analysis.

**Sections 10–13 — Inspection and Analysis [1 Mark]:**

- **Section 10** — Authorised officers can enter, inspect, and seize.
- **Section 11** — Power to take samples of air, water, soil.
- **Section 12** — Designation of environmental laboratories.
- **Section 13** — Appointment of Government Analysts.

**Sections 14–17 — Penalties [2 Marks]:**

- **Section 14** — Imprisonment up to **5 years** or fine up to **₹1 lakh**, or both. The offence is **cognizable and non-bailable**.
- **Section 15** — Additional fine of **₹5,000 per day** for continuing offences.
- **Section 16** — Offences by companies — MD/Director/Manager held liable.
- **Section 17** — Offences by Government Departments — Head of Department liable.

**Section 19 — Citizen Suit [1 Mark]:**
Any person can file a complaint before a court after giving **60 days' notice** to the Central or State Government, ensuring public participation in environmental enforcement.

**Section 25 — Rule-Making Power [1 Mark]:**
The Central Government is empowered to make rules for carrying out the purposes of the Act. Numerous critical rules have been framed under this section — Hazardous Waste Rules 2016, E-Waste Rules 2022, Plastic Waste Rules 2022, etc.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Part B:**
> 1. Students often **forget to cite Section numbers** — always mention "Section 5", "Section 14", etc. (-1 to -2 marks).
> 2. Many confuse **EPA 1986 with Water/Air Acts** — keep the central-government focus clear.
> 3. Avoid writing only the **list of rules** without explaining how they flow from Section 25.
> 4. **Penalties** are scoring — quote both the imprisonment term AND the fine amount.

---

**Q3 (B). Discuss the role of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 as an umbrella legislation in the Indian environmental legal framework. Compare it with the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974 and the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981.** [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Role of EPA 1986 as Umbrella Legislation [7 Marks]**

The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 functions as an **"Umbrella Act"** in the Indian environmental legal framework in the following ways:

**1. Gap-Filling Function:**
Pre-existing laws like the Water Act 1974 and the Air Act 1981 addressed only specific media (water and air). EPA 1986 was enacted to cover **all forms of environmental pollution** — soil, noise, hazardous substances, industrial accidents, and emerging concerns like e-waste, plastic waste, and biomedical waste.

**2. Coordination Function:**
The Act empowers the Central Government to **coordinate actions** under multiple environmental statutes and ensure they function in harmony. It also enables the government to set **uniform environmental standards** across the country.

**3. Flexibility Through Subordinate Legislation:**
Under **Section 25**, the Central Government can frame **rules and notifications** to address any new environmental issue. This has resulted in the creation of multiple modern rules — e.g., **E-Waste Management Rules 2022**, **Battery Waste Management Rules 2022**, **Plastic Waste Management Rules 2022** — which would not have been possible under older laws.

**4. Definition of "Hazardous Substance":**
EPA 1986 uniquely defines "hazardous substance" under **Section 2(e)** and provides for regulation of manufacture, storage, and import through the **MSIHC Rules, 1989**.

**5. Centralised Power:**
Unlike the Water and Air Acts, which involve both Central and State Governments, EPA 1986 vests **all major powers in the Central Government**, enabling uniform national action — critical for trans-boundary pollution.

**6. Citizen Empowerment:**
**Section 19** allows any citizen to file a case after 60 days' notice, promoting environmental justice.

**(b) Comparative Analysis with Water Act 1974 and Air Act 1981 [7 Marks]**

| Parameter | EPA 1986 | Water Act 1974 | Air Act 1981 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | All environmental pollution | Water pollution only | Air pollution only |
| **Trigger** | Bhopal Tragedy + Stockholm | Domestic concerns | Stockholm implementation |
| **Governance** | Central Government only | Central + State (CPCB + SPCB) | Central + State (CPCB + SPCB) |
| **Penalty Max Jail** | 5 years | 6 years | 6 years |
| **Hazardous Substances** | Covered (Section 2(e)) | Not covered | Not covered |
| **Citizen Suit** | Section 19 | Limited | Limited |
| **Rule-Making** | Section 25 (very broad) | Section 64 | Section 53 |
| **Coverage of New Issues** | Yes (E-waste, plastic, etc.) | No | No |

**Key Differentiators [1 Mark Each]:**

1. **Subject Matter:** EPA 1986 is the broadest — it covers water, air, land, and emerging pollution types. Water and Air Acts are **media-specific**.
2. **Authority Structure:** EPA 1986 centralises power; Water/Air Acts use a **federal model** with CPCB and SPCBs.
3. **Penalty Severity:** Water and Air Acts allow imprisonment up to **6 years**; EPA 1986 allows up to **5 years**.
4. **Hazardous Substance Coverage:** Only EPA 1986 directly regulates hazardous substances.
5. **Flexibility:** EPA 1986 is more **adaptive** because of its subordinate rule-making power.

**Conclusion:** EPA 1986 does not replace the Water and Air Acts but **strengthens, supplements, and bridges gaps** in the Indian environmental legal regime.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Comparison Question:**
> 1. Avoid one-sided comparison — cover **all three** laws structurally.
> 2. **Do not mix up authorities** — CPCB is Central, SPCB is State.
> 3. Quote **Section numbers** for maximum marks.
> 4. Conclude with a clear statement on the **complementary** (not competitive) role of the Acts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Revision Checklist — The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**

- **Enactment Date:** 23rd May 1986 (notified); 19th November 1986 (in force).
- **Trigger:** Bhopal Gas Tragedy (December 1984) + Stockholm Conference (1972).
- **Constitutional Source:** Article 253 of the Constitution.
- **Definitions (Must Memorise):** Environment, Environmental Pollutant, Hazardous Substance, Occupier, Emission.
- **Umbrella Character:** Coexists with Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, and others.
- **Key Sections:**
  - Section 3 — Powers of Central Government
  - Section 5 — Power to issue directions
  - Section 6 — Regulate industrial sites
  - Sections 7–8 — Hazardous substances handling
  - Sections 10–11 — Entry, inspection, sampling
  - Section 14 — Penalty (5 years jail / ₹1 lakh fine)
  - Section 15 — Additional fine (₹5,000 per day for continuation)
  - Section 19 — Citizen Suit (60-day notice)
  - Section 25 — Rule-Making Power
- **Important Rules Framed Under Section 25:**
  - Hazardous Waste Management Rules 2016
  - E-Waste Management Rules 2022
  - Plastic Waste Management Rules 2022
  - Battery Waste Management Rules 2022
  - Biomedical Waste Management Rules 2016
  - Construction & Demolition Waste Rules 2016
  - MSIHC Rules 1989
- **Penalty Formula:** $F_{\text{total}} = F_{\text{base}} + (5{,}000 \times \text{days of continuation})$
- **Cognizable and Non-Bailable Offence** — enhances deterrence.
- **No compensation mechanism** for victims — a major criticism of the Act.
- **Citizen Suit:** 60-day mandatory notice before approaching the court.
- **Compare with:** Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981 — central vs. federal governance.
- **Engineering Relevance:** Every environmental compliance audit, EIA report, and consent application in India traces back to EPA 1986 or its rules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981** is an enabling **legislative framework** enacted by the Indian Parliament (Act No. 14 of 1981) that came into force on **16th May 1981**, with the primary objective of **preserving the quality of air** and **controlling atmospheric pollution** across the territorial boundaries of India. The Act confers upon the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** and the **State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs)** the statutory authority to plan, execute, and enforce nation-wide programmes for the prevention, control, and abatement of air pollution.

The Act was passed under **Article 252(1)** of the Indian Constitution, initially applicable to the Union Territories, but it gained **pan-Indian status** once adequate number of State Resolutions were passed by the State Legislatures authorizing Parliament to legislate on this subject (which is otherwise in the **State List – Entry 6 of List II** of the Seventh Schedule).

> [!NOTE]
> **Statutory Sibling:** The Air Act, 1981 must always be studied in conjunction with the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**, because they share the same institutional architecture (CPCB and SPCBs), and many amendments to the Air Act have been made *via* amendments to the Water Act (e.g., the 1987 amendment).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Atmospheric Traffic Police"

Imagine a busy Indian city junction (say, Majestic Junction, Bengaluru) without any traffic police. Vehicles (industrial emissions) would spew black smoke, blow horns arbitrarily, and park anywhere — leading to complete chaos and a "polluted" environment. 

Now appoint a **uniformed authority** (the **Pollution Control Boards**) with:
- A **uniform rulebook** (the Act itself) — defining what constitutes an "offence".
- **Authority to check vehicles** (consent to operate under **Section 21**).
- **Power to issue challans** (penalties under **Section 25**).
- **Appellate authority** to hear grievances (**Section 31** – Appellate Authority).

The Air Act of 1981 is exactly this — an **"Atmospheric Traffic Police Force"** for the whole nation, with **CPCB** acting as the **Central Headquarters Inspector-General** and **SPCBs** acting as **State-level Station House Officers (SHOs)** enforcing rules within their jurisdiction.

## 1.3 Key Physical and Statutory Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Date of Enactment:** **16th May 1981** (Commencement Date).
> - **Original Gazette Citation:** **Act No. 14 of 1981**.
> - **Constitutional Basis:** **Article 252(1)** of the Constitution of India.
> - **Subject Matter Location:** Originally **State List (Entry 6)** — Concurrent post-42nd Amendment.
> - **Implementing Ministries:** Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) at Central level; State Departments of Environment at State level.
> - **Parent Authority:** Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), established under Section 3 of the Water Act, 1974.

## 1.4 Salient Features at a Glance

1. **Prevention-based, not just Control-based:** Title explicitly includes *Prevention*, indicating a *proactive* legal mandate.
2. **Consent Regime:** Section 21 mandates obtaining "Consent to Establish" and "Consent to Operate" before commissioning any industrial plant.
3. **Penal Provisions:** Section 25 prescribes imprisonment up to **6 years** and fine up to **₹1 lakh** (subsequently enhanced).
4. **Designated Standards Authority:** CPCB is the designated body to lay down **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)**.
5. **Jurisdictional Reach:** Extends to the whole of India.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Matrix

## 2.1 The Legislative Genesis – Why the Act Was Needed

The **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (December 2–3, 1984)** occurred *after* the enactment of the Act, but a series of preceding events catalysed the legislative urgency:

- **Stockholm Conference (1972)** on the Human Environment.
- **Indian Council for Environmental Legal Research** recommendations.
- **Bhargava Committee Report (1976)** – recommended a comprehensive air pollution law.
- Public pressure following the **Mathura Oil Refinery** controversy.
- Absence of any specific statutory mechanism to penalize emitters of toxic gases.

The Act was therefore the **first dedicated statute in India** to address air pollution as a distinct legal and scientific subject (the Water Act, 1974 had already set the precedent for institutional architecture).

## 2.2 Chapter-wise Structural Breakdown of the Act

The Act consists of **7 Chapters** and **54 Sections**. For KTU 14-mark answers, the following table is the **highest-yield structural memory aid**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Chapter-wise Architecture of The Air Act, 1981**

| Chapter No. | Chapter Title | Sections Covered | Key Provision(s) | KTU Weightage (High/Med/Low) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| I | Preliminary | Sections 1 – 2 | Short title, extent, commencement, **Definitions** | **HIGH** |
| II | Central and State Boards for the Prevention and Control of Air Pollution | Sections 3 – 15 | Constitution of State Boards, Joint Boards | **HIGH** |
| III | Powers and Functions of Boards | Sections 16 – 18 | Functions of CPCB and SPCBs | **HIGH** |
| IV | Prevention and Control of Air Pollution | Sections 19 – 31 | Consent mechanism, Directions, Penalties, Appeals | **HIGHEST** |
| V | Miscellaneous | Sections 32 – 54 | Cognizance, Offences by companies, Power to make rules | **MEDIUM** |
| VI (Inserted by 1987 Amendment) | Funds, Accounts and Audit | Sections 33A onward | Finance of Boards | **LOW** |
| VII (Inserted by 1987 Amendment) | Various amendments to the Water Act | — | Consequential amendments | **LOW** |

## 2.3 Critical Definitions – Section 2 (Mandatory for KTU 3-Mark Questions)

| Term | Statutory Definition (Simplified for KTU) | Engineering/Industrial Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **"Air Pollutant"** (Sec 2(a)) | Any solid, liquid, or gaseous substance present in the atmosphere in such concentration as may be *injurious* to human health, animal life, vegetation, or property. | Forms the legal basis for identifying which emissions are actionable. |
| **"Air Pollution"** (Sec 2(b)) | Presence of any air pollutant in the atmosphere. | Universal definition — even trace levels qualify. |
| **"Approved"** (Sec 2(c)) | Approved by the Board. | Any equipment/method not approved is legally suspect. |
| **"Industrial Plant"** (Sec 2(i)) | Any plant used for industrial or trade purposes emitting air pollutants. | The fundamental *subject* of consent under Sec 21. |
| **"Occupier"** (Sec 2(j)) | The person who has **effective control over the affairs** of the factory/premises. | Liability attaches to the *occupier*, not the owner. |
| **"Prescribed"** (Sec 2(l)) | Prescribed by rules made under the Act. | All delegated legislative actions must trace to Rule-making power. |
| **"State Board"** (Sec 2(n)) | State Pollution Control Board constituted under Sec 4. | Primary enforcement agency at State level. |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Knowledge Matrix: Powers & Functions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Functions of the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) – Section 16**

1. Advise the Central Government on matters concerning prevention, control, and abatement of air pollution.
2. Coordinate the activities of State Boards and resolve disputes among them.
3. Lay down standards for the **quality of air** (i.e., **NAAQS**).
4. Collect and disseminate information relating to air pollution.
5. Plan and execute a nationwide programme for prevention, control, and abatement of air pollution.
6. Plan and cause to be executed nation-wide programmes for mass education and awareness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Functions of the State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) – Section 17**

1. Inspect, at all reasonable times, any **control equipment**, **industrial plant**, or **manufacturing process** to ascertain compliance.
2. Advise the State Government on matters for prevention/control of air pollution.
3. Lay down standards for emissions from **automobiles** (with reference to fuel quality).
4. Receive applications for **Consent** under Section 21 and grant/refuse the same.
5. Carry out the directions issued by the CPCB.

## 2.5 The Consent Mechanism – Operational Heart of the Act

> [!NOTE]
> The **Consent Mechanism** is the most examinable feature of the Act. Memorize the two-stage architecture:

**Stage 1: Consent to Establish (CTE)**
- Applied under **Section 21** before construction/erection of any industrial plant in an air pollution control area.
- Granted for a **fixed validity period**.
- Establishes that the proposed plant has adequate pollution control equipment *in design*.

**Stage 2: Consent to Operate (CTO)**
- Applied *after* installation, before commissioning.
- Confirms that the installed equipment functions as designed.
- Subject to periodic renewal (typically every **5 years**, as per State variation).

**Section 22** specifies the **application procedure**; **Section 23** mandates the **Board to either grant or refuse consent within 4 months** (failing which refusal is deemed, appealable under Section 31).

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Practice

- **Environmental Engineers:** Must conduct **stack monitoring**, **ambient air quality monitoring**, and submit **consent compliance reports** to SPCBs.
- **Civil/Mechanical Engineers:** Plant design must incorporate **Air Pollution Control Equipment (APCE)** — ESPs (Electrostatic Precipitators), Scrubbers, Cyclones, Bag Filters.
- **Industrial Project Approval:** EIA studies and Consent to Establish/Operate are pre-requisites for **Environmental Clearance (EC)** under **EIA Notification, 2006**.
- **Litigation:** Any deviation from consent conditions constitutes an **offence** under Section 25 (now Section 25/26 post-1987 amendment).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix and Exhaustive Sectional Analysis

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Logical Analysis: The Consent Mechanism Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked analytical question** in KTU examinations (worth up to 7 marks). The step-by-step logical flow is as follows:

**Step 1: Declaration of Air Pollution Control Area (APCA)**
- Initiating Authority: **State Government** under **Section 19** of the Act.
- Action: The State Government, in consultation with the State Board, declares any area as an "Air Pollution Control Area" where standards of air quality are exceeded.
- Trigger: Scientific evidence of air pollution in excess of NAAQS.

**Step 2: Submission of Application for Consent**
- Applicant: **Occupier** of the industrial plant.
- Form: As prescribed by the State Board (typically **Form-I** for CTE and **Form-II** for CTO under Air Rules).
- Authority: Submitted to the **State Pollution Control Board** of the relevant State/UT.
- Statutory Reference: **Section 21** read with **Section 22** of the Act.

**Step 3: Scrutiny by the State Board**
- The Board evaluates the application on parameters such as:
  - Type of industry and its pollution potential.
  - Proposed **Air Pollution Control Measures (APCMs)**.
  - **Stack height** as per CPCB guidelines.
  - Proximity to the **Air Pollution Control Area (APCA)**.
  - Compliance history (if the unit is an expansion of an existing unit).

**Step 4: Grant or Refusal of Consent**
- The Board must communicate its decision **within 4 months** of receipt of the application.
- If granted → **Consent Order** issued with conditions.
- If refused → Refusal order issued, stating reasons.
- Failure to decide within 4 months = **Deemed Refusal** (which is itself appealable).

**Step 5: Compliance and Monitoring**
- Once Consent is granted, the occupier must:
  - Install approved **Air Pollution Control Equipment (APCE)**.
  - Maintain **stack monitoring** records.
  - Submit **quarterly/half-yearly compliance reports**.
  - Allow **Board officials** to inspect premises at *any reasonable time* under Section 24.

**Step 6: Renewal of Consent**
- Consent has a finite validity (commonly 5 years, but varies by State).
- Renewal application must be filed **at least 120 days** before expiry (in most States).
- The Board may **refuse, revoke, or modify** the consent if conditions are violated — **Section 23(2)**.

**Step 7: Appeals**
- Aggrieved party can appeal to the **Appellate Authority** constituted under **Section 31** of the Act.
- Appellate Authority = Secretary to the State Government in charge of Environment.
- Appeal must be filed within **30 days** of the order.
- The decision of the Appellate Authority is final, subject to writ jurisdiction of the High Court under Article 226.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Sectional Analysis: Sections 21, 22, 23, 24, 25, 26, 27

> [!NOTE]
> This forms the **exhaustive analytical matrix** for any 14-mark KTU question on "Salient Features of the Air Act, 1981" or "Consent Mechanism."

### **Section 21: Prohibition of Operation of Certain Industrial Plants Without Consent**

- **Substance:** No person shall, without the prior consent of the State Board, establish or operate any industrial plant in an air pollution control area.
- **Legal Consequence:** Operation without consent is a punishable offence.
- **Key Words:** "Establish", "Operate", "Air Pollution Control Area", "Prior consent".

### **Section 22: Application for Consent**

- **Substance:** Every application for consent shall be made in the prescribed form and shall be accompanied by prescribed fees.
- **Engineering Implication:** Documentation must be meticulous; fees are non-refundable.

### **Section 23: Grant, Refusal or Cancellation of Consent**

- **Substance:** The State Board may grant consent subject to such conditions as it may deem fit, or refuse consent for reasons recorded in writing. It may also cancel consent if conditions are violated.
- **Time Limit:** Decision within **4 months** of application receipt.
- **Deemed Refusal:** Failure to communicate within 4 months.

### **Section 24: Power to Declare Air Pollution Control Areas**

- **Substance:** The State Government, in consultation with the State Board and after giving reasonable opportunity to affected parties, may declare any area as an "Air Pollution Control Area."
- **Engineering Implication:** Stricter emission norms apply within declared APCAs.

### **Section 25: Penalties for Contravention of Provisions**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Original Penalty Structure (Pre-1987):**
> - Imprisonment: Up to **3 months** (first offence) / Up to **6 months + fine** (subsequent offence).
> - Fine: Up to **₹1,000** (first) / **₹5,000** (subsequent).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Enhanced Penalty Structure (Post-1987 Amendment):**
> - Imprisonment: Up to **6 years** OR fine up to **₹5,000 per day** of continued violation — whichever is higher.
> - Enhanced for industries operating without consent or violating consent conditions.
> - Subsequent offence: Minimum imprisonment of **2 years** (cognizable, non-bailable).

### **Section 26: Offences by Companies**

- **Substance:** Where any offence is committed by a company, every person who, at the time of the offence, was in charge of and responsible to the company for the conduct of the business — shall be deemed guilty.
- **Vicarious Liability:** Applies to **Directors, Managers, Secretaries**, and any other officer in-charge.
- **Defence:** If the person proves the offence was committed **without their knowledge** or that they had exercised all due diligence to prevent it.

### **Section 27: Cognizance of Offences**

- **Substance:** No court shall take cognizance of any offence except on a **complaint made by the Board** or any officer authorized by the Board.
- **No court inferior to a Metropolitan Magistrate or Judicial Magistrate First Class** shall try any offence.
- **Bail:** Offences are **bailable** (originally); however, post-amendments, certain offences are **non-bailable**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pre-1987 vs. Post-1987 Amendments

| Aspect | Pre-1987 Position | Post-1987 Amendment Position |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Penalties (Section 25)** | Max 3 months imprisonment + ₹1,000 fine | Up to 6 years + ₹5,000/day continuing fine |
| **Constitution of State Board** | State Govt could refuse to constitute | Mandatorily required; Central Board can step in |
| **Power to remove difficulties** | Limited | Central Govt can remove difficulties (Sec 63A) |
| **Funds & Audit** | Ad hoc | Formalized — State Board can receive grants from CPCB and multilateral agencies |
| **Offences by Govt Departments** | No specific provision | Deemed guilty if knowingly in default |
| **Locus Standi for Prosecution** | Restrictive | Broadened to include any officer authorized by Board |

## 3.4 The Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Rules, 1982

These are the **subsidiary legislation** framed under **Section 53** of the Act. They prescribe:

- **Form-I:** Application for consent.
- **Form-II:** Notice of refusal to grant consent.
- **Form-III:** Form of appeal under Section 31.
- **Schedule I:** Lists **parameters and their tolerance limits** for emissions from various industries.
- **Schedule II:** Quality of fuel that may be used.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU purposes, always cite the parent Section (e.g., "Rule 4 of the Air Rules, 1982 read with Section 22 of the Air Act, 1981") for full marks in an answer.

## 3.5 Judicial Pronouncements of KTU-Level Importance

| Case Name | Year | Key Principle Established | KTU Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **M.C. Mehta v. Union of India** | 1985 (Oleum Gas Leak) | Absolute Liability doctrine; Hazardous industries owe an absolute, non-delegable duty. | Reinforces spirit of the Air Act. |
| **Indian Council for Enviro-Legal Action v. Union of India** | 1996 (Bichhri Village) | Polluter Pays principle; Industries must compensate affected villagers. | Operational principle behind Sec 25 penalties. |
| **Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India** | 1996 | Precautionary Principle; Sustainable Development. | Foundation of all environmental statutes including Air Act. |
| **Municipal Council, Ratlam v. Vardhichand** | 1980 | Even municipalities must enforce environmental duties. | Subordinate authorities cannot plead financial inability. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Architecture of Air Pollution Control Authorities

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Constitution of India] --> B[Article 252 Para 1]
    B --> C[Air Act 1981 Enacted]
    C --> D[Central Pollution Control Board CPCB]
    C --> E[State Pollution Control Boards SPCBs]
    D --> F[Advises Central Government]
    D --> G[Lays down NAAQS Standards]
    D --> H[Coordinates between State Boards]
    E --> I[Grants Refuses Consent under Sec 21]
    E --> J[Inspects Industrial Plants under Sec 17]
    E --> K[Enforces Penalties under Sec 25]
    I --> L[Occupier of Industrial Plant]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Appellate Authority under Sec 31]
    M --> N[High Court under Article 226]
```

## 4.2 Two-Stage Consent Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Project Conception Industrial Plant] --> P2[State Govt declares APCA under Sec 19]
    P2 --> P3[Occupier submits Application for Consent to Establish CTE]
    P3 --> P4{State Board Evaluation}
    P4 --> P5[Grant CTE with conditions]
    P4 --> P6[Refuse CTE Deemed refusal if no decision in 4 months]
    P5 --> P7[Construction and Installation of APCE]
    P7 --> P8[Application for Consent to Operate CTO]
    P8 --> P9{State Board Verification of Installed APCE}
    P9 --> P10[Grant CTO with validity period 5 years typical]
    P9 --> P11[Refuse CTO]
    P10 --> P12[Periodic Compliance Monitoring]
    P12 --> P13{Renewal Required}
    P13 --> P14[File Renewal Application 120 days prior]
    P13 --> P15[Continue Operation]
    P6 --> P16[Appeal to Appellate Authority Sec 31 within 30 days]
    P11 --> P16
```

## 4.3 Penalty and Prosecution Flow (Section 25-27)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1[Violation Detected Operating without consent or breach of conditions] --> Q2[SPCB Inspection or Complaint]
    Q2 --> Q3[Show Cause Notice by SPCB]
    Q3 --> Q4{Occupier Responds within stipulated time}
    Q4 --> Q5[Adherence and Remedial Action]
    Q4 --> Q6[No Response or Unsatisfactory Response]
    Q6 --> Q7[Board Authorizes Prosecution under Sec 25]
    Q7 --> Q8[Court takes Cognizance under Sec 27]
    Q8 --> Q9[Magistrate First Class or Metropolitan Magistrate]
    Q9 --> Q10[Conviction Imprisonment up to 6 years and fine]
    Q10 --> Q11[Subsequent Offence Minimum 2 years imprisonment non bailable]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture – The Air Act as a Control System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[INPUT Industrial Emissions from Plants] --> A2[CONTROL UNIT State Pollution Control Board]
    A2 --> A3[Actuator Consent Grant Refusal Cancellation]
    A2 --> A4[Sensor Stack Monitoring Ambient Air Quality Monitoring]
    A3 --> A5[OUTPUT Compliance or Prosecution]
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6[FEEDBACK Compliance Reports to CPCB]
    A6 --> A1
    A2 -.-> A7[Advisory Layer CPCB National Standards NAAQS]
    A7 -.-> A2
```

## 4.5 Appeals and Dispute Resolution Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Original Order of SPCB Grant Refusal Cancellation] --> S2[Aggrieved Party]
    S2 --> S3[Appeal to Appellate Authority Sec 31 within 30 days]
    S3 --> S4{Decision of Appellate Authority}
    S4 --> S5[Final Adjudication]
    S5 --> S6[Further Remedy Writ Petition to High Court Article 226]
    S6 --> S7[Supreme Court of India Article 136]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define the term "Air Pollutant" and "Air Pollution" as per The Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981. Why was this Act enacted?**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Air Pollutant [Sec 2(a)]:** Any solid, liquid, or gaseous substance present in the atmosphere in such concentration as may be *injurious or potentially injurious* to human beings, other living creatures, plants, property, or the environment.
- **Air Pollution [Sec 2(b)]:** The presence in the atmosphere of one or more air pollutants in such quantities and duration as may be, or may tend to be, injurious to human health or welfare, animal or plant life, or property.
- **Reason for Enactment:** Following the Stockholm Conference (1972) and the recommendations of the Bhargava Committee (1976), it was felt that despite the Water Act, 1974, no comprehensive statute addressed atmospheric pollution. The Act was therefore enacted to provide a dedicated legal framework for the prevention, control, and abatement of air pollution in India.

### **Question 2: Differentiate between the functions of CPCB and SPCB under the Air Act, 1981.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | CPCB (Sec 16) | SPCB (Sec 17) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Jurisdiction** | National | State-level |
| **Primary Function** | Lays down NAAQS, coordinates SPCBs | Direct enforcement via consent mechanism |
| **Key Power** | Advisory, standard-setting | Inspectional, prosecutorial |
| **Source Authority** | Sec 3 of Water Act, 1974 | Sec 4 of Air Act, 1981 |

- The **CPCB** plays an **advisory and standard-setting** role (e.g., NAAQS, emission guidelines).
- The **SPCBs** are the **operational arms** of the Act, directly granting consent, inspecting premises, and prosecuting violators.
- The CPCB coordinates and resolves disputes *between* SPCBs.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students commonly confuse **Sections 21, 22, and 23**. Memorize the sequence: **Section 21** = *Prohibition*; **Section 22** = *Application*; **Section 23** = *Grant/Refusal*.
> 2. Many students incorrectly write that the Air Act applies to the whole of India *originally* — it gained pan-Indian application only through State Resolutions under Article 252(1). One mark is deducted for this error.
> 3. Penalty figures (₹1,000 / ₹5,000 / ₹5,000 per day) are *numerically* asked. Do not write vague terms like "heavy fine."
> 4. **Section 31** is the *Appellate Authority*, NOT the High Court. Do not confuse the two.
> 5. Use exact Section numbers (Sec 21, Sec 25) in answers — not generic phrases like "the relevant section."

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **Consent Mechanism** established under The Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981. Discuss the roles of **Sections 21, 22, 23, and 24** in this regard. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **powers and functions of the Central Pollution Control Board** and the **State Pollution Control Boards** under the Act. How do they coordinate in the implementation of air pollution control measures? **(7 marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) The Consent Mechanism (Sections 21, 22, 23, 24):**

The Consent Mechanism is the **operational heart** of the Air Act, 1981. It is a two-stage regulatory approval process that ensures no industrial plant emits pollutants without statutory sanction.

**1. Section 21 – Prohibition of Operation Without Consent:**
This section states that no person shall, without the **prior consent of the State Board**, establish or operate any industrial plant in any air pollution control area declared under Section 19. The provision is **mandatory**; non-compliance is a **cognizable offence**.

**2. Section 22 – Application for Consent:**
An application for consent must be made in the **prescribed form** (Form-I under Air Rules, 1982) and accompanied by **prescribed fees**. The application must include details of the industry, manufacturing process, raw materials, fuels used, and proposed air pollution control equipment.

**3. Section 23 – Grant, Refusal, or Cancellation:**
The State Board, after due enquiry, may **grant consent subject to conditions** (e.g., installation of specific APCEs, stack height, monitoring frequency), or **refuse consent** for reasons recorded in writing. The Board is mandated to communicate its decision **within 4 months**; failure to do so is a **deemed refusal**. The Board can also **cancel** an existing consent if the conditions are violated.

**4. Section 24 – Power to Declare APCA:**
The State Government, in consultation with the SPCB, declares any area with high pollution levels as an **Air Pollution Control Area (APCA)**. Within an APCA, all industrial plants must mandatorily obtain consent.

**Engineering Significance:** The mechanism ensures that *both* the **design intent** and the **operational reality** of pollution control are verified — once before establishment (CTE) and once before operation (CTO). **(7 marks)**

**(b) Powers and Functions of CPCB and SPCBs (Sections 16, 17, 18):**

**1. Central Pollution Control Board – Section 16:**
- **Advisory role:** Advise the Central Government on matters of air pollution prevention, control, and abatement.
- **Standard-setting role:** Lay down **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** for the entire country.
- **Coordinating role:** Coordinate the activities of State Boards and resolve disputes between them.
- **Research & training:** Plan and execute nation-wide programmes; collect and disseminate information.
- **Mass awareness:** Undertake programmes for public education on air pollution.

**2. State Pollution Control Boards – Section 17:**
- **Inspection:** Inspect any control equipment, industrial plant, or manufacturing process at all reasonable times.
- **Advisory role:** Advise the State Government on air pollution matters.
- **Automobile standards:** Lay down standards for emissions from automobiles (in consultation with CPCB).
- **Consent authority:** Receive and decide applications for consent under Section 21.
- **Compliance enforcement:** Direct any person to take measures to prevent, control, or abate air pollution under Section 18.

**3. Coordination Mechanism:**
- The **CPCB is the apex body**; SPCBs are the **state-level operational arms**.
- CPCB issues **uniform national standards**; SPCBs **implement** them with local adaptation.
- Under **Section 18**, SPCBs are bound to follow the **directions of the CPCB** and the Central Government, ensuring a unified enforcement chain.
- In case of inter-state disputes, the **CPCB acts as mediator**, ensuring the Act operates as a *single, integrated legal regime* across India. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice:**
**(a)** What are the **penalties and offences** under The Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981? Discuss **Sections 25, 26, and 27** with reference to the 1987 amendments. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Critically analyze the **role of the State Pollution Control Board** in implementing the Air Act, 1981, with reference to the **consent mechanism**, **inspection powers**, and **limitations** of the existing framework. **(7 marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Penalties and Offences (Sections 25, 26, 27):**

**1. Section 25 – Penalties (As Amended in 1987):**
- **Original Position (1981):** Imprisonment up to **3 months** + fine up to **₹1,000** for first offence; up to **6 months** + fine up to **₹5,000** for subsequent offence.
- **Post-1987 Amendment:** Drastically enhanced — imprisonment up to **6 years** OR fine up to **₹5,000 per day** during which the contravention continues (whichever is higher).
- **Subsequent Offence:** Minimum imprisonment of **2 years** — these offences are now **cognizable and non-bailable**.

**2. Section 26 – Offences by Companies:**
- When an offence is committed by a **company**, every person in charge of and responsible for the conduct of the business (Directors, Managers, Secretaries) is deemed guilty.
- **Defence Available:** The accused can prove the offence was committed *without their knowledge* or that they exercised *all due diligence* to prevent it.
- **Government Departments:** Even government agencies are covered.

**3. Section 27 – Cognizance of Offences:**
- No court shall take cognizance except on a **complaint made by the Board** or an officer authorized by the Board.
- The case is triable only by a **Metropolitan Magistrate** or **Judicial Magistrate First Class**.
- This provision ensures that prosecutions are **not frivolous** and originate from the technical authority that understands pollution science. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Critical Analysis of the Role of SPCBs:**

**Strengths:**
- **Decentralization:** SPCBs bring enforcement closer to the operational level, allowing for ground-level inspection and rapid response.
- **Statutory Authority:** Consent mechanism under Section 21 gives SPCBs *prior* control over industrial establishment.
- **Inspection Power:** Section 17 grants unrestricted access to industrial plants, ensuring compliance verification.

**Limitations:**
- **Understaffing:** Most SPCBs suffer from severe shortage of scientific and technical manpower, weakening enforcement.
- **Funding Constraints:** Many SPCBs are financially dependent on State Governments, leading to **political interference**.
- **Penal Powers are Weak:** Despite the 1987 enhancement, **conviction rates remain abysmally low** (often less than 5%) — pollution cases drag for years in courts.
- **Self-regulation Conflict:** SPCBs are often expected to *promote* industrialization and *control* it simultaneously — a **structural conflict of interest**.

**Conclusion:** While the SPCBs are the *backbone* of air pollution governance in India, their effectiveness is **diluted by institutional, financial, and political constraints**. A successful implementation of the Air Act requires **institutional strengthening**, **ring-fenced finances**, and **judicial fast-tracking of environmental cases**. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – The Air Act, 1981 (Module 1)**

### **A. Constitutional and Statutory Foundation**
- Enacted under **Article 252(1)** of the Indian Constitution.
- **Act No. 14 of 1981**; commenced on **16th May 1981**.
- Applies to whole of India via State Resolutions.
- Air pollution is in **Concurrent List** post-42nd Amendment.

### **B. Institutional Architecture**
- **CPCB** (under Sec 16) — National-level, advisory + standard-setting.
- **SPCB** (under Sec 17) — State-level, operational + enforcement.
- **Joint Board** (under Sec 5) — For inter-state industrial clusters.
- **Appellate Authority** (under Sec 31) — Secretary to State Government.

### **C. Consent Mechanism (Memorize the Flow)**
- **Sec 21** = Prohibition (no plant without consent).
- **Sec 22** = Application procedure.
- **Sec 23** = Grant/Refusal/Cancellation — 4-month time limit.
- **Sec 24** = Power to declare APCA.
- **Sec 31** = Appeal — 30-day limit.

### **D. Penalties (Numerically Important)**
- **Pre-1987:** 3 months / ₹1,000 (first) → 6 months / ₹5,000 (subsequent).
- **Post-1987:** 6 years imprisonment + ₹5,000 per day fine.
- **Subsequent offence:** Minimum 2 years, non-bailable.

### **E. Critical Definitions (Memorize the Following Eight)**
1. Air Pollutant
2. Air Pollution
3. Industrial Plant
4. Occupier
5. State Board
6. Approved
7. Prescribed
8. Air Pollution Control Area

### **F. Common Pitfalls (Avoid These in KTU Exam)**
- ❌ Confusing CPCB (CPCB = National) with SPCB (SPCB = State).
- ❌ Forgetting that the 1987 amendment *strengthened* penalties — this is a regular KTU question.
- ❌ Writing "Air Act is a Central legislation under the State List" — **wrong**: it is a **Central legislation** enacted using **Article 252**.
- ❌ Citing Section 31 as the High Court — **wrong**: Section 31 is the **Appellate Authority**, distinct from Article 226 jurisdiction.
- ❌ Omitting the **two-stage** nature of the consent mechanism (CTE + CTO).

### **G. Related Rules and Amendments to Mention for Full Marks**
- **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Rules, 1982** (under Section 53).
- **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Amendment Act, 1987** — major penalty enhancement.
- **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS), 2009** — issued by CPCB under Section 16.
- **EIA Notification, 2006** — operates *concurrently* with the Air Act for major projects.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes – The Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974 ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** (commonly called the **Water Act, 1974**) is an umbrella legislation enacted by the **Parliament of India** under **Article 252** of the Constitution to provide for the prevention and control of water pollution and the maintaining or restoring of wholesomeness of water in the country. It is the first comprehensive environmental statute enacted at the central level in independent India, specifically targeting **water quality management** in streams, rivers, wells, lakes, and seas. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Article 252 Basis:** The Act was originally adopted by Parliament under Article 252(1) of the Constitution, which allows the central legislature to legislate on state-subject matters provided **two or more state legislatures consent** to such legislation. Initially applicable only to participating states, it was later extended to all Indian states via subsequent amendments. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **large residential apartment complex** where every resident (industry/municipality) independently pumps water from a shared underground tank (rivers/lakes) and dumps waste back into it. Without any "society rules," the tank becomes murky and unusable for everyone. **Analogy Mapping:** - **Shared Tank** = Water bodies (rivers, lakes, aquifers) — a *commons* resource - **Residents polluting** = Industrial effluents and sewage discharge - **Society Committee** = **Pollution Control Boards (CPCB & SPCBs)** appointed under the Act - **Society Rules & Penalties** = **Consent mechanism, standards, and penalties** under Sections 25, 26, and 43 - **Building Inspector** = **Board officers** with powers of entry, inspection, and sampling under Section 23 The Act essentially creates the **"Society Rules" (legal framework)** and appoints the **"Committee" (Boards)** to enforce water quality, ensuring the commons remain usable for everyone. ### 1.3 Salient Features of the Act > [!NOTE] > **Core Salient Features (Must-Know for KTU Exams):** > 1. **Long Title:** An Act to provide for the prevention and control of water pollution and the maintaining or restoring of wholesomeness of water. > 2. **Objective:** To confer powers and assign functions on the Central and State Pollution Control Boards for the prevention and control of water pollution. > 3. **Applicability:** Extends to the whole of India (after 1988 amendment, all states covered). > 4. **Source of Power:** Article 252 of the Constitution (legislative entry 17, State List). > 5. **Commencement:** Came into force on **23rd March, 1974** (with state-specific dates). > 6. **Key Authorities Created:** Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) and State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs). ### 1.4 Key Definitions under the Act (Section 2) The Act defines several critical terms in **Section 2**. For KTU examinations, the following definitions are high-yield: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Section 2 Definitions:** > - **"Board"** (Sec 2(a)) — Means the Central Board or a State Board. > - **"Central Board"** (Sec 2(b)) — The Central Pollution Control Board constituted under Section 3. > - **"State Board"** (Sec 2(c)) — A State Pollution Control Board constituted under Section 4. > - **"Occupier"** (Sec 2(f)) — The person who has control over the affairs of a factory or premises — **including the owner, agent, or manager**. > - **"Pollution"** (Sec 2(g)) — Such contamination of water or such alteration of the physical, chemical, or biological properties of water (including temperature) as is likely to cause a nuisance or renders the water harmful or injurious to public health or domestic, commercial, industrial, agricultural, or other legitimate uses. > - **"Stream"** (Sec 2(j)) — Includes river, water course, inland water (whether natural or artificial), submarine water, sea, or tidal water. > - **"Trade Effluent"** (Sec 2(k)) — Any liquid, gaseous, or solid substance discharged from any premises used for carrying on any industry, trade, or manufacture. ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Administrative Machinery — Authorities Created The Act establishes a **two-tier administrative structure**: #### 2.1.1 Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) — Section 3 The CPCB is constituted by the **Central Government** under Section 3. Key highlights: > [!IMPORTANT] > **CPCB Composition (Section 3):** > - **Chairman** nominated by the Central Government. > - Maximum of **5 official members** representing Central Government departments (health, environment, industry, etc.). > - Maximum of **5 non-official members** nominated by the Central Government. > - **Member-Secretary** appointed by the Central Government (acts as principal executive officer). **Powers and Functions of CPCB (Sections 16 & 17):** | Function Category | Specific Powers | |-------------------|-----------------| | Coordination | Coordinate activities of State Boards and resolve disputes | | Standards | Lay down standards for water quality parameters | | Training | Organize training programmes for personnel engaged in pollution control | | Research | Sponsor investigations and research in pollution control | | Collection | Collect, compile, and publish technical/statistical data | | Advisory | Advise the Central Government on water pollution matters | | Planning | Plan and execute nationwide programmes for prevention/control | #### 2.1.2 State Pollution Control Boards (SPCB) — Section 4 Each State Government constitutes its own State Board. SPCB composition is similar to CPCB but with state-level officials. SPCBs have jurisdiction over the entire state. > [!NOTE] > **Joint Boards (Section 5):** If two or more states request, the Central Government can constitute a **Joint Pollution Control Board** to exercise powers in lieu of separate State Boards for those states (e.g., previously existed between Karnataka and Andhra Pradesh for the Tungabhadra river region). #### 2.1.3 Functions of State Boards (Section 17) - Plan programmes for prevention, control, and abatement of water pollution. - Advise the State Government on pollution-related matters. - Collect and disseminate information relating to water pollution. - Inspect sewage/trade effluents and review plans/specifications for disposal systems. - Lay down standards for effluents (subject to CPCB guidelines). - Establish effluent treatment plants (jointly with local authorities). - Advise local authorities on matters relating to sanitation and sewage. ### 2.2 The Consent Mechanism — Section 25 & 26 (Operational Core) The **consent mechanism** is the operational backbone of the Water Act. Any industry or local authority **cannot establish or operate any industrial plant without prior consent** of the SPCB. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Consent to Establish (Section 25):** Required **before** setting up an industry or treatment plant. > **Consent to Operate (Section 26):** Required **before** commencing actual discharge/treatment operations. | Aspect | Consent to Establish (Sec 25) | Consent to Operate (Sec 26) | |--------|-------------------------------|------------------------------| | **When Required** | Before construction/setup | Before commencing operations | | **Validity** | As prescribed by State Board | Typically 5 years (renewable) | | **Revocation** | Yes, for non-compliance | Yes, with conditions | | **Application** | With prescribed fees | With prescribed fees | | **Refusal** | Cannot be refused without 30 days' notice and opportunity of being heard | ### 2.3 Enforcement Powers — Sections 20, 22, 23 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Power to Enter & Inspect (Section 23):** Board officers can: > - Enter and inspect any place at **reasonable times** > - Examine **plant, register, documents** > - Take **samples** for analysis > - Apply **prescribed tests** The sample collected is divided into **three parts** — one for the occupier, one for analysis, and one for the Board's record. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula/Cheat Sheet (Quick Revision Table) > [!NOTE] > This is a legislative topic; "formulas" are replaced with **structured key facts and penalty thresholds** for rapid revision. | Section | Provision | Key Detail | Marks Weight | |---------|-----------|------------|--------------| | 2(g) | Definition of Pollution | Includes physical, chemical, biological contamination + temperature alteration | 2-Mark definition | | 3 | CPCB Constitution | Central Government nominated body | 3-Mark short | | 4 | SPCB Constitution | State Government constituted | 3-Mark short | | 16 | CPCB Functions | Advisory, coordination, standards | 14-Mark essay | | 17 | SPCB Functions | Local inspection, sewage treatment | 14-Mark essay | | 20-22 | Emergency Measures | Power to take immediate action on pollution | 7-Mark sub-Q | | 23 | Entry & Inspection | Right of entry, sampling (3-part division) | 7-Mark sub-Q | | 24 | Prohibition on use of stream for disposal | Compulsory treatment before disposal | 7-Mark sub-Q | | 25 | Consent to Establish | Required **before** setting up industry | 14-Mark essay | | 26 | Consent to Operate | Required **before** discharging effluents | 14-Mark essay | | 33 | Water Quality Courts | Adjudication of disputes | 7-Mark sub-Q | | 41-49 | Penalties | Imprisonment up to **6 years** and/or **fine** | 14-Mark essay | | 43 | Penalties for contravention | Up to **1 year 6 months** + fine | 14-Mark essay | | 44 | Penalty for failure to comply | Up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹10,000/day** | 14-Mark essay | ### 2.5 Penalties and Offences (Sections 41–49) > [!WARNING] > **Penalty Table (Frequently Tested):** | Section | Offence | Punishment | |---------|---------|------------| | 41 | Failure to comply with directions of CPCB/SPCB | Imprisonment up to **3 months** or fine up to **₹10,000**, or both | | 43 | Contravention of Section 24 (disposal without treatment) | Imprisonment up to **1 year 6 months**, or fine, or both | | 44 | Failure to comply with orders | Imprisonment up to **3 years**, or fine up to **₹10,000** (continuing offence: extra **₹5,000/day**) | | 45 | Wilful failure to provide information | Imprisonment up to **3 months**, or fine up to **₹1,000**, or both | | 49 | Cognizance of offences | Magistrate with rank of **First Class** or above | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The Water Act is the **statutory backbone** of: - **EIA studies** — most industrial EIA reports must include the consent status under the Water Act as a baseline regulatory compliance check. - **Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP) design** — design parameters (BOD, COD, TSS, pH limits) are dictated by SPCB consent conditions issued under the Act. - **Environmental clearance audits** — compliance audits always begin with verification of consent validity. ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL MATRIX & STRUCTURED PROVISION ANALYSIS Since this is a legislative topic (not mathematical), the "derivations" are replaced with **systematic structural analysis** of key legal provisions, in step-by-step format suitable for KTU 14-mark answers. ### 3.1 Analytical Breakdown of Section 25–26 (Consent Mechanism) — Logical Flow **Step 1 — Identify the Triggering Event** A person desires to establish an industry or a local authority wants to set up a sewage treatment plant. The event triggers the requirement to seek consent under Section 25 (Consent to Establish) **before** any construction begins. **Step 2 — Application Submission** The applicant must submit an application to the SPCB in the prescribed form, along with: - Plans/specifications of the disposal system (Section 25(1)). - Documented pollution control measures proposed. - Prescribed fees. **Step 3 — SPCB Review and Decision** The SPCB reviews the application and may either: - Grant consent unconditionally, - Grant consent with conditions, or - Refuse consent — but only after giving **30 days' notice** and an opportunity of being heard (Section 25(4)). **Step 4 — Conditional Grant (Operational Reality)** In practice, consent is almost always granted **with conditions**, including: - Installation of Effluent Treatment Plants (ETPs) to specified standards. - Monitoring of discharge quality (BOD, COD, TSS, pH). - Submission of compliance reports periodically. **Step 5 — Consent to Operate (Section 26)** After construction and before commencing actual discharge, the occupier applies for **Consent to Operate** under Section 26. This is typically valid for **5 years** and is renewable. **Step 6 — Ongoing Compliance & Renewal** The consent carries continuous obligations: - Periodic self-monitoring. - Maintenance of records. - Submission of compliance reports. - Renewal applications before expiry to avoid lapse. **Step 7 — Enforcement of Conditions** If the occupier violates any consent condition, the SPCB may: - Issue directions under Section 33A (notice period), - Modify the consent conditions, - Revoke consent, leading to closure, - Prosecute under Section 43/44 (penalty provisions). ### 3.2 Analytical Breakdown of Penalties (Sections 41–49) — Escalation Logic The Act follows a **graduated enforcement pyramid**, escalating from administrative to criminal sanctions: $$\text{Layer 1: Board Directions} \xrightarrow{\text{failure}} \text{Layer 2: Notice under Section 33A}$$ $$\xrightarrow{\text{continued failure}} \text{Layer 3: Prosecution under Section 43/44}$$ $$\xrightarrow{\text{conviction}} \text{Layer 4: Imprisonment + Fine}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Layered Penalty Escalation (For KTU Answers):** > 1. **First Instance:** Issue of directions by the Board. > 2. **Continued Non-Compliance:** Prosecution begins; Section 44 applies. > 3. **Continuing Offence (Section 45A):** Additional fine of **₹5,000 per day** for continuing default after first conviction. > 4. **Cognizance:** Offences are **cognizable** but **bailable** and triable by a Magistrate of First Class rank or above. ### 3.3 Analytical Breakdown of SPCB Powers — Section 23 (Entry & Sampling) **Step 1 — Designation** Board officers of rank **Class I or above** (state cadre) are authorized under Section 23. **Step 2 — Entry Conditions** Entry must be at **reasonable times** and into places where: - Industrial activity is carried on. - Sewage/trade effluent is generated or discharged. **Step 3 — Sampling Procedure** Three-part sample collection: - **Part A** — Given to the occupier (for independent analysis). - **Part B** — Sent to Board's laboratory (official record). - **Part C** — Retained by Board's inspector (court exhibit). **Step 4 — Refusal Penalty** If occupier refuses entry or sampling, it becomes a punishable offence under Section 44. ### 3.4 Landmark Amendments — Chronological Impact The Water Act has undergone key amendments that KTU students must recognize: | Year | Amendment | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | **1978** | Amendment Act | Brought Union Territories under Central Board's jurisdiction | | **1988** | Major Amendment | Extended the Act to **all Indian states**; replaced "State Government" with broader applicability | | **2003** | Water Cess Act Amendment | Linked Water Cess Act 1977 with the Water Act for incentivizing treatment | | **2017** | National Green Tribunal (NGT) Amendment | Acknowledged NGT as adjudicating authority for environmental disputes | | **2024 (Proposed)** | Draft Water (Prevention & Control of Pollution) Amendment Bill | Proposes structural reforms to CPCB/SPCB, including online consent and water quality assessment | > [!NOTE] > **Key Concept — Water Cess Act, 1977 (Interlinked Legislation):** > While distinct, the Water Cess Act complements the Water Act by **levying a cess on water consumed** by industries and local authorities. The cess collected funds the SPCBs. Industries that install ETPs and treat effluents receive a **rebate of up to 70%** on the cess — a direct financial incentive built into the regulatory framework. ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Administrative Hierarchy of the Water Act, 1974 ```mermaid graph TD Parliament["PARLIAMENT OF INDIA
Article 252 Basis"] CentralGov["CENTRAL GOVERNMENT

Given to Occupier"] PartB["PART B
