<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define National Environment Policy, 2006. Mention its salient features.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — Structured for Board Valuation):**

The **National Environment Policy (NEP), 2006** is the comprehensive policy framework of the Government of India, approved by the Union Cabinet on **18 May 2006**, that aims at mainstreaming environmental concerns into all developmental activities. It seeks to integrate economic efficiency, social equity and environmental sustainability as the three pillars of sustainable development.

**Salient features include:**
1. **Conservation of critical environmental resources** — forests, wetlands, rivers, aquifers and biodiversity hotspots.
2. **Inter-sectoral integration** of environmental concerns across agriculture, industry, energy and urban planning.
3. **Efficiency in resource use** through the 4R strategy (Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover).
4. **Liability and compensation** mechanism based on the Polluter Pays Principle.
5. **Awareness, capacity building and R&D** through environmental education and institutional strengthening.

The NEP 2006 is **not a legislation** but a policy framework that coordinates the existing statutes — Environment (Protection) Act 1986, Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, Forest Conservation Act 1980, and Wildlife Protection Act 1972 — into a unified sustainable-development vision.

---

**Q2. Explain the Polluter Pays Principle (PPP) as adopted in the National Environment Policy 2006.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — Structured for Board Valuation):**

The **Polluter Pays Principle (PPP)** is one of the foundational principles of NEP 2006, codified into Indian environmental jurisprudence through the landmark judgment in **Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India (1996)**.

**Definition:** PPP requires that the entity responsible for causing pollution must bear the **full cost of abatement, restitution, and compensation** rather than externalising the damage to the State or the community.

**Application under NEP 2006:**
- Internalisation of environmental externalities through pollution taxes, market-based instruments and tradable permits.
- Mandatory environmental restoration funds and insurance for hazardous industries.
- Strict liability — proven in *Indian Council for Enviro-Legal Action v. UoI (1996)* and *Research Foundation v. UoI (2005)*.

**Distinction from Precautionary Principle:**
- PPP is **ex-post** (applied after damage occurs).
- PP is **ex-ante** (preventive action before damage occurs).

The combined application of PPP and PP forms the **dual justice foundation** of Indian environmental governance.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **historical background, objectives and guiding principles** of the National Environment Policy 2006. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **five strategic pillars** of NEP 2006 and explain how they translate into practical action through the existing legal framework. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answers:**

**(a) Historical background, objectives and guiding principles [7 marks]:**

**Historical Background:**
- The Stockholm Conference (1972) catalysed India's environmental policy thinking.
- The Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984) exposed legislative gaps and led to the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**.
- The Rio Earth Summit (1992) and Agenda 21 compelled India to formulate a holistic environmental policy.
- A Draft Policy was circulated in **2000**; after extensive multi-stakeholder consultations, NEP 2006 was finalised.

**Objectives of NEP 2006:**
1. To protect critical ecological systems and natural heritage.
2. To ensure equitable access to environmental goods and services.
3. To mainstream environmental considerations into all developmental sectors.
4. To apply the Polluter Pays Principle and Precautionary Principle in governance.
5. To strengthen regulatory institutions and enforcement machinery.
6. To promote environmental education, awareness, research and capacity building.
7. To encourage public participation in environmental decision-making.

**Guiding Principles (Five):**
1. **Polluter Pays Principle** — cost internalisation by the polluting entity.
2. **Precautionary Principle** — preventive action in the face of scientific uncertainty.
3. **Public Trust Doctrine** — natural resources held by State for public benefit.
4. **Inter-generational Equity** — present use must not compromise future generations.
5. **Subsidiarity Principle** — environmental management at the lowest competent governance level.

**(b) Five Strategic Pillars and Practical Translation [7 marks]:**

**Pillar 1 — Conservation of Critical Environmental Resources:**
- Forest cover maintenance at 33% of geographical area.
- Wetland conservation through the National Wetland Conservation Programme.
- Biodiversity hotspot protection through the National Biodiversity Action Plan (NBA 2008).

**Pillar 2 — Inter-sectoral Integration:**
- Mandatory EIA under the 2006 Notification for 39 categories of projects.
- Strategic Environmental Assessment (SEA) for plans and policies.
- Green clearance single-window through PARIVESH portal.

**Pillar 3 — Efficiency in Resource Use:**
- 4R strategy adoption in MSME clusters.
- Clean Development Mechanism (CDM) under UNFCCC.
- Perform-Achieve-Trade (PAT) scheme for energy-intensive industries.

**Pillar 4 — Liability & Compensation:**
- Environmental Compensation Charges (ECC) by SPCBs.
- National Green Tribunal (NGT) Act 2010 for environmental adjudication.
- Corporate Environmental Responsibility (CER) under Companies Act 2013.

**Pillar 5 — Awareness, Capacity Building & R&D:**
- Environmental Education in school and university curricula.
- NEERI, CPCB, SPCBs as research and training hubs.
- Eco-labeling (Ecomark), GRIHA, IGBC green-building ratings.

**Translation into Existing Legal Framework:**

| Pillar | Operationalised Through |
|--------|------------------------|
| Conservation | Wildlife Protection Act 1972, Forest Conservation Act 1980 |
| Inter-sectoral Integration | EPA 1986, EIA Notification 2006 |
| Efficiency | Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, Energy Conservation Act 2001 |
| Liability | EPA 1986, NGT Act 2010, Public Liability Insurance Act 1991 |
| Awareness | National Green Corps (Eco-clubs), EIA Awareness Programme |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Precautionary Principle (PP)** and the **Public Trust Doctrine (PTD)** as adopted under NEP 2006. Compare and contrast their application in Indian environmental jurisprudence with relevant case laws. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Critically evaluate the **strengths and limitations** of the National Environment Policy 2006. Suggest measures for its effective implementation. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answers:**

**(a) Precautionary Principle and Public Trust Doctrine under NEP 2006 [7 marks]:**

**Precautionary Principle (PP):**
PP mandates that where there is a threat of serious or irreversible damage, **lack of full scientific certainty shall not be used as a reason to postpone cost-effective preventive measures**. Adopted into Indian law in *Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. UoI (1996)* and reinforced in *A.P. Pollution Control Board v. M.V. Nayudu (1999)*. Under NEP 2006, PP manifests as mandatory EIA, public hearings, and risk assessment for hazardous industries.

**Public Trust Doctrine (PTD):**
PTD requires that natural resources (air, water, forests) are held by the State as a **trustee for the public**, and cannot be alienated or transferred to private parties in violation of public interest. Recognized in *M.C. Mehta v. Kamal Nath (1997)*. NEP 2006 explicitly endorses PTD as the basis for forest and wildlife conservation laws.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Precautionary Principle | Public Trust Doctrine |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Trigger | Threat of future damage | Existing resource usage |
| Focus | Prevention | Custodianship |
| Burden of Proof | On proponent | On State |
| Legal Source | Vellore case, 1996 | Kamal Nath case, 1997 |
| Scope | Project-level | Resource-level |
| Relationship with PPP | Complementary (ex-ante) | Complementary (overarching) |

Both principles are reflected in the **National Green Tribunal Act 2010**, which provides an effective enforcement mechanism for environmental rights.

**(b) Strengths, Limitations & Implementation Measures [7 marks]:**

**Strengths:**
1. **Holistic Coverage** — first comprehensive policy addressing air, water, soil, biodiversity, climate, and equity in one document.
2. **Principle-based Foundation** — anchors policy in legally tested principles (PPP, PP, PTD).
3. **Inter-sectoral Coordination** — bridges sectoral silos through EIA and SEA.
4. **Equity Orientation** — explicit commitment to environmental justice and inter-generational equity.
5. **Alignment with International Commitments** — consistent with Rio (1992), Johannesburg (2002), and SDGs.

**Limitations:**
1. **Non-statutory Status** — being a policy document, not a law, it has **no direct enforceable teeth**.
2. **Implementation Deficit** — state-level capacity is uneven; SPCBs are under-staffed.
3. **Conflict with Economic Growth** — growth-centric ministries often dilute environmental priorities.
4. **Time-bound Targets Missing** — unlike the National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC), NEP 2006 lacks quantified, time-bound deliverables.
5. **Limited Public Awareness** — environmental education and participation remain low in practice.

**Implementation Measures:**
1. Enact a **National Environment Act** to give statutory force to NEP 2006.
2. Strengthen **CPCB and SPCBs** with adequate manpower and laboratory infrastructure.
3. Make **public hearings** statutory and mandatory for all Category B projects.
4. Establish an **independent Environment Protection Authority** on the lines of the Australian model.
5. Integrate **environmental accounting** into the national economic budget (Green GDP).
6. Promote **corporate environmental disclosure** through Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) under SEBI guidelines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not write "NEP 2006 is a legislation"** — it is a *policy*, NOT a statute. Students lose 1 mark per error in this conceptual distinction.
> 2. **Do not confuse principles with pillars** — Principles = Polluter Pays, Precautionary, Public Trust Doctrine, Inter-generational Equity, Subsidiarity. Pillars = Conservation, Inter-sectoral, Efficiency, Liability, Awareness. Mixing them is a 3-mark deduction.
> 3. **Always cite the originating case law** when discussing PPP (Vellore, 1996) and PTD (Kamal Nath, 1997). Examiners reward judicial citations.
> 4. **Mention the date of approval (18 May 2006)** and the lead ministry (MoEFCC) for one extra mark in definition questions.
> 5. In **14-mark questions**, structure answers as: Definition → Background → Objectives → Principles → Pillars → Implementation → Case Laws → Conclusion. A non-structured answer is capped at 8–9 marks regardless of content length.
> 6. **Avoid the spelling "Principle"** confusion — write the full form once, then abbreviate. Spelling errors in "Precautionary" or "Polluter Pays" cost 0.5 marks each.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — NEP 2006**

- **Date of Approval:** 18 May 2006 by Union Cabinet; formulated by **MoEFCC**.
- **Status:** Policy document, **NOT a legislation**.
- **Successor to:** National Forest Policy (1988) and Policy Statement on Abatement of Pollution (1992).
- **Vision:** Conservation + Sustainable Development + Environmental Justice.
- **Guiding Principles (5):** Polluter Pays, Precautionary, Public Trust Doctrine, Inter-generational Equity, Subsidiarity.
- **Strategic Pillars (5):** Conservation of Critical Resources, Inter-sectoral Integration, Efficiency in Resource Use, Liability & Compensation, Awareness & R&D.
- **Key Case Laws (must memorize):**
  - *Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. UoI (1996)* — PPP & PP
  - *Indian Council for Enviro-Legal Action v. UoI (1996)* — Oleum industry liability
  - *M.C. Mehta v. Kamal Nath (1997)* — Public Trust Doctrine
  - *Research Foundation for Science v. UoI (2005)* — Vehicular pollution, Delhi
- **Enabling Statutes Recognized:** EPA 1986, Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, Wildlife Act 1972, Forest Act 1980, Biodiversity Act 2002, Public Liability Insurance Act 1991, NGT Act 2010.
- **Institutional Backing:** MoEFCC, CPCB, SPCBs, NBA, WII, NGT, EAC/SEAC.
- **Cross-linkage with EIA 2006:** NEP 2006 and the EIA Notification 2006 were notified within months of each other — they must be studied as a **policy-legislation pair**.
- **Critique:** Non-binding, no time-bound targets, implementation gaps, conflict with growth agenda.
- **Two-minute definition (for Part A):** *"NEP 2006 is the comprehensive policy framework of the Government of India, approved on 18 May 2006, that integrates economic efficiency, social equity and environmental sustainability, and operates through the Polluter Pays Principle, Precautionary Principle, and Public Trust Doctrine to mainstream environmental concerns in all developmental activities."*
- **Mnemonic for Principles — "3 P + I + S":** PPP, PP, PTD, Inter-generational Equity, Subsidiarity.
- **Mnemonic for Pillars — "C-I-E-L-A":** Conservation, Integration, Efficiency, Liability, Awareness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Pollution and Pollutants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Pollution and Pollutants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pollution — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pollution (KTU 2024 Definition):** *Pollution is the introduction of contaminants into the natural environment at a rate greater than the environment's assimilative capacity, causing adverse effects on human health, ecosystem stability, and quality of life, and which is regulated under the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974, the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981, and the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 of India.*

The phrase "rate greater than assimilative capacity" is the **most-tested line** in KTU ESE papers — it shifts pollution from a static state to a dynamic imbalance.

### 1.1.1 The Carrying Capacity Lens

Any receiving medium (airshed, watershed, soil column) can absorb a finite quantum of waste before threshold concentrations are crossed. The threshold is governed by:

$$
C_{\text{ambient}} \;\leq\; C_{\text{standard}}
$$

where $C_{\text{standard}}$ is set by CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board) / KSPCB (Kerala State PCB) for each parameter — **BOD ≤ 30 mg/L** for inland surface water, **PM2.5 ≤ 60 µg/m³** (24-hr average, NAAQS 2009), etc.

---

## 1.2 Pollutant — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pollutant:** *Any substance (solid, liquid, or gas) or physical agent (heat, sound, radiation) introduced into the environment that has the potential to cause pollution or is measured to be present in concentrations above prescribed standards.*

A pollutant is **substance-relative** (intrinsic property of the material) while pollution is **concentration-relative** (state of the environment).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bucket and the Tap

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump for First-Time Readers:**
> Imagine a bucket with a small drain hole at the bottom. You pour water in through a tap (industrial effluent). If the tap drips slowly, the drain keeps up — the bucket never overflows. That is **assimilative capacity not exceeded → no pollution**.
> The moment the tap gushes faster than the drain can release, water spills over the rim → **pollution occurs**.
> The water itself is the **pollutant**; the spill-over is **pollution**.
> Now imagine the bucket is lined with rust — even a slow drip ruins the contents. That is a **degradable / persistent pollutant** difference.

This simple visual anchors every subsequent KTU sub-classification.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in EIA

In **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)**, before any project is appraised, the baseline must categorize *what* could be discharged and *into which environmental compartment*. Without this taxonomy, no **TOR (Terms of Reference)** under the EIA Notification 2006 (amended 2020) can be drafted. This topic is the **gatekeeper module** of the entire course.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — OECHT617):** *Classification forms CO1 (Understand the fundamental components and drivers of environmental pollution). Direct 14-mark questions on classification typology appear almost every December/May cycle.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Pollution — The Four Axes

Pollution is classified along **four independent axes** in the KTU syllabus. Examiners frequently ask students to draw a 2x2 matrix or tree diagram — practice this rigorously.

### Axis 1 — By Environmental Compartment (Most Frequently Tested)

| Compartment | Primary Medium | Typical Pollutant Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Air Pollution | Atmosphere | SO$_2$, NO$_x$, PM, CO, VOCs |
| Water Pollution | Hydrosphere | BOD, COD, Nutrients, Heavy Metals |
| Soil/Land Pollution | Pedosphere | Pesticides, Solid Waste, Leachate |
| Noise Pollution | Acoustic field | dB(A) levels above ambient |
| Thermal Pollution | Hydrosphere/Air | Heated cooling water discharge |
| Radioactive Pollution | All media | Alpha, Beta, Gamma emitters |
| Light Pollution | Atmosphere | Skyglow, Glare |

### Axis 2 — By Origin (Anthropogenic vs Natural)

| Type | Source Nature | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Anthropogenic** | Human activity | Vehicular emissions, sewage, industrial discharge |
| **Natural** | Biogeochemical cycles | Volcanic SO$_2$, pollen, dust storms, forest fire |

### Axis 3 — By Spatial Reach

- **Local** — within a few km radius (e.g., roadside CO)
- **Regional** — transboundary (acid rain, smog)
- **Global** — planetary scale (CO$_2$ → climate change, CFCs → ozone depletion)

### Axis 4 — By Temporal Persistence

- **Transient** — disperses within hours (CO)
- **Episodic** — periodic spikes (photochemical smog in summer)
- **Continuous** — chronic background contamination (lead in urban soils)
- **Cumulative** — bioaccumulative across food chains

---

## 2.2 Classification of Pollutants — The Five-Tier System

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Framework — Memorize This List Verbatim for ESE:**
> (1) By Nature (Quantitative vs Qualitative)
> (2) By Degradability (Biodegradable vs Non-Biodegradable)
> (3) By Form (Primary vs Secondary)
> (4) By Origin (Qualitative vs Quantitative)
> (5) By Resource Recovery Potential (Recoverable vs Non-Recoverable)

### Tier 1 — Quantitative (Quantitative / Qualitative Pollutants)

| Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Quantitative** | Substances that are normally present in the environment; pollution = excess concentration | CO$_2$, Nitrogen, Phosphate |
| **Qualitative** | Substances alien to the natural environment | DDT, Mercury, Lead, Dioxins |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Mnemonic — "Q-A-L-I-T-Y":** **Q**uantity-causers are **natural**, **Q**ualitative (alien) are **synthetic**. This single line appears in **almost every KTU Dec 2022 / May 2023 answer sheet**.

### Tier 2 — Degradability

| Class | Definition | Half-life Order |
|---|---|---|
| **Biodegradable** | Broken down by microbes naturally | Sewage, paper, food waste (days–weeks) |
| **Non-Biodegradable** | Resist microbial attack | Plastics (centuries), DDT (decades), Radionuclides (millennia) |

### Tier 3 — Form (Primary vs Secondary)

- **Primary Pollutant** — emitted directly into the atmosphere/water from a source.
  $$ \text{Examples: SO}_2,\ \text{NO},\ \text{CO},\ \text{Particulates},\ \text{VOCs} $$
- **Secondary Pollutant** — formed in the environment by chemical reaction between primary pollutants or with natural constituents.

The photochemical smog reaction is the canonical example:

$$
\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2
$$

Ozone (the secondary pollutant) is **NOT directly emitted**; it forms in the troposphere.

### Tier 4 — Recoverability

| Class | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Recoverable** | Can be recycled or re-used | Aluminium scrap, paper, glass |
| **Non-recoverable** | Converted to another form, not reusable | DDT, radioactive waste, pesticides |

### Tier 5 — Effect of Pollutants on Living Organisms

- **Toxic Pollutants** — produce biochemically adverse effects (Hg, Pb, Cd, pesticides)
- **Carcinogens** — induce cancer (Asbestos, Benzene, PAHs)
- **Mutagens** — cause genetic mutations (Ionising radiation, nitrous acid)
- **Teratogens** — cause foetal malformations (Thalidomide, Methyl mercury)
- **Allergens** — produce hypersensitivity (Pollen, dust mites)
- **Pathogens** — cause disease (Coliforms, *Salmonella*, *E. coli*)

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Boundary | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ambient vs Standard Concentration | $C_{\text{amb}} \leq C_{\text{std}}$ | µg/m³, mg/L, dB(A) | Regulatory compliance check |
| 2 | Mass Loading Rate | $L = Q \times C$ | kg/day | Effluent discharge calculation |
| 3 | Assimilative Capacity (simplified) | $AC = C_{\text{std}} - C_{\text{amb}}$ | mg/L | Determines allowable discharge |
| 4 | Noise Level Addition (Two Sources) | $L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10})$ | dB(A) | Multiple industrial sources |
| 5 | Noise Inverse-Square Attenuation | $L_2 = L_1 - 20 \log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ | dB(A) | Distance-based mitigation |
| 6 | BOD Dilution Equation | $BOD_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \times f}{P}$ | mg/L | Sewage strength |
| 7 | Stagnation Half-Life Concept | $t_{1/2}$ = fixed per pollutant | days–years | Persistence classification |
| 8 | Ozone Formation (Smog) | $\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}$ then $\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + M \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + M$ | ppb | Secondary pollutant genesis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Formula 4 and 5:** Avoid using vertical pipes inside tables — they break markdown rendering. Use $\log_{10}$ instead of $\vert \log_{10}(x) \vert$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering** — Designing STPs and designing building ventilation requires pollutant classification.
- **Chemical Engineering** — Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP) sizing hinges on biodegradable vs non-biodegradable categorization.
- **Mechanical Engineering** — Engine emission norms (BS-VI, Euro 6) classify pollutants as primary (CO, HC) vs secondary (O$_3$, PAN).
- **Environmental Consulting** — EIA scoping matrix maps every pollutant class against receiving environment for cumulative impact assessment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Matrices

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Noise Pollution Superposition

> **Problem:** Two industrial blowers operate simultaneously. Blower A produces **85 dB(A)** at the receptor, Blower B produces **83 dB(A)**. What is the combined noise level? Does it exceed the CPCB industrial zone limit of **75 dB(A) (day)**?

### Step 1 — Identify Known Quantities

We are given two sound pressure levels:

$$
L_1 = 85\ \text{dB(A)}, \quad L_2 = 83\ \text{dB(A)}
$$

We must compute:

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Normalize Each Level by Dividing by 10

For L$_1$:

$$
L_1 / 10 = 85 / 10 = 8.5
$$

For L$_2$:

$$
L_2 / 10 = 83 / 10 = 8.3
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Exponentials

For L$_1$:

$$
10^{8.5} = 10^{8} \times 10^{0.5} = 10^{8} \times 3.1623 \approx 3.1623 \times 10^{8}
$$

For L$_2$:

$$
10^{8.3} = 10^{8} \times 10^{0.3} = 10^{8} \times 1.9953 \approx 1.9953 \times 10^{8}
$$

### Step 4 — Sum the Sound Intensities

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 3.1623 \times 10^{8} + 1.9953 \times 10^{8} = 5.1576 \times 10^{8}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Logarithm

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(5.1576 \times 10^{8})
$$

Break the log:

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 \left[ \log_{10}(5.1576) + \log_{10}(10^{8}) \right]
$$

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 \left[ 0.7125 + 8 \right]
$$

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 \times 8.7125 = 87.12\ \text{dB(A)}
$$

### Step 6 — Compare Against CPCB Standard

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 87.12\ \text{dB(A)} \gg 75\ \text{dB(A)}\ \text{(Industrial Day Limit)}
$$

**Conclusion:** Violation by **12.12 dB(A)**. Mandatory acoustic enclosure needed. Mitigation barrier must provide attenuation:

$$
\Delta L = 87.12 - 75 = 12.12\ \text{dB(A)}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Distance Attenuation for a Point Source

> **Problem:** A construction crusher emits **92 dB(A)** at **5 m** from the nearest receptor boundary located **40 m** away. What is the receptor noise level? Does it exceed the residential day limit of **55 dB(A)**?

### Step 1 — Recall Inverse-Square Law in dB Form

$$
L(r_2) = L(r_1) - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Known Values

Known values are $L(r_1) = 92$ dB(A), $r_1 = 5$ m, $r_2 = 40$ m.

$$
L(40) = 92 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{40}{5}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Ratio

$$
\dfrac{40}{5} = 8
$$

### Step 4 — Take the Logarithm

$$
\log_{10}(8) = \log_{10}(2^3) = 3 \log_{10}(2) = 3 \times 0.3010 = 0.9031
$$

### Step 5 — Final Subtraction

$$
L(40) = 92 - 20 \times 0.9031 = 92 - 18.06 = 73.94\ \text{dB(A)}
$$

### Step 6 — Compare with Standard

$$
L(40) = 73.94\ \text{dB(A)} \gg 55\ \text{dB(A)}\ \text{(Residential Day)}
$$

**Excess:** $73.94 - 55 = 18.94$ dB(A). EIA mitigation must specify acoustic barrier providing at least 19 dB(A) insertion loss.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pollutant-to-Compartment Mapping

This is the **single most-likely 7-mark sub-question** in the KTU ESE. Construct the matrix mentally before every exam.

| Pollutant Class | Air | Water | Soil | Noise | Thermal | Radioactive |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heavy Metals | Pb in PM | Hg in fish | Cd in paddy | — | — | — |
| Gases | SO$_2$, NO$_x$ | Dissolved CO$_2$ | Methane from landfill | — | — | Radon-222 |
| Nutrients | NH$_3$ gas | Eutrophication | Nitrate leaching | — | — | — |
| Organics | VOCs, PAH | BOD/COD load | Pesticides | — | — | Tritium |
| Particulates | PM10, PM2.5 | Turbidity | Dust fallout | — | — | Fallout dust |
| Microbes | Bioaerosols | Coliforms | Soil pathogens | — | — | — |
| Energy waves | — | — | — | dB(A) | °C rise | Becquerels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Eye:** Notice how the same pollutant (e.g., Mercury) lives in **three compartments** simultaneously. EIA assessors must score this **cumulative multi-media behaviour** when assigning project impact significance.

---

## 3.4 Worked Classification Walk-Through (PAH)

Let us classify **Benzo[a]pyrene (BaP)** — a Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbon emitted from diesel exhaust — using the five-tier system.

**Step 1 — Quantitative vs Qualitative:**
BaP does not occur naturally in the atmosphere at measurable levels. **→ Qualitative (alien pollutant).**

**Step 2 — Biodegradability:**
Microbial oxidation is extremely slow; half-life in soil exceeds **months to years**. **→ Non-biodegradable (persistent).**

**Step 3 — Primary vs Secondary:**
Diesel engines emit **unburnt PAH molecules directly**. **→ Primary pollutant.**

**Step 4 — Recoverability:**
Cannot be economically extracted from soil or air. **→ Non-recoverable.**

**Step 5 — Biological Effect:**
Classified by IARC as **Group 1 Carcinogen** (carcinogenic to humans). **→ Carcinogen.**

**Final Tag:** **Qualitative × Non-biodegradable × Primary × Non-recoverable × Carcinogen.** This 5-D label is what an EIA scoping matrix records for every pollutant of concern.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Environmental Pollution] --> B1[Air Pollution]
    A1 --> B2[Water Pollution]
    A1 --> B3[Soil Pollution]
    A1 --> B4[Noise Pollution]
    A1 --> B5[Thermal Pollution]
    A1 --> B6[Radioactive Pollution]

    B1 --> C1[Gases]
    B1 --> C2[Particulates]
    B1 --> C3[Vapours]
    B2 --> C3W[Organic]
    B2 --> C4W[Inorganic]
    B2 --> C5W[Biological]
    B3 --> C6S[Chemical]
    B3 --> C7S[Biological]
    B3 --> C8S[Physical]
    B4 --> C9N[Industrial]
    B4 --> C10N[Traffic]
    B4 --> C11N[Community]
    B5 --> C12T[Industrial Cooling Water]
    B5 --> C13T[Power Plant Discharge]
    B6 --> C14R[Alpha Emitters]
    B6 --> C15R[Beta Emitters]
    B6 --> C16R[Gamma Emitters]

    D1[Pollutant Classification] --> E1[Quantitative]
    D1 --> E2[Qualitative]
    D1 --> E3[Biodegradable]
    D1 --> E4[Non Biodegradable]
    D1 --> E5[Primary]
    D1 --> E6[Secondary]
    D1 --> E7[Recoverable]
    D1 --> E8[Non Recoverable]
    D1 --> E9[Toxic]
    D1 --> E10[Carcinogen]
    D1 --> E11[Mutagen]
    D1 --> E12[Teratogen]

    style A1 fill:#ff9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D1 fill:#9cf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Source-Pathway-Receptor Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[Source Identification] --> node2[Pollutant Release]
    node2 --> node3[Transport Pathway]
    node3 --> node4[Transformation Stage]
    node4 --> node5[Receptor Exposure]
    node5 --> node6[Impact Manifestation]
    node6 --> node7[Mitigation Intervention]
    node7 --> node8[Monitoring Feedback]

    style node1 fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node4 fill:#fbb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node6 fill:#fb9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node8 fill:#9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Functional Narrative:**
- *node1 → node2* identifies what is being released (using the qualitative/quantitative axis).
- *node3* determines whether the pollutant is local/regional/global.
- *node4* distinguishes primary from secondary pollutant chemistry.
- *node5 → node6* captures the toxicological class (carcinogen/mutagen/teratogen).
- *node7 → node8* closes the loop with CPCB-mandated compliance verification.

## 4.3 Comparative Matrix Flow — Primary vs Secondary Pollutants

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Primary Pollutant] --> P2[Emitted Directly from Source]
    P1 --> P3[Examples SO2 NO CO Particulates VOC]
    P1 --> P4[Time Scale Seconds to Hours]

    S1[Secondary Pollutant] --> S2[Formed in Environment]
    S1 --> S3[Examples Ozone PAN Smog Acid Rain]
    S1 --> S4[Time Scale Minutes to Days]

    style P1 fill:#ffe,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S1 fill:#efe,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Distinguish between quantitative and qualitative pollutants. Give two examples of each.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (Structured, ~3-mark length):**

> **Quantitative Pollutants:** Substances naturally present in the environment but reaching harmful levels due to anthropogenic excess.
> *Examples:* Carbon dioxide (CO$_2$), Nitrates, Phosphates, Suspended solids.
>
> **Qualitative Pollutants:** Substances alien to the natural environment, introduced entirely by human activity.
> *Examples:* DDT, Lead (Pb), Mercury (Hg), Dioxins, PCBs.
>
> **Key Distinction:** Quantitative pollutants are concentration-driven problems in a substance that already belongs to nature; qualitative pollutants are identity-driven problems of substances that never existed naturally before synthesis.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between primary and secondary air pollutants with two examples each.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Primary Pollutant:** Emitted directly into the atmosphere from identifiable sources such as factories, vehicles, or volcanoes.
> *Examples:* Sulphur dioxide (SO$_2$), Carbon monoxide (CO), Particulate Matter (PM), Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs).
>
> **Secondary Pollutant:** Formed in the atmosphere through chemical reactions involving primary pollutants and natural constituents such as sunlight, water vapour, or oxygen.
> *Examples:* Ozone (O$_3$), Peroxyacetyl Nitrate (PAN), Smog, Acid Rain (H$_2$SO$_4$).
>
> **Mechanism Example:** Photochemical ozone formation proceeds via NO$_2$ + sunlight → NO + O, followed by O + O$_2$ + M → O$_3$ + M. Note that ozone itself is never directly emitted; it is a *secondary* derivative of NO$_x$ chemistry.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question with Internal Choice

### **Question A (Choice 1):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**"Classify pollution in detail. Discuss the various bases on which pollution can be classified, with suitable examples for each category."**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

> *"Classify pollution based on the environmental compartment affected. Draw a schematic diagram and provide at least three examples per category."*

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Air Pollution — 1 mark** + 3 examples (SO$_2$, NO$_x$, PM, CO) = **1 mark**
2. **Water Pollution — 1 mark** + 3 examples (BOD, heavy metals, pathogens) = **1 mark**
3. **Soil/Land Pollution — 1 mark** + 3 examples (pesticides, leachates, plastics) = **1 mark**
4. **Noise Pollution — 1 mark** + 3 examples (industrial, traffic, community) = **1 mark**
5. **Thermal Pollution — 0.5 mark** + 1 example (cooling water discharge) = **0.5 mark**
6. **Radioactive Pollution — 0.5 mark** + 1 example (radon, tritium) = **0.5 mark**
7. **Neat schematic diagram with 6 boxes labelled — 1 mark**

**Detailed Written Answer:**

Pollution classified by environmental compartment yields six primary branches. *Air pollution* arises from gaseous and particulate emissions that alter atmospheric chemistry, exemplified by Sulphur dioxide (SO$_2$), Oxides of Nitrogen (NO$_x$), Carbon Monoxide (CO), Suspended Particulate Matter (SPM), and Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs). *Water pollution* stems from the discharge of organic, inorganic, and biological contaminants into surface or groundwater bodies, examples being high BOD effluents, dissolved heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd), and faecal coliforms. *Soil or land pollution* involves contamination of the pedosphere through solid waste dumping, pesticide application, and leachate percolation, with examples including chlorinated pesticides, microplastics, and industrial sludge. *Noise pollution* refers to unwanted sound above prescribed dB(A) thresholds, with principal sources being industrial machinery, vehicular traffic, and community/construction activity. *Thermal pollution* is the degradation of water or air temperature due to the discharge of heated effluents, classically from thermal power plant cooling water. Finally, *radioactive pollution* involves ionizing radiation released from nuclear installations, mining tailings, or natural radon emanation.

A schematic tree diagram should accompany the answer showing six outward branches from a central "Pollution" node.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

> *"Classify pollutants based on their nature and degradability. Discuss with examples how the five-tier pollutant classification guides Environmental Impact Assessment scoping."*

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. Introduction to five-tier framework — **1 mark**
2. Tier 1 — Quantitative vs Qualitative (examples) — **1.5 marks**
3. Tier 2 — Biodegradable vs Non-Biodegradable (examples + half-lives) — **1.5 marks**
4. Tier 3 — Primary vs Secondary (examples + reaction) — **1 mark**
5. Tier 4 — Recoverable vs Non-Recoverable (examples) — **0.5 mark**
6. Tier 5 — Toxicological class (carcinogen/mutagen/teratogen/allergen/pathogen) — **0.5 mark**
7. EIA Scoping linkage — **1 mark**

**Detailed Written Answer:**

The KTU framework classifies pollutants along five independent axes. *Axis 1 (Quantitative vs Qualitative):* Quantitative pollutants are natural substances exceeding their ambient assimilative threshold — examples include excess nitrates from agricultural runoff and phosphate-driven eutrophication. Qualitative pollutants are synthetic or xenobiotic substances alien to nature — examples include DDT, PCBs, and dioxins.

*Axis 2 (Degradability):* Biodegradable pollutants are decomposed by microbial metabolism; sewage organics have half-lives of days to weeks. Non-biodegradable pollutants resist microbial attack; aluminium cans persist for centuries, DDT for decades, and plutonium-239 for tens of millennia.

*Axis 3 (Form):* Primary pollutants are emitted directly into the receiving medium; SO$_2$ and NO from a coal boiler are textbook primary pollutants. Secondary pollutants form by atmospheric chemistry; ozone (O$_3$) and peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN) emerge via photolysis of NO$_2$ in the presence of VOCs and sunlight.

*Axis 4 (Recoverability):* Recoverable pollutants such as aluminium, copper, and paper can be economically recycled. Non-recoverable pollutants like DDT and radioactive isotopes cannot be re-extracted cost-effectively.

*Axis 5 (Biological Effect):* Toxic (Hg, Pb), Carcinogenic (Benzene, Asbestos), Mutagenic (ionising radiation), Teratogenic (Methyl mercury), Allergenic (pollen), and Pathogenic (*E. coli*, *Salmonella*).

**EIA Scoping Linkage:** Under the EIA Notification 2006 (amended 2020), the project proponent must list *every* pollutant of concern across these five axes. The qualitative, non-biodegradable, non-recoverable, carcinogenic class receives the highest impact significance score and triggers mandatory Environmental Clearance conditions.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"Discuss in detail the classification of pollutants. Explain the primary and secondary pollutants with photochemical smog formation as a case study. Also compute the noise pollution for a two-source scenario."**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

> *"Discuss the classification of pollutants based on nature, degradability, form, recoverability, and biological effect."*

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. Introduction — Pollutant vs Pollution distinction — **0.5 mark**
2. Quantitative vs Qualitative — **1.5 marks**
3. Biodegradable vs Non-Biodegradable — **1.5 marks**
4. Primary vs Secondary — **1 mark**
5. Recoverable vs Non-Recoverable — **0.5 mark**
6. Toxicological class — **1 mark**
7. EIA relevance + neat table — **1 mark**

**Detailed Written Answer:**

A pollutant is any substance or physical agent whose presence in the environment causes measurable harm. Pollutants are classified along five axes for EIA and regulatory purposes.

**Nature Axis:** Quantitative pollutants are naturally occurring substances whose concentration exceeds natural assimilative capacity — for example, excess nitrates causing methemoglobinemia in infants. Qualitative pollutants are synthetic or xenobiotic substances entirely foreign to natural cycles — for example, chlorinated pesticides and synthetic polymers.

**Degradability Axis:** Biodegradable pollutants are decomposed by microbial enzymatic action; sewage, paper, and food waste fall in this class. Non-biodegradable pollutants persist in the environment; plastics persist for 500–1000 years, DDT half-life in soil exceeds 15 years, and plutonium-239 remains radiologically active for 240,000 years.

**Form Axis:** Primary pollutants are emitted directly from identifiable sources. Secondary pollutants are formed by environmental reactions involving primary pollutants and natural constituents.

**Recoverability Axis:** Recoverable pollutants can be economically reclaimed for reuse. Non-recoverable pollutants are transformed or dispersed irretrievably.

**Biological Effect Axis:** Toxic pollutants damage biochemical pathways; carcinogens induce neoplasia; mutagens cause genetic alteration; teratogens cause foetal malformation; allergens elicit hypersensitivity; pathogens transmit disease.

**Photochemical Smog Case Study:** Photochemical smog is a classical secondary pollutant phenomenon. The reaction sequence proceeds as follows.

$$
\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O} \quad (\lambda \leq 430\ \text{nm})
$$

$$
\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}
$$

Ozone accumulates as a secondary pollutant. VOCs react with NO to form PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate), another secondary pollutant responsible for eye irritation and crop damage.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

> *"Two compressors operate simultaneously, producing 88 dB(A) and 85 dB(A) at a residential receptor 30 m away. The residential night-time limit is 45 dB(A). Compute the combined noise level and recommend mitigation if needed."*

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. Identify formula — **1 mark**
2. Substitute values — **1 mark**
3. Compute exponentials — **2 marks**
4. Apply log and obtain total — **1 mark**
5. Compare with standard — **0.5 mark**
6. Recommendation with insertion loss — **1.5 marks**

**Detailed Written Answer:**

Step 1 — Use the decibel addition formula:

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right)
$$

Step 2 — Substitute $L_1 = 88$ dB(A) and $L_2 = 85$ dB(A):

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{8.8} + 10^{8.5}\right)
$$

Step 3 — Compute exponentials:

$$
10^{8.8} = 6.3096 \times 10^{8}, \quad 10^{8.5} = 3.1623 \times 10^{8}
$$

Step 4 — Sum and log:

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left((6.3096 + 3.1623) \times 10^{8}\right)
$$

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(9.4719 \times 10^{8}\right)
$$

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 \left[\log_{10}(9.4719) + 8\right] = 10 [0.9766 + 8] = 89.77\ \text{dB(A)}
$$

Step 5 — Compare with the residential night-time standard of 45 dB(A):

$$
\Delta L = 89.77 - 45 = 44.77\ \text{dB(A)}
$$

Step 6 — **Mitigation Recommendation:** Acoustic enclosure around both compressors providing at least **45 dB(A) insertion loss** is mandatory. Additionally, a 4-metre-high barrier wall at the receptor boundary, combined with a green belt of 30-metre depth, can contribute a further 8–10 dB(A) attenuation. Schedule operations to daylight hours only, as residential day-time standard (55 dB(A)) is also violated, requiring operation in soundproofed enclosures irrespective of time.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in this Topic:**
> 1. **Conflating Pollution with Pollutant.** Pollution is a *state*; Pollutant is a *substance*. Examiners deduct **1 mark** if students use these terms interchangeably in 14-mark answers.
> 2. **Omitting the EIA Linkage.** A purely descriptive answer without connecting classification to EIA scoping (TOR, baseline data, impact significance) loses **2–3 marks** in the ESE. Always close with one sentence on EIA relevance.
> 3. **Skipping the Schematic Diagram.** A 14-mark answer without a tree/flow diagram is capped at ~10 marks in most valuation keys. Always include a Mermaid/tree drawing.
> 4. **Listing Examples without Naming the Axis.** Writing "DDT, Mercury, Lead" without stating "Qualitative, Non-biodegradable, Persistent" loses marks. **Tag every example with its classification axis.**
> 5. **Decibel Addition Mistakes.** Students frequently *add* dB values directly. The correct logarithmic sum is non-obvious; valuation key explicitly tests this with 2-mark sub-part.
> 6. **Forgetting $C_{\text{amb}} \leq C_{\text{std}}$.** The pollution definition hinges on the assimilative capacity argument. Omitting this clause in a 7-mark opening part loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read Twice Before ESE:**
>
> - **Pollution = state; Pollutant = substance.** Memorize this distinction.
> - **Six compartments of pollution** — Air, Water, Soil, Noise, Thermal, Radioactive.
> - **Five axes of pollutant classification** — Nature, Degradability, Form, Recoverability, Biological Effect.
> - **Quantitative vs Qualitative** — Quantitative are natural substances in excess; Qualitative are xenobiotics.
> - **Primary vs Secondary** — Primary = directly emitted; Secondary = formed by atmospheric chemistry (Ozone, PAN).
> - **Photochemical smog sequence** — NO$_2$ + $h\nu$ → NO + O; O + O$_2$ → O$_3$.
> - **Biodegradable** has half-life days-weeks; **Non-biodegradable** ranges decades to millennia (DDT, plastics, plutonium).
> - **Carcinogen** = cancer; **Mutagen** = genetic; **Teratogen** = foetal malformation; **Allergen** = hypersensitivity; **Pathogen** = disease.
> - **Decibel addition formula** — $L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10})$ — never add dB values directly.
> - **Inverse-square attenuation** — $L_2 = L_1 - 20 \log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$.
> - **NAAQ standards** to memorize — PM2.5 ≤ 60 µg/m³ (24-hr), PM10 ≤ 100 µg/m³ (24-hr), SO$_2$ ≤ 80 µg/m³, NO$_2$ ≤ 80 µg/m³, Industrial noise ≤ 75 dB(A) day / 70 dB(A) night.
> - **CPCB water criteria** — BOD ≤ 30 mg/L, COD ≤ 250 mg/L (inland surface discharge).
> - **EIA Notification 2006 (amended 2020)** mandates pollutant enumeration across all five axes for scoping.
> - **Schematic diagrams are compulsory** in 14-mark answers — practice drawing the classification tree and the source-pathway-receptor flow.
> - **Mnemonic "Q-A-L-I-T-Y"** — Quantitative/Qualitative natural-synthetic pairing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Pollution: Major Pollutants of Water and Their Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Water Pollution: Major Pollutants of Water and Their Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Water Pollution** is defined under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** of India as such contamination of water, or such alteration of the physical, chemical, or biological properties of water, or such discharge of any sewage, or trade effluent, or any other liquid, gaseous, or solid substance into any water body (whether directly or indirectly) as may, or is likely to, create a nuisance or render such water harmful or injurious to public health, safety, or to aquatic life, or to domestic, commercial, industrial, agricultural, or other legitimate uses.

In environmental engineering terms, water pollution is the **introduction of contaminants into a water body** (river, lake, ocean, aquifer, or groundwater) at a rate that exceeds the natural assimilative capacity of the receiving water, leading to degradation of water quality and ecosystem dysfunction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A pollutant is any substance that alters the natural physical, chemical, or biological character of water. The study of water pollutants is foundational to EIA because nearly every developmental project (industrial, mining, urban infrastructure) carries a water pollution impact pathway that must be predicted, mitigated, and monitored.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **clean fish tank** that contains a balanced ecosystem — fish breathe, plants produce oxygen, bacteria break down fish waste into harmless nitrates. Now imagine someone starts **pouring dish soap, food scraps, paint, and motor oil** into that same tank every day.

What happens?
- The **soap** forms a film on top, blocking oxygen transfer (analogous to surface-active pollutants).
- The **food scraps** cause bacteria to multiply rapidly, consuming dissolved oxygen (analogous to organic pollutants causing high **BOD**).
- The **paint** adds toxic metals like lead and chromium (analogous to heavy metal pollution).
- The **oil** floats and coats the gills of the fish (analogous to oil and grease pollution).

The tank's natural cleaning capacity is overwhelmed, and the fish die. **A river or lake works exactly the same way**, except on a much larger scale. When human activities (sewage, factories, farms, runoff) release contaminants faster than nature can process them, the water becomes "polluted."

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Pollution is not merely the *presence* of a substance in water, but its presence at a **concentration or quantity** that disrupts the ecological balance or renders water unfit for intended use.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Water Pollutants (KTU 2024 Framework)

For the KTU 2024 syllabus, water pollutants are classified into the following **eight major categories**:

1. **Pathogenic (Biological) Pollutants** — bacteria, viruses, protozoa, helminths.
2. **Organic Pollutants** — biodegradable organics, non-biodegradable organics (pesticides, hydrocarbons, plastics).
3. **Inorganic / Heavy Metal Pollutants** — lead (Pb), mercury (Hg), cadmium (Cd), arsenic (As), chromium (Cr), nickel (Ni).
4. **Nutrient Pollutants (Plant Nutrients)** — nitrates (NO₃⁻), phosphates (PO₄³⁻), ammonia (NH₃).
5. **Suspended Solids and Sediments** — silt, clay, organic particulates.
6. **Radioactive Pollutants** — uranium, radium, thorium, cesium-137, strontium-90.
7. **Thermal Pollutants** — heated water discharged from power plants/industries.
8. **Oil and Grease Pollutants** — petroleum hydrocarbons, lubricants, fats.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** Examiners frequently ask students to "List the major categories of water pollutants and give one example from each category." Memorizing the above eight categories with examples guarantees full marks in any short-answer question.

---

### 1.4 Standard Water Quality Metrics (Bolded Constants)

These are the **standard indicators** used by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) and Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS:IS 10500) to classify water:

- **BOD₅ (Biochemical Oxygen Demand, 5-day at 20°C)** — measured in **mg/L**.
- **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand)** — measured in **mg/L**.
- **DO (Dissolved Oxygen)** — saturation value at 20°C and 1 atm ≈ **9.2 mg/L**.
- **TDS (Total Dissolved Solids)** — desirable limit **500 mg/L**, permissible **2000 mg/L**.
- **TSS (Total Suspended Solids)** — desirable limit **≤ 100 mg/L** for surface water.
- **pH** — desirable range **6.5 – 8.5**.
- **Turbidity** — desirable limit **1 NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Unit)**, permissible **5 NTU**.
- **Total Hardness** — desirable **200 mg/L as CaCO₃**, permissible **600 mg/L**.
- **Fluoride (F⁻)** — desirable **1.0 mg/L**, permissible **1.5 mg/L**.
- **Nitrate (NO₃⁻)** — permissible **45 mg/L**.
- **Heavy metals** — Pb ≤ **0.01 mg/L**, Cd ≤ **0.003 mg/L**, Hg ≤ **0.001 mg/L**, As ≤ **0.01 mg/L**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter:** KTU questions often test whether you know the standard permissible limits set by BIS 10500 (drinking water) or CPCB (inland surface water). Memorize at least 6–8 of these values for full marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Pollutants: Sources, Effects, and Standards

The following structured breakdown aligns directly with Module 1 of OECHT617 and the manner in which KTU examiners frame Part A and Part B questions.

---

#### 2.1.1 Pathogenic (Biological) Pollutants

**Sources:** Domestic sewage, hospital wastewater, animal husbandry runoff, septic tank leakage, contaminated surface water.

**Key Organisms:**
- **Bacteria:** *Escherichia coli* (faecal coliform), *Salmonella typhi* (typhoid), *Vibrio cholerae* (cholera).
- **Viruses:** Hepatitis A, Rotavirus, Poliovirus.
- **Protozoa:** *Entamoeba histolytica* (amoebic dysentery), *Giardia lamblia*.
- **Helminths:** Ascaris, hookworm.

**Effects on Humans:**
- Waterborne diseases: typhoid, cholera, dysentery, hepatitis, gastroenteritis.
- Outbreaks of bacterial and viral infections, especially in communities dependent on untreated surface water.

**Effects on Ecosystem:**
- Introduction of non-native microbial species can disrupt native microbial communities.
- Faecal coliforms indicate recent faecal contamination.

**Standard:** CPCB bathing water quality — **Faecal Coliform ≤ 500 MPN/100 mL**; drinking water (after disinfection) — **0 MPN/100 mL**.

---

#### 2.1.2 Organic Pollutants

**Sources:** Domestic sewage, food processing, pulp & paper, textile, petrochemical, and tannery industries; agricultural runoff (pesticides, herbicides).

**Two Sub-Categories:**

**(a) Biodegradable Organics** — measured by BOD and COD.

**(b) Non-Biodegradable / Recalcitrant Organics** — pesticides (DDT, endosulfan), polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs), polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), plastics, microplastics.

**Effects:**
- Biodegradable organics cause **oxygen depletion** in receiving waters (high BOD → low DO → fish kills, anaerobic conditions).
- Non-biodegradable organics **bioaccumulate** (concentrate up the food chain) and cause long-term toxic effects, endocrine disruption, and cancer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bioaccumulation vs Biomagnification:** Bioaccumulation is the buildup of a substance in a single organism over its lifetime. Biomagnification is the increasing concentration of a substance as it moves up trophic levels (e.g., DDT in water 0.003 ppm → zooplankton 0.04 ppm → small fish 0.5 ppm → large fish 2.0 ppm → fish-eating birds 25 ppm).

---

#### 2.1.3 Inorganic / Heavy Metal Pollutants

**Sources:** Mining, electroplating, battery manufacturing, smelters, dyeing and tanning industries, fossil fuel combustion, agricultural fertilizers (cadmium in phosphates).

**Top Six Heavy Metals of Concern (KTU Syllabus Mandate):**

| Metal | Symbol | Common Source | Major Health Effect |
|-------|--------|---------------|---------------------|
| Lead | Pb | Lead-acid batteries, paints, pipes | Neurological damage, especially in children |
| Mercury | Hg | Chloralkali plants, coal combustion, thermometers | Minamata disease, neurological damage |
| Cadmium | Cd | Electroplating, Ni-Cd batteries, Zn smelting | Itai-Itai disease, kidney damage |
| Arsenic | As | Natural geological sources, pesticides | Skin lesions, cancer (Blackfoot disease) |
| Chromium (Cr⁶⁺) | Cr | Tanneries, electroplating, steel | Lung cancer, dermatitis |
| Nickel | Ni | Stainless steel, batteries, coins | Dermatitis, respiratory issues |

**Why Heavy Metals are Particularly Dangerous:**
- They are **non-biodegradable** — they persist in the environment indefinitely.
- They undergo **bioaccumulation and biomagnification** in the food chain.
- They cause damage even at very low concentrations (**µg/L range**).

---

#### 2.1.4 Nutrient Pollutants (Eutrophication)

**Sources:** Agricultural fertilizers (NPK), detergents (phosphates), sewage, animal waste, aquaculture.

**Key Nutrients:** Nitrate (NO₃⁻), Phosphate (PO₄³⁻), Ammonia (NH₃/NH₄⁺).

**Effects — Eutrophication Sequence:**
1. Excess nutrients enter the water body.
2. Algal bloom (rapid algal growth) occurs.
3. Light cannot penetrate below the surface.
4. Submerged aquatic plants die due to lack of photosynthesis.
5. Dead algae and plants are decomposed by aerobic bacteria.
6. Dissolved oxygen is consumed, leading to **hypoxia** (low DO) or **anoxia** (no DO).
7. Fish and other oxygen-dependent organisms die.
8. Anaerobic decomposition produces H₂S, CH₄, NH₃ — foul smell, "dead zone."

> [!NOTE]
> **Classic KTU Example:** The Gulf of Mexico hypoxic "dead zone" (≈ 16,000 km²) is caused by nitrogen and phosphorus runoff from the Mississippi River basin.

---

#### 2.1.5 Suspended Solids and Sediments

**Sources:** Soil erosion from construction sites, mining, deforestation, agricultural plowing, urban runoff, sewage.

**Effects:**
- Increase **turbidity** → reduces light penetration → reduces photosynthesis.
- Settle on stream beds → **smothering** of benthic organisms, fish eggs, and bottom-dwelling invertebrates.
- Carry adsorbed pollutants (pesticides, heavy metals, nutrients).
- Clog fish gills.

**Indicator:** TSS (Total Suspended Solids) measured in mg/L.

---

#### 2.1.6 Radioactive Pollutants

**Sources:** Nuclear power plants, uranium mining, medical and research facilities, atmospheric fallout from nuclear weapons testing, natural radioactive decay of radon in groundwater.

**Key Isotopes:** Uranium-238, Radium-226, Radon-222, Strontium-90, Cesium-137, Iodine-131, Tritium (³H).

**Effects:**
- Genetic mutations, cancers (leukemia, thyroid cancer), birth defects, organ damage.
- Long biological half-lives (e.g., Cs-137 ≈ 30 years, Sr-90 ≈ 28.8 years, Pu-239 ≈ 24,000 years).

---

#### 2.1.7 Thermal Pollution

**Sources:** Cooling water discharged from thermal power plants, nuclear power plants, and various industrial processes.

**Effects:**
- Reduces **DO** capacity of water (warmer water holds less dissolved oxygen).
- Disrupts **aquatic life** — many species are sensitive to even a 2–3 °C temperature rise.
- May cause **thermal shock** and changes in breeding cycles.
- Accelerates metabolic rates of aquatic organisms, increasing oxygen demand.

**Quantitative Effect on DO:**

$$\text{DO saturation at } T = \frac{468}{31.6 + T}$$

where $T$ is in °C. At 20°C, DO saturation ≈ 9.2 mg/L; at 30°C, it falls to ≈ 7.6 mg/L.

---

#### 2.1.8 Oil and Grease Pollutants

**Sources:** Petroleum refineries, oil spills, tanker accidents, bilge water from ships, urban runoff from roads, restaurants and food industries.

**Effects:**
- Forms a film on the water surface, **blocking oxygen transfer** from air.
- Coats fish gills and feathers of waterfowl, leading to death by suffocation and hypothermia.
- Destroys the insulating capacity of fur and feathers.
- Toxic aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene, toluene, xylene) cause long-term health effects.
- Long-term persistence in sediments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Approximately 1 litre of oil can spread to cover about 4 hectares of water surface as a 0.1 mm film** — a frequently asked KTU numerical.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol / Unit | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------------------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\text{BOD}_5 = \text{DO}_{\text{initial}} - \text{DO}_{\text{after 5 days, 20°C}}$ | mg/L | Oxygen consumed by microbes in 5 days |
| 2 | $\text{COD} = \dfrac{8 \times \text{(mL of } 0.025\text{N K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7 \times \text{N)}}{\text{Volume of sample (L)}}$ | mg/L | Oxygen equivalent of total oxidizable matter |
| 3 | $\text{BOD/COD Ratio}$ | dimensionless | Biodegradability indicator; $\geq 0.5$ = easily biodegradable |
| 4 | $\text{DO}_{\text{saturation}} = \dfrac{14.652 - 0.41022T + 0.007991T^2 - 0.00014T^3}{1 + \text{(climatic correction)}}$ | mg/L | Theoretical max DO at temperature $T$ (°C) |
| 5 | $\text{Streeter-Phelps DO sag}: D_t = \dfrac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t}$ | mg/L | Dissolved oxygen deficit at time $t$ downstream |
| 6 | $\text{Dilution factor (DF)} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{stream}} + Q_{\text{waste}}}{Q_{\text{waste}}}$ | dimensionless | Mix of waste and stream |
| 7 | $\text{Bioaccumulation Factor} = \dfrac{C_{\text{organism}}}{C_{\text{environment}}}$ | dimensionless | Tendency to accumulate in tissue |
| 8 | $\text{Hardness as CaCO}_3 = \dfrac{\text{Ca}^{2+}\text{ (mg/L)} \times 50}{20} + \dfrac{\text{Mg}^{2+}\text{ (mg/L)} \times 50}{12.15}$ | mg/L as CaCO₃ | Total hardness calculation |
| 9 | $\text{Stability Index (Langelier)} = \text{pH}_{\text{actual}} - \text{pH}_{\text{saturation}}$ | dimensionless | Water corrosivity / scaling tendency |
| 10 | $\text{Eutrophication Index} = \dfrac{[\text{N}] + [\text{P}]}{\text{Reference threshold}}$ | ratio | Trophic status |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Tip for KTU:** Master formulas **#1, #2, #3, #5, and #6** — these five appear in over 80% of KTU water-pollution numerical questions.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of BOD and the Stoichiometry of Organic Matter Oxidation

The BOD test is **the single most asked parameter** in KTU water pollution questions. Let us derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Conceptual Foundation**

When organic matter is discharged into water, aerobic microorganisms use dissolved oxygen to oxidize the carbonaceous organic material to CO₂ and water. The general stoichiometry for a carbohydrate (representing average organic matter) is:

$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{microbes}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Standard Reference Equation (Glucose as Model Compound)**

Using glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) as the model substrate:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 6\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 6\,\text{CO}_2 + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

This shows that **1 mole of glucose consumes 6 moles of oxygen** — i.e., 180 g of glucose consumes 192 g of O₂.

**Step 3 — Conversion to Mass Basis**

$$\text{Theoretical O}_2 \text{ demand} = \dfrac{192}{180} = 1.067 \text{ g O}_2 / \text{g glucose}$$

In general, for organic matter with empirical formula $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z$:

$$\text{ThOD} = \dfrac{(x + y/4 - z/2) \times 32}{12x + y + 16z} \times \text{conc. (mg/L)}$$

**Step 4 — Definition of BOD₅**

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = \text{DO}_{\text{initial}} - \text{DO}_{\text{after 5 days at 20°C}}}$$

**Step 5 — Why 5 Days and 20°C?**

The 5-day, 20°C BOD test (BOD₅) is used because:
- In standardized sewage, approximately **65–68% of the total carbonaceous BOD** is exerted in 5 days at 20°C.
- This standardization was historically adopted in the UK in 1908 and is used worldwide.
- Ultimate BOD (BOD_L or BOD_u) can be estimated as:

$$\text{BOD}_L = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{0.68}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical:** If a question gives BOD₅ = 200 mg/L and asks for BOD_L (ultimate BOD), use the above conversion to get ≈ 294 mg/L.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical: BOD Calculation with Dilution

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A 5 mL wastewater sample is diluted with 295 mL of dilution water. The initial DO of the diluted sample is 8.0 mg/L. After 5 days at 20°C, the DO is 2.0 mg/L. Calculate the BOD₅ of the wastewater.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write down all given values.**
- Sample volume $V_s = 5$ mL
- Dilution water volume $V_d = 295$ mL
- Total volume $V_t = V_s + V_d = 300$ mL
- Initial DO = 8.0 mg/L
- Final DO (after 5 days) = 2.0 mg/L

**Step 2 — Calculate DO depletion (BOD of diluted sample).**

$$\text{BOD}_5^{\text{diluted}} = 8.0 - 2.0 = 6.0 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Dilution Factor (DF).**

$$\text{DF} = \dfrac{V_s + V_d}{V_s} = \dfrac{300}{5} = 60$$

**Step 4 — Calculate BOD₅ of the original wastewater.**

$$\text{BOD}_5^{\text{wastewater}} = \text{BOD}_5^{\text{diluted}} \times \text{DF}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5^{\text{wastewater}} = 6.0 \times 60 = 360 \text{ mg/L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** $\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = 360 \text{ mg/L}}$ — this represents a **strong domestic sewage**.

---

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation of the Streeter-Phelps DO Sag Equation

This is the cornerstone equation for predicting dissolved oxygen downstream of a wastewater discharge point. It is heavily tested in KTU Module 1 and Module 2.

**Step 1 — Set Up the Two Competing Processes**

In a stream receiving organic waste:
- **Process A (Deoxygenation):** Microbial oxidation of organic matter consumes oxygen at rate $k_1$ (deoxygenation constant, day⁻¹).
- **Process B (Reaeration):** Atmospheric oxygen enters the water at the surface at rate $k_2$ (reaeration constant, day⁻¹).

**Step 2 — Express Deoxygenation Rate**

Rate of deoxygenation depends on the remaining oxidizable organic matter $L_t$:

$$\dfrac{dL_t}{dt} = -k_1 L_t$$

Solving (with $L_0$ = initial BOD at discharge point):

$$\boxed{L_t = L_0 \, e^{-k_1 t}}$$

This shows organic matter decays **exponentially** along the stream.

**Step 3 — Express Reaeration Rate**

Rate of reaeration depends on the **oxygen deficit** $D_t$:

$$\dfrac{dD_t}{dt}\bigg|_{\text{reaeration}} = k_2 D_t$$

where $D_t = \text{DO}_{\text{saturation}} - \text{DO}_t$ is the oxygen deficit.

**Step 4 — Net Rate of Change of Deficit**

The net rate of change of oxygen deficit is the difference between deoxygenation (which increases deficit) and reaeration (which decreases deficit):

$$\dfrac{dD_t}{dt} = k_1 L_t - k_2 D_t$$

**Step 5 — Solve the First-Order Linear ODE**

Substituting $L_t = L_0 e^{-k_1 t}$:

$$\dfrac{dD_t}{dt} + k_2 D_t = k_1 L_0 e^{-k_1 t}$$

The integrating factor is $e^{k_2 t}$. Multiplying both sides:

$$\dfrac{d}{dt}\left(D_t \, e^{k_2 t}\right) = k_1 L_0 e^{(k_2 - k_1) t}$$

Integrating from $0$ to $t$, with $D_t = D_0$ at $t = 0$:

$$D_t \, e^{k_2 t} - D_0 = \dfrac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1} \left(e^{(k_2 - k_1) t} - 1\right)$$

**Step 6 — Final Form**

$$\boxed{D_t = \dfrac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 \, e^{-k_2 t}}$$

This is the **Streeter-Phelps DO Sag Equation**, with $D_t$ being the oxygen deficit at distance downstream (or equivalently at time $t$).

**Step 7 — Critical Point (Maximum Deficit)**

Setting $\dfrac{dD_t}{dt} = 0$ gives the critical time:

$$t_c = \dfrac{1}{k_2 - k_1} \ln\left[\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\left(1 - \dfrac{D_0(k_2 - k_1)}{k_1 L_0}\right)\right]$$

For most practical cases where $D_0$ is small compared to $k_1 L_0 / (k_2 - k_1)$:

$$t_c \approx \dfrac{1}{k_2 - k_1} \ln\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)$$

The corresponding critical deficit is:

$$D_c = \dfrac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} \, e^{-k_1 t_c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Examiners often give $k_1$, $k_2$, $L_0$, and $D_0$ and ask you to find either $D_t$ at a specific time or the critical deficit $D_c$. Practice the above derivation thoroughly.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pollutant–Source–Effect Mapping

The following matrix is the **gold standard answer template** for any KTU question asking "List the major water pollutants, their sources, and their effects."

| # | Pollutant | Primary Sources | Effect on Human Health | Effect on Aquatic Ecosystem | Standard / Limit |
|---|-----------|-----------------|------------------------|------------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Faecal Coliforms & Pathogens** | Sewage, hospital waste, animal farms | Typhoid, cholera, hepatitis, dysentery | Disrupts microbial balance, fish diseases | 0 MPN/100 mL (drinking) |
| 2 | **Biodegradable Organics (BOD)** | Sewage, food processing, paper mills | Indirect (oxygen loss) | Fish kills, anaerobic conditions, foul odour | BOD ≤ 3 mg/L (drinking), ≤ 30 mg/L (CPCB bathing) |
| 3 | **Non-Biodegradable Organics (Pesticides, PCBs, PAHs)** | Agricultural runoff, industrial waste | Cancer, endocrine disruption, neurological damage | Bioaccumulation, eggshell thinning (DDT) | Each compound has specific limits (µg/L) |
| 4 | **Heavy Metals (Pb, Hg, Cd, As, Cr, Ni)** | Mining, electroplating, smelters, batteries | Itai-Itai, Minamata, cancers, neurological damage | Bioaccumulation, bioaccumulation in fish | Pb ≤ 0.01 mg/L, Cd ≤ 0.003 mg/L |
| 5 | **Nutrients (N, P)** | Fertilizers, detergents, sewage | Methemoglobinemia (blue baby syndrome, NO₃⁻) | Eutrophication, algal blooms, dead zones | NO₃⁻ ≤ 45 mg/L |
| 6 | **Suspended Solids / Sediments** | Soil erosion, construction, mining | Carries adsorbed toxicants | Turbidity, smothering of benthic life | TSS ≤ 100 mg/L (surface water) |
| 7 | **Radioactive Substances** | Nuclear plants, mining, weapons fallout | Cancer, genetic mutations, organ damage | Genetic mutations in aquatic organisms | Background ≤ 1 Bq/L (gross alpha) |
| 8 | **Thermal Discharge** | Power plant cooling water | Indirect (ecosystem effects) | Reduces DO, disrupts breeding | ΔT ≤ 7°C above ambient (CPCB) |
| 9 | **Oil & Grease** | Oil spills, refineries, urban runoff | Aromatic hydrocarbons cause cancer, skin irritation | Coats gills/feathers, suffocation, hypothermia | ≤ 10 mg/L (visible film ≤ 0.3 mg/L) |
