- **Sag Region:** DO continues to fall as deoxygenation > reaeration, reaches a **minimum** at the **critical point**.
- **Critical Point (x = x_c, t = t_c):** Lowest DO. If $D_c$ > allowable limit, aquatic life is stressed; fish kills may occur.
- **Recovery Region:** Reaeration dominates; DO asymptotically rises back to $DO_{sat}$.

**Critical Point Formulae:**

$$
t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1} \ln\left[\frac{k_2}{k_1}\left(1 - D_0 \frac{k_2 - k_1}{k_1 L_0}\right)\right]
$$

$$
D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1} e^{-k_1 t_c} \cdot \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1} - 1\right)\ \text{(at critical time)}
$$

**Engineering Use in EIA:**
- Locate the **worst-case impact zone** downstream of an outfall.
- Decide on **discharge location** to avoid intersecting sensitive water users.
- Determine the **required BOD removal efficiency** of the treatment plant.
- Set the **downstream monitoring station** at $x_c$.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- DO = 8.0 mg/L, $DO_{sat} = 10.5$ mg/L
- $L_0 = 15$ mg/L, $k_1 = 0.25$/day, $k_2 = 0.45$/day

**Step (i) — Initial Deficit:**

$$
D_0 = DO_{sat} - DO_{mix} = 10.5 - 8.0 = 2.5\ mg/L
$$

**Step (ii) — Critical Time:**

First, compute the bracketed term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - D_0 \cdot \frac{k_2 - k_1}{k_1 L_0} &= 1 - 2.5 \times \frac{0.45 - 0.25}{0.25 \times 15} \\
&= 1 - 2.5 \times \frac{0.20}{3.75} \\
&= 1 - 2.5 \times 0.05333 \\
&= 1 - 0.1333 \\
&= 0.8667
\end{aligned}
$$

Now:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{k_2}{k_1} \times 0.8667 &= \frac{0.45}{0.25} \times 0.8667 = 1.8 \times 0.8667 = 1.5601 \\
\ln(1.5601) &= 0.4445 \\
t_c &= \frac{0.4445}{0.45 - 0.25} = \frac{0.4445}{0.20} = 2.2225\ days
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii) — Maximum Deficit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_c &= \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1} e^{-k_1 t_c} + D_0 e^{-k_2 t_c} \\
    &= \frac{0.25 \times 15}{0.20} \left(e^{-0.25 \times 2.2225} - e^{-0.45 \times 2.2225}\right) + 2.5 \cdot e^{-0.45 \times 2.2225} \\
    &= 18.75 \times \left(e^{-0.5556} - e^{-1.0001}\right) + 2.5 \times e^{-1.0001} \\
    &= 18.75 \times (0.5736 - 0.3678) + 2.5 \times 0.3678 \\
    &= 18.75 \times 0.2058 + 0.9195 \\
    &= 3.8588 + 0.9195 \\
    &= 4.778\ mg/L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**
- $D_0 = 2.5$ mg/L
- $t_c = 2.22$ days
- $D_c = 4.78$ mg/L
- $DO_{min} = 10.5 - 4.78 = 5.72$ mg/L → Suitable for Class B (Bathing).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing units in hardness:** Many students write Ca²⁺ "as CaCO₃" without converting. The equivalent weight of CaCO₃ (50) MUST be applied. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 2. **Mixing up Acceptable vs Permissible limits:** IS 10500 has TWO columns; examiners expect you to state BOTH and then conclude. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 3. **Streeter–Phelps sign error:** The critical time formula is often written without the $D_0$ correction term, giving a wrong $t_c$. *Loss: 2–3 marks.*
> 4. **Forgetting DO saturation depends on temperature:** $DO_{sat}$ at 20 °C ≈ 9.2 mg/L; at 25 °C ≈ 8.3 mg/L. Quoting a fixed 14.6 mg/L loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Writing DBU classes in wrong order:** Always A → E (highest → lowest quality), not reverse.
> 6. **No units in final answer:** Every numerical answer MUST carry units (mg/L, days, etc.).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Water Quality Standards**

- ☐ **IS 10500:2012** — the primary drinking water standard in India; remember both *Acceptable* and *Permissible* columns.
- ☐ **DBU Classes (A → E)** — order is critical: A (best) → E (worst).
- ☐ **Hardness Formula:** $\text{TH} = 2.5[Ca^{2+}] + 4.12[Mg^{2+}]$ — both inputs in **mg/L**, output in **mg/L as CaCO₃**.
- ☐ **Streeter–Phelps Equation** — derived from $dD/dt = k_1 L_t - k_2 D_t$; gives $D_t$ as function of time/distance.
- ☐ **Critical Time** — the time at which the dissolved oxygen reaches its minimum; use to locate the worst-case downstream impact.
- ☐ **DO Sag Curve** — starts at upstream DO, dips to a minimum at $t_c$, recovers asymptotically to $DO_{sat}$.
- ☐ **BOD₅ vs BOD_L (Ultimate):** $BOD_5 = L_0(1 - e^{-5k_1})$.
- ☐ **TDS = EC / 1.56** (at 25 °C).
- ☐ **SAR** for irrigation water uses $Na^+$, $Ca^{2+}$, $Mg^{2+}$ in meq/L.
- ☐ **CPCB Schedule VI** governs industrial effluent discharge — BOD ≤ 30 mg/L (general).
- ☐ **IS 2296:1982** is the **surface water classification** standard (Class A–E).
- ☐ **WHO 2017 Guidelines** — global benchmark, used for international EIA reports.
- ☐ **Heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Hg, As)** are the **most strictly regulated** — even µg/L concentrations matter.
- ☐ **Fecal Coliform limit for Class B bathing = 500 MPN/100 mL (geomean).**
- ☐ Always **state units** in numerical answers; examiners deduct marks otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Pollution: Primary and Secondary Pollutants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Air Pollution: Primary and Secondary Pollutants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Air Pollution — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Pollution (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> Air pollution is defined as the presence of one or more contaminants (gaseous, particulate, or biological) in the atmosphere in quantities, characteristics, and duration such that they are injurious to human health, animal life, vegetation, property, or which unreasonably interfere with the comfortable enjoyment of life or the conduct of business.

In the context of **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)**, identifying the type of pollutant is the *first critical step* of the baseline study (Screening & Scoping stage). The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), under the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**, regulates ambient air quality across India using the **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)**.

## 1.2 Primary vs. Secondary Pollutants — The Two Families

Pollutants are broadly classified into two families based on **how they enter the atmosphere**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Pollutant:** A pollutant that is **emitted directly** into the atmosphere from a source (natural or anthropogenic) in a harmful form. It does **not require any chemical reaction** to become a pollutant.
>
> **Secondary Pollutant:** A pollutant that is **formed in the atmosphere** by chemical reactions (photochemical, oxidation, or hydrolysis) between primary pollutants and natural atmospheric constituents.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Kitchen & Chemistry Lab" Model

Imagine you are cooking in a kitchen:

- **Primary Pollutants** are like the *raw ingredients* you throw onto the counter directly — say, chopped onions, raw spices, raw smoke from the stove. They are already pollutants the moment they leave the source (the burner).
- **Secondary Pollutants** are like the *finished dish that cooks itself* while sitting on the counter. Sunlight (UV radiation) acts as the chef, mixing raw emissions (NOx, VOCs) with ordinary air ingredients (oxygen, water vapor) to "cook up" new toxic substances like **ozone (O₃)** or **peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN)**.

So: **Primary = Direct emission.** **Secondary = Atmospheric chemistry product.**

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metrics Every KTU Student Must Memorize:**
> - **NAAQS (National Ambient Air Quality Standards)** — India, regulated by CPCB.
> - **WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines, 2021** — International benchmark (PM2.5 ≤ 15 µg/m³ annual mean).
> - **AQI (Air Quality Index)** — A composite numerical indicator (0–500 scale) derived from concentrations of 8 pollutants: **PM2.5, PM10, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, and Pb**.
> - **Units of concentration:** **µg/m³** (micrograms per cubic meter) or **ppm/ppb** (parts per million/billion by volume).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic — The Master Tree

Pollutants are classified using a **two-tier hierarchy**:

### Tier 1 — By Origin in the Atmosphere
1. **Primary Pollutants** (Directly emitted)
2. **Secondary Pollutants** (Formed by atmospheric reactions)

### Tier 2 — By Physical State
1. **Gaseous Pollutants** (e.g., SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃)
2. **Particulate Pollutants** (e.g., PM10, PM2.5, dust, soot)
3. **Hazardous Air Pollutants (HAPs)** (e.g., benzene, asbestos)

## 2.2 Primary Pollutants — Detailed Catalogue

Primary pollutants originate from identifiable point/area/mobile sources:

- **Carbon Monoxide (CO):** Product of incomplete combustion. Vehicular exhaust is the largest contributor. Colorless, odorless — a silent asphyxiant.
- **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂):** Released from combustion of fossil fuels containing sulphur (coal, oil). Smelters and power plants dominate. Major contributor to **acid rain**.
- **Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ):** Formed at high combustion temperatures (thermal NOₓ) from N₂ + O₂ reaction. Vehicle engines and power plants are key sources.
- **Particulate Matter (PM10 / PM2.5):**
  - *PM10* (diameter ≤ 10 µm) — coarse dust, pollen, fly ash.
  - *PM2.5* (diameter ≤ 2.5 µm) — fine soot, smoke — penetrates deep into alveoli.
- **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs):** Like benzene (C₆H₆), toluene, formaldehyde — from paints, solvents, fuel evaporation.
- **Lead (Pb) and Heavy Metals:** From smelters, battery plants, leaded petrol (now phased out in India).
- **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂):** Not a classical "pollutant" but a **greenhouse gas** driving climate change.
- **CFCs (Chlorofluorocarbons):** From refrigerants and aerosols — primary ozone-depleting substances.
- **Ammonia (NH₃):** From agricultural fertilizer application and livestock waste.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — "C-S-N-P-V-L-C-A"**: Carbon Monoxide, SO₂, NOₓ, Particulates, VOCs, Lead, CFCs, Ammonia.

## 2.3 Secondary Pollutants — The Atmospheric Chemistry Products

Secondary pollutants are *not emitted* but **synthesized in situ**. They form via three primary reaction pathways:

### A. Photochemical Reactions (driven by UV sunlight)
- **Ground-level Ozone (O₃):** Formed when NOₓ and VOCs react under sunlight.
  $$NO_2 + h\nu (\lambda < 420\,nm) \rightarrow NO + O$$
  $$O + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M$$
- **Photochemical Smog (Los Angeles-type smog):** A brownish haze containing **O₃, PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate), aldehydes, and acrolein**. PAN is a potent eye irritant and phytotoxin.

### B. Oxidation & Acidification Reactions
- **Sulphuric Acid Mist (H₂SO₄ aerosol):**
  $$2SO_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2SO_3$$
  $$SO_3 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_4$$
- **Nitric Acid Mist (HNO₃ aerosol):**
  $$4NO_2 + 2H_2O + O_2 \rightarrow 4HNO_3$$
- **Acid Rain:** Any precipitation (rain, fog, snow) with **pH < 5.6** (natural unpolluted rain pH ≈ 5.6 due to dissolved CO₂).

### C. Secondary Particulate Formation
- **Secondary Inorganic Aerosols:** Sulphates (SO₄²⁻), Nitrates (NO₃⁻) formed by gas-to-particle conversion.
- **Secondary Organic Aerosols (SOA):** Formed by oxidation of VOCs → low-vapority products → condense into particles.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in EIA Studies

In a real EIA Report (e.g., for a Thermal Power Plant EIA), the consultant:
1. **Identifies primary pollutants** from the Emission Inventory (chimney stack data).
2. **Predicts secondary pollutant formation** using **AERMOD / WRF-Chem dispersion models**.
3. **Assesses impacts** on ambient AQI, agricultural crops (ozone injury), and acid-sensitive ecosystems (lakes, monuments like the Taj Mahal — **Marble Cancer case study**).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is essential for KTU numerical problems in EIA.**

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Description & Units |
|---|---|---|
| Mass Concentration of Pollutant | $C = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | $C$ in $\mu g/m^3$, $m$ in $\mu g$, $V$ in $m^3$ |
| Volume Mixing Ratio (ppm) | $\chi = \dfrac{V_{pollutant}}{V_{air}} \times 10^6$ | Dimensionless ratio expressed in ppm |
| Conversion (ppm → µg/m³) | $C(\mu g/m^3) = \dfrac{\chi \times M \times P}{R \times T}$ | $M$ = molecular weight (g/mol), $P$ in Pa, $T$ in K |
| Ideal Gas Constant | $R = 8.314\, J/(mol \cdot K)$ | Universal gas constant |
| Gaussian Plume Concentration | $C(x,y,z) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | Steady-state plume from elevated source |
| Emission Rate from Source | $Q = m \times f$ | $Q$ in g/s, $m$ = mass flow, $f$ = emission factor |
| Acid Rain Threshold | $pH < 5.6$ | Any precipitation below this is acidic |
| AQI Sub-Index Formula | $I_p = \dfrac{I_{Hi}-I_{Lo}}{C_{Hi}-C_{Lo}} (C_p - C_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$ | CPCB piecewise linear interpolation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Conversion of ppm to µg/m³ (Most Frequently Asked Numerical)

The Ideal Gas Law is the foundation: $PV = nRT$.

### Step 1 — Express ppm as a volume ratio
A mixing ratio of $\chi$ ppm means that in $10^6$ moles of air, there are $\chi$ moles of pollutant.

### Step 2 — Moles of pollutant in volume V
$$n_{pollutant} = \dfrac{\chi \times P \times V}{10^6 \times R \times T}$$

### Step 3 — Convert moles to mass using molecular weight M
$$m_{pollutant} = n_{pollutant} \times M = \dfrac{\chi \times P \times V \times M}{10^6 \times R \times T}$$

### Step 4 — Compute concentration $C = m/V$
$$C = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{\chi \times P \times M}{10^6 \times R \times T}$$

### Step 5 — Final usable form
$$\boxed{C \, (\mu g/m^3) = \dfrac{\chi \, (ppm) \times M \times P}{R \times T \times 10^6} \times 10^6 = \dfrac{\chi \times M \times P}{R \times T}}$$

(Units work out when $P$ is in Pa and $M$ in kg/mol; if $M$ is in g/mol, $C$ is in $\mu g/m^3$ directly.)

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem — SO₂ Concentration Conversion

**Problem:** The NAAQS 24-hour limit for SO₂ is **80 µg/m³**. Express this limit in **ppm** at standard conditions ($T = 298$ K, $P = 101{,}325$ Pa, $M_{SO_2} = 64$ g/mol).

### Step 1 — Invert the derived formula
$$\chi \, (ppm) = \dfrac{C \times R \times T}{M \times P}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute values
$$\chi = \dfrac{80 \times 8.314 \times 298}{64 \times 101{,}325}$$

### Step 3 — Numerator evaluation
$$80 \times 8.314 = 665.12$$
$$665.12 \times 298 = 198{,}205.76$$

### Step 4 — Denominator evaluation
$$64 \times 101{,}325 = 6{,}484{,}800$$

### Step 5 — Final ratio
$$\chi = \dfrac{198{,}205.76}{6{,}484{,}800} = 0.03056\, ppm \approx 30.56\, ppb$$

**Verification:** This matches CPCB standard reference data. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation — Net Ozone Formation from NOₓ + VOC Photochemistry

### Step 1 — Initial photolysis of NO₂
$$NO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow NO + O^{\bullet}$$

### Step 2 — Ozone formation (Chapman-like)
$$O^{\bullet} + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M$$

### Step 3 — Net production of O₃
$$NO_2 + O_2 + h\nu \rightarrow NO + O_3$$

### Step 4 — Steady-state (no VOC): O₃ reaches equilibrium
$$NO + O_3 \rightarrow NO_2 + O_2 \quad \text{(Titration cycle — NO destroys O₃)}$$

### Step 5 — With VOCs present: VOCs react with NO to produce NO₂ (without consuming O₃)
$$VOC + OH^{\bullet} \rightarrow RO_2^{\bullet}$$
$$RO_2^{\bullet} + NO \rightarrow RO^{\bullet} + NO_2$$

This **extra NO₂** is photolyzed to produce **extra O₃** — net O₃ accumulation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pollutant Comparison Table

| Property | Primary Pollutants | Secondary Pollutants |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Emitted directly from a source | Formed by atmospheric reactions |
| Examples | CO, SO₂, NO, PM, VOCs | O₃, PAN, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, SOA |
| Time of Formation | Instantaneous upon emission | Requires residence time (minutes–days) |
| Spatial Scale | Local (near source) | Regional / Transboundary |
| Control Strategy | End-of-pipe treatment (scrubbers, ESP, catalytic converters) | Source control of precursors (NOₓ & VOC reduction) |
| EIA Modeling | Direct emission inventory | Photochemical dispersion models (AERMOD, CALPUFF, WRF-Chem) |
| Health Impact | Acute (e.g., CO binds Hb) | Chronic (e.g., O₃ causes lung inflammation) |
| Environmental Impact | Visibility reduction, direct toxicity | Acid rain, photochemical smog, global warming |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Master Classification of Air Pollutants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Air Pollutants] --> nodeB[Primary Pollutants]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Secondary Pollutants]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[Gaseous Primary]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Particulate Primary]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Hazardous Primary]

    nodeB1 --> nodeB1a[Carbon Monoxide CO]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1b[Sulphur Dioxide SO2]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1c[Nitrogen Oxide NO]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1d[Ammonia NH3]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1e[VOCs Benzene Toluene]

    nodeB2 --> nodeB2a[PM10 Coarse Dust]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB2b[PM2.5 Fine Soot]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB2c[Lead Pb Aerosol]

    nodeB3 --> nodeB3a[Asbestos Fibres]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB3b[Heavy Metals Hg Cd]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[Photochemical]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Acidification]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Secondary PM]

    nodeC1 --> nodeC1a[Ground Ozone O3]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1b[PAN Peroxyacetyl Nitrate]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1c[Photochemical Smog]

    nodeC2 --> nodeC2a[Sulphuric Acid Mist]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2b[Nitric Acid Mist]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2c[Acid Rain pH below 5.6]

    nodeC3 --> nodeC3a[Sulphate Aerosols]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC3b[Nitrate Aerosols]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC3c[Secondary Organic Aerosols]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Flow: From Source to Secondary Pollutant Formation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Stationary Sources Power Plants] -->|Emit NOx SOx| emit1[Primary Pollutants]
    srcB[Mobile Sources Vehicles] -->|Emit CO VOCs| emit1
    srcC[Industrial Sources Smelters] -->|Emit PM Heavy Metals| emit1
    srcD[Natural Sources Volcanoes Dust] -->|Emit SO2 PM| emit1

    emit1 -->|Dispersed in atmosphere| react[Atmospheric Reactions]
    emit1 -->|Direct impact| dir[Immediate Local Impact]

    react --> photo[Photolysis by Sunlight UV]
    react --> oxid[Oxidation by OH and O3]
    react --> hydr[Hydrolysis with H2O]

    photo --> sec1[O3 and PAN Formed]
    oxid --> sec2[H2SO4 and HNO3 Formed]
    hydr --> sec3[Acid Rain pH below 5.6]

    sec1 --> impact1[Regional Smog Crop Damage]
    sec2 --> impact2[Material Corrosion Taj Mahal]
    sec3 --> impact3[Aquatic Ecosystem Damage]

    dir --> impact4[Local Health Effects Respiratory]

    impact1 --> mon[AQI Monitoring Required]
    impact2 --> mon
    impact3 --> mon
    impact4 --> mon
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — EIA Workflow Context for Air Pollution Assessment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[Project Proposal Thermal Plant] --> p2[Screening Stage]
    p2 --> p3[Scoping Stage]
    p3 --> p4[Baseline Air Quality Monitoring]
    p4 --> p5[Identify Primary Pollutants from Emission Inventory]
    p5 --> p6[Model Secondary Pollutant Formation AERMOD]
    p6 --> p7[Predict Ambient Concentrations]
    p7 --> p8[Compare with NAAQS Standards]
    p8 --> p9[Impact Significance Evaluation]
    p9 --> p10[Mitigation Measures ESP SCR Scrubbers]
    p10 --> p11[EIA Report Submission to SEIAA]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q: Differentiate between primary and secondary air pollutants with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Primary Pollutants:** Substances emitted directly into the atmosphere from identifiable sources in a harmful form.
  *Examples:* Carbon Monoxide (CO) from vehicle exhaust; Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂) from coal-fired power plants.
- **Secondary Pollutants:** Substances formed in the atmosphere by chemical reactions between primary pollutants and natural atmospheric constituents.
  *Examples:* Ground-level Ozone (O₃) from photochemical reaction of NOₓ + VOCs; Sulphuric Acid Mist (H₂SO₄) from oxidation of SO₂.

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q: What is photochemical smog? List its two major constituents and one health impact.**

**Model Answer:**
Photochemical smog is a brownish haze formed when NOₓ and VOCs react in the presence of **sunlight (UV radiation)**.
- **Major constituents:** Ground-level Ozone (O₃) and Peroxyacetyl Nitrate (PAN).
- **Health impact:** Causes eye irritation (especially PAN), reduces lung function, and aggravates asthma.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Pattern]**

**Q (a):** Classify air pollutants with a neat flowchart into primary and secondary categories, giving at least four examples of each. Discuss in detail the sources and health effects of **Suspended Particulate Matter (SPM)**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Classification:**

Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1. The classification tree branches into Primary and Secondary.

**Primary Pollutants (at least four examples):**
1. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — from incomplete combustion.
2. **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)** — from coal/oil combustion.
3. **Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ)** — from vehicular exhaust.
4. **Particulate Matter (PM10/PM2.5)** — from dust, smoke.

**Secondary Pollutants (at least four examples):**
1. **Ground-level Ozone (O₃)** — from photolysis of NO₂.
2. **PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate)** — from VOC oxidation.
3. **Sulphuric Acid Mist (H₂SO₄)** — from SO₂ oxidation.
4. **Acid Rain** — combination of H₂SO₄ + HNO₃.

**Sources of SPM:**
- *Natural:* Volcanic dust, pollen, sea salt.
- *Anthropogenic:* Vehicular emissions (especially diesel), thermal power plants, construction dust, cement factories, brick kilns.

**Health Effects:**
- PM10 causes respiratory irritation, bronchitis, and aggravates asthma.
- PM2.5 penetrates deep into alveoli → reduced lung capacity, cardiovascular diseases.
- Fine particulates carry toxic heavy metals (Pb, Cd) adsorbed on their surface → carcinogenic risk.

---

**Q (b):** Explain in detail the formation mechanism of **acid rain**, including the chemical equations involved. Discuss its environmental impacts on soil, water bodies, and monuments. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Formation of Acid Rain:**

**Step 1 — Emission of SO₂ and NOₓ** from power plants and vehicles.

**Step 2 — Oxidation to SO₃:**
$$2SO_2 + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{catalyst}} 2SO_3$$

**Step 3 — Dissolution to form Sulphuric Acid:**
$$SO_3 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_4 \quad \text{(acidic vapour)}$$

**Step 4 — Formation of Nitric Acid:**
$$4NO_2 + 2H_2O + O_2 \rightarrow 4HNO_3$$

**Step 5 — Precipitation as Acid Rain:**
$$H_2SO_4 + H_2O \rightarrow \text{Rain } (pH < 5.6)$$
$$HNO_3 + H_2O \rightarrow \text{Rain } (pH < 5.6)$$

**Environmental Impacts:**

- **Soil:** Leaches essential nutrients (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, K⁺) → soil becomes acidic → reduced crop yield. Releases toxic Al³⁺ ions that damage root systems.
- **Water Bodies:** Acidification of lakes and rivers → pH drops below 4.5 → fish mortality (especially trout and salmon eggs).
- **Monuments:** **"Marble Cancer"** of the Taj Mahal — H₂SO₄ reacts with CaCO₃ in marble:
  $$CaCO_3 + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow CaSO_4 + H_2O + CO_2$$
  CaSO₄ (gypsum) flakes off, ruining the marble surface.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice — Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q (a):** Define AQI (Air Quality Index). Explain the CPCB AQI computation methodology with the breakpoint method. List the 8 pollutants used in AQI calculation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** AQI is a composite numerical index (0–500 scale) that transforms measured ambient pollutant concentrations into a single number indicating overall air quality and associated health risk.

**8 Pollutants in CPCB AQI:**
PM2.5, PM10, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, Pb.

**CPCB AQI Computation — Breakpoint Method:**

For each pollutant, define concentration breakpoints ($C_{Lo}$, $C_{Hi}$) and corresponding AQI breakpoints ($I_{Lo}$, $I_{Hi}$):

$$I_p = \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{C_{Hi} - C_{Lo}} \times (C_p - C_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$$

Where:
- $C_p$ = measured pollutant concentration.
- $I_p$ = sub-index for pollutant $p$.
- Overall AQI = **maximum** of all sub-indices.

**AQI Categories:**
- 0–50: Good
- 51–100: Satisfactory
- 101–200: Moderate
- 201–300: Poor
- 301–400: Very Poor
- 401–500: Severe

---

**Q (b):** Discuss the role of **VOCs and NOₓ** in the formation of photochemical smog. Use balanced equations to show the formation of ozone and PAN. Suggest 4 control measures. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of VOCs and NOₓ:**

Photochemical smog requires three ingredients: **NOₓ + VOCs + Sunlight (UV)**. The smog is dominated by **O₃, PAN, aldehydes, and acrolein**.

**Ozone Formation:**
$$NO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow NO + O^{\bullet}$$
$$O^{\bullet} + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M$$

**PAN Formation:**
$$VOC + OH^{\bullet} \rightarrow RO_2^{\bullet}$$
$$RO_2^{\bullet} + NO \rightarrow NO_2 + RO^{\bullet}$$
$$CH_3CHO + O_2 \rightarrow CH_3COO^{\bullet}$$
$$CH_3COO^{\bullet} + NO_2 \rightarrow CH_3COOONO_2 \text{ (PAN)}$$

**Control Measures:**
1. **Catalytic converters** in vehicles to oxidize CO and unburnt hydrocarbons.
2. **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** for NOₓ reduction in industrial stacks.
3. **Use of unleaded petrol and low-sulphur diesel.**
4. **Promoting public transport, EV adoption, and green belts** around cities.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mistake 1:** Writing "acid rain is any rain below pH 7" — **Wrong!** Natural unpolluted rain has pH ≈ 5.6 due to dissolved CO₂ forming carbonic acid. Acid rain is defined as **pH < 5.6**.
> 2. **Mistake 2:** Listing **CO₂** as a primary pollutant in classical air pollution tables — **Wrong!** CO₂ is a greenhouse gas but not a classical NAAQS criteria pollutant.
> 3. **Mistake 3:** In numerical problems, **forgetting to convert** ppm → µg/m³ before comparing with NAAQS standards.
> 4. **Mistake 4:** Confusing **stratospheric ozone (good — blocks UV)** with **ground-level ozone (bad — pollutant)**. Always specify "tropospheric" or "ground-level" for O₃ in secondary pollutant context.
> 5. **Mistake 5:** Using the variable name `|` (pipe) inside table cells — breaks markdown rendering. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in mathematical expressions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Air Pollution: Primary & Secondary Pollutants**

- ✅ **Primary Pollutants** are **directly emitted**; **Secondary** are **atmospherically formed**.
- ✅ Remember the **"C-S-N-P-V-L-C-A"** mnemonic for primary pollutants (CO, SO₂, NOₓ, PM, VOCs, Lead, CFCs, Ammonia).
- ✅ **Photochemical smog** = **NOₓ + VOCs + Sunlight → O₃ + PAN** (Los Angeles-type smog).
- ✅ **Classical smog** = SO₂ + Smoke + Fog (London-type, reducing smog).
- ✅ **Acid rain threshold** = **pH < 5.6**.
- ✅ **Taj Mahal Marble Cancer** is the textbook case study for sulphuric acid damage.
- ✅ **CPCB AQI** uses **8 pollutants** and a **piecewise linear breakpoint method**.
- ✅ **Master formula:** $C(\mu g/m^3) = \dfrac{\chi(ppm) \times M \times P}{R \times T}$.
- ✅ **NAAQS** is India's regulatory standard; **WHO 2021 Guidelines** are stricter international benchmarks.
- ✅ EIA modeling for secondary pollutants requires **photochemical dispersion models** (AERMOD, CALPUFF, WRF-Chem).
- ✅ **Stratospheric ozone = good; Tropospheric ozone = bad.**
- ✅ **PM2.5** penetrates alveoli → most dangerous particulate metric.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Air Pollutants on Human, Vegetation and Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Impact of Air Pollutants on Human, Vegetation and Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Air Pollutants — Formal KTU Definition

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Environmental Impact Assessment, an **air pollutant** is defined as any substance — solid, liquid, or gaseous — present in the ambient atmosphere in concentrations, durations, and characteristics that are injurious to human health, animal life, vegetation, property, or which unreasonably interferes with the comfortable enjoyment of life or property or the conduct of business.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Any atmospheric substance, energy form, or characteristic that, when present in sufficient concentration and duration, tends to interfere directly or indirectly with human comfort, safety, health, or with the full use and enjoyment of property, is termed an air pollutant."*
> — Adapted from the Indian Clean Air Act framework and KTU EIA syllabus.

The **ambient air** is the outside air to which the general population has access. Pollutants are classified into two master categories:

| Classification | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Pollutants** | Emitted directly from identifiable sources | CO, SO₂, NO₂, PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, VOCs, Lead (Pb) |
| **Secondary Pollutants** | Formed in the atmosphere by chemical reactions among primary pollutants and natural components | O₃ (ozone), PAN, Smog, HNO₃, H₂SO₄ |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Lung of the Earth"

Think of the Earth's atmosphere as the **lungs of the planet**. Just like human lungs continuously inhale clean air and exhale carbon dioxide, Earth's atmosphere cycles gases in a delicate balance. Now imagine someone pouring ink, smoke, and chemicals into these lungs:

- **Visible smoke** (PM, soot) = like mucus blocking air sacs in alveoli
- **Invisible CO** = like carbon monoxide silently grabbing onto hemoglobin, refusing to let go (200× more tightly than oxygen)
- **Acid gases (SO₂, NOₓ)** = like acid rain droplets that burn both leaves and skin
- **Ozone at ground level** = like "good employee in the wrong department" — protective in stratosphere, but a toxic irritant when it stays at ground level

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in EIA:** Every EIA report must quantify how a proposed project (thermal power plant, highway, refinery) will push pollutant concentrations **above** or keep them **below** the National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS). The "impact" is not just concentration — it is **concentration × exposure duration × receptor sensitivity**.

## 1.3 The Receptors — Three Impact Targets

The KTU syllabus explicitly categorizes impact assessment into **three receptor groups**:

1. **Human Beings** — health endpoints (mortality, morbidity, irritation, systemic toxicity)
2. **Vegetation & Crops** — yield loss, foliar damage, ecosystem shifts
3. **Environment (broader)** — materials, visibility, climate, acid deposition, eutrophication

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Always identify the receptor first, then the pollutant, then the pathway, then the endpoint. This **R–P–P–E** (Receptor–Pollutant–Pathway–Endpoint) chain is the universal answer structure for any 7 or 14 mark question in this module.

## 1.4 Classification of Air Pollutants by Chemical Nature

For board examination purposes, pollutants are grouped as follows:

- **Sulphur Compounds:** SO₂, SO₃, H₂S
- **Nitrogen Compounds:** NO, NO₂, NH₃
- **Carbon Compounds:** CO, CO₂, VOCs, formaldehyde
- **Halogen Compounds:** Cl₂, HF, HCl
- **Particulate Matter:** PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, dust, smoke
- **Photochemical Oxidants:** O₃, PAN, H₂O₂
- **Radioactive Pollutants:** Radon, I-131
- **Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs):** Dioxins, PCBs

## 1.5 Indian Legal Context (NAAQS 2009 — CPCB)

The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), Government of India, prescribes **National Ambient Air Quality Standards** for 12 pollutants under the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**. These standards are the legal benchmark for any EIA report.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Reference Values (Must Know):** For a 24-hour average, NAAQS sets PM₂.₅ at **60 µg/m³**, PM₁₀ at **100 µg/m³**, SO₂ at **80 µg/m³**, NO₂ at **80 µg/m³**, CO at **4 mg/m³**, and O₃ (8-hr) at **100 µg/m³**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Conceptual Logic Chain — How Pollutants Cause Impact

Every air quality impact follows a four-step physiological/environmental pathway:

1. **Emission** — pollutant enters atmosphere at rate $E$ (kg/s or g/s)
2. **Dispersion & Transformation** — Gaussian plume, photochemical reactions
3. **Exposure** — receptor (human/animal/plant) contacts pollutant at concentration $C$ for duration $t$
4. **Dose-Response** — biological/chemical response at the receptor

The **dose** is computed as:

$$
D = \int_{0}^{t} C(t)\,dt
$$

Where $D$ is the integrated dose (µg·hr/m³), $C(t)$ is the time-varying concentration, and $t$ is the exposure duration.

## 2.2 Pollutant-Specific Impact Pathways

### 2.2.1 Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)
- **Source:** Fossil fuel combustion (coal, oil), smelters, volcanic
- **Atmospheric lifetime:** 1–5 days
- **Secondary product:** H₂SO₄ (acid rain)
- **Mechanism on humans:** Irritates upper respiratory tract; combines with water in bronchi to form H₂SO₃
- **Mechanism on vegetation:** Enters through stomata → reacts with chlorophyll → bleaching of leaves

### 2.2.2 Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂)
- **Source:** Vehicular exhaust, power plants, lightning
- **Atmospheric lifetime:** 1–4 days
- **Secondary product:** O₃ (via photolysis chain), nitric acid (HNO₃)
- **Mechanism:** Converts hemoglobin to methemoglobin (cannot carry oxygen)

### 2.2.3 Carbon Monoxide (CO)
- **Source:** Incomplete combustion, vehicular exhaust
- **Toxic mechanism:** Binds to hemoglobin forming **carboxyhemoglobin (COHb)**
- **Affinity ratio:** CO binds Hb ~200–250 times more strongly than O₂

### 2.2.4 Particulate Matter (PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅)
- **PM₁₀:** Particles ≤ 10 µm diameter (thoracic, enters bronchi)
- **PM₂.₅:** Fine particles ≤ 2.5 µm (respirable, enters alveoli)
- **PM₀.₁:** Ultrafine, crosses into bloodstream

### 2.2.5 Ozone (O₃) — Ground Level
- Formed by photolysis of NO₂:
$$
NO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow NO + O
$$
$$
O + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M
$$

### 2.2.6 Lead (Pb)
- Neurotoxin, affects CNS; banned in petrol in India since 2000

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula/Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Integrated Dose | $D = \int C(t)\,dt$ | µg·hr/m³ | Used in chronic exposure |
| COHb Equilibrium | $\%\text{COHb} = \dfrac{[CO]}{[Hb]} \times K$ | % | K = affinity constant |
| BEHI (Buffer Effect) | $\text{BE} = \dfrac{S + A + L}{3}$ | score | Soil sensitivity index |
| Mean Concentration | $\bar{C} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i$ | µg/m³ | Arithmetic average |
| Geometric Mean | $C_g = \left(\prod C_i\right)^{1/n}$ | µg/m³ | Used for log-normal data |
| Air Quality Index (Indian AQI) | $I_p = \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}}(C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$ | index | Linear interpolation |
| Stomatal Conductance | $g_s = \dfrac{\text{mol H}_2\text{O}}{m^2\cdot s}$ | mmol/m²/s | Phytotoxicity input |
| Acid Rain pH | $pH = -\log[H^+]$ | unitless | Normal rain pH ≈ 5.6 |
| Visibility (Koschmieder) | $L_v = \dfrac{3.912}{b_{ext}}$ | km | $b_{ext}$ = extinction coeff |
| Global Warming Potential | $GWP_x = \dfrac{\int_0^{TH} F_x(t)\,dt}{\int_0^{TH} F_{CO_2}(t)\,dt}$ | ratio vs CO₂ | TH = time horizon |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** When reporting damage to vegetation in an EIA, the dose must always be expressed as **concentration × hours of exposure (AOT40 concept)** — AOT40 = accumulated ozone exposure above 40 ppb threshold.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In EIA practice for industrial projects, the **impact magnitude** is computed as:

$$
\text{Impact Magnitude} = \text{Predicted Concentration} - \text{Background Concentration}
$$

This value is compared to NAAQS to determine:
- **Magnitude:** Negligible / Minor / Moderate / Major
- **Significance:** based on receptor sensitivity × magnitude × duration

For vegetation specifically, critical levels are defined by UNECE:

| Pollutant | Critical Level (Vegetation) | Averaging Time |
|---|---|---|
| SO₂ | 20 µg/m³ | Annual mean |
| NOₓ | 30 µg/m³ | Annual mean |
| O₃ (AOT40) | 3000 ppb·h | May–July, daylight |
| HF | 0.3 µg/m³ | 24-hour |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) Formation Kinetics

### Step 1 — Establish the Equilibrium Reaction

Hemoglobin (Hb) and Carbon Monoxide (CO) react reversibly:

$$
Hb + CO \rightleftharpoons HbCO
$$

The equilibrium constant is:

$$
K = \dfrac{[HbCO]}{[Hb][CO]}
$$

### Step 2 — Express Fractional Saturation

Let $\phi$ be the fraction of hemoglobin bound to CO:

$$
\phi = \dfrac{[HbCO]}{[Hb]_{total}} = \dfrac{[HbCO]}{[HbCO] + [HbO_2] + [Hb]}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Competitive Binding Principle

Since CO and O₂ compete for the same binding sites:

$$
\dfrac{[HbCO]}{[HbO_2]} = \dfrac{M \cdot [CO]}{[O_2]}
$$

Where $M \approx 210$ (the Haldane coefficient — the affinity ratio).

### Step 4 — Express %COHb in Terms of Atmospheric CO Concentration

$$
\%\text{COHb} = \dfrac{M \cdot [CO]}{M \cdot [CO] + [O_2]} \times 100
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Validation (Worked Example)

Given:
- Ambient CO = 50 ppm = $50 \times 10^{-6}$ atm
- Partial pressure of O₂ = 0.21 atm (sea level)
- $M = 210$

**Substituting:**

$$
\%\text{COHb} = \dfrac{210 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{210 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} + 0.21} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\text{COHb} = \dfrac{0.0105}{0.0105 + 0.21} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.0105}{0.2205} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\text{COHb} \approx 4.76\%
$$

**Clinical Interpretation:**
- 0–5 % COHb: Normal for non-smokers
- 5–10 % : Slight headache in sensitive individuals
- 10–20 % : Headache, fatigue (smoker baseline)
- >50 % : Coma and risk of death

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** This derivation is a high-probability 14-mark question. Memorize the Haldane coefficient **M ≈ 210** and the equilibrium form. The examiner will test both the derivation AND numerical substitution.

## 3.2 Derivation: Ground-Level Ozone (O₃) Photostationary State

### Step 1 — The Leighton Ratio

The three governing reactions are:

$$
NO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow NO + O
$$

$$
O + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M
$$

$$
NO + O_3 \rightarrow NO_2 + O_2
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Steady-State Assumption

At photostationary state, $\dfrac{d[O_3]}{dt} = 0$, yielding the Leighton ratio:

$$
\Phi = \dfrac{[NO_2]}{[O_3][NO]} = \dfrac{k_3}{k_1}
$$

Where:
- $k_1$ is the photolysis rate constant of NO₂ (depends on $J_{NO_2}$, solar flux)
- $k_3$ is the rate constant of NO + O₃ reaction

### Step 3 — Impact Implication

When VOCs are introduced (urban smog scenario), they consume NO without consuming O₃, thereby shifting the equilibrium and **accumulating O₃**:

$$
\text{VOC} + \text{OH} \rightarrow \text{RO}_2 \rightarrow \text{...} \rightarrow \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 \rightarrow O_3
$$

This explains why cities like Delhi and Los Angeles experience chronic ozone exceedances.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pollutant × Receptor × Endpoint

| Pollutant | Human Health Endpoint | Vegetation Endpoint | Environmental Endpoint |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SO₂** | Bronchoconstriction, asthma exacerbation, mortality at > 1000 µg/m³ | Chlorosis, necrosis of broadleaf plants, pine needle damage | Acid rain (pH < 5.6), corrosion of marble (Taj Mahal effect), lake acidification |
| **NO₂** | Respiratory infections, decreased lung function in children | Reduced photosynthetic rate, leaf lesions | Smog formation, eutrophication via nitrate deposition |
| **CO** | Headache at 100 ppm, coma at > 1000 ppm, binds Hb (COHb formation) | Indirect (only via plant respiration inhibition) | Contributes to tropospheric warming indirectly |
| **PM₂.₅** | Cardiovascular mortality, lung cancer (IARC Group 1), asthma | Reduces photosynthesis by blocking stomata | Visibility impairment (haze), soiling of monuments, climate forcing |
| **O₃** | Chest pain, throat irritation, lung inflammation | Foliar injury, crop yield loss (wheat: 5–15 % globally), AOT40 critical levels | Greenhouse gas, damages rubber, textiles |
| **Pb** | CNS damage in children, IQ reduction, anemia | Stunted growth, root uptake | Bioaccumulation in food chain, soil contamination |
| **HF** | Skeletal fluorosis at chronic exposure | Tip burn in gladioli, citrus damage (highly toxic to plants at low concentrations) | Etching of glass, livestock poisoning |

## 3.4 Dose-Response Threshold Values (NAAQS + WHO)

| Pollutant | WHO Guideline | NAAQS India (24-hr) | Plant Critical Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| PM₂.₅ | 15 µg/m³ (annual) | 60 µg/m³ | — |
| PM₁₀ | 45 µg/m³ (annual) | 100 µg/m³ | — |
| SO₂ | 40 µg/m³ (24-hr) | 80 µg/m³ | 20 µg/m³ (annual) |
| NO₂ | 25 µg/m³ (24-hr) | 80 µg/m³ | 30 µg/m³ (annual) |
| O₃ | 100 µg/m³ (8-hr) | 100 µg/m³ (8-hr) | AOT40 = 3000 ppb·h |
| CO | — | 4 mg/m³ (8-hr) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Plant critical levels are **far lower** than human standards for SO₂, NO₂, and O₃. This is why EIA reports must assess **both** human and ecological receptors — a project that "passes" human NAAQS may still damage surrounding vegetation.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical: Acid Rain Calculation

### Problem
Sulphur content in coal = 2.5 % by weight. Plant burns 1000 tonnes/day. Stack height releases SO₂ that eventually forms H₂SO₄. Estimate annual acid deposition in a 10 km × 10 km area, assuming all SO₂ converts and 50 % deposits within the area.

### Step 1 — Mass of Coal Burned Daily
$$
M_{coal} = 1000 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

### Step 2 — Mass of Sulphur in Coal
$$
M_S = 0.025 \times 1000 = 25 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

### Step 3 — Mass of SO₂ Emitted (Stoichiometry: S → SO₂, M_S = 32, M_SO₂ = 64)

$$
M_{SO_2} = 25 \times \dfrac{64}{32} = 50 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to H₂SO₄ (M_H₂SO₄ = 98, M_SO₂ = 64)

$$
M_{H_2SO_4} = 50 \times \dfrac{98}{64} = 76.5625 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

### Step 5 — Annual Deposition (50 % within 10×10 km²)

$$
M_{annual} = 76.5625 \times 365 \times 0.5 = 13{,}973.66 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

### Step 6 — Per Square Kilometre

$$
\text{Deposition rate} = \dfrac{13{,}973.66}{100} = 139.74 \text{ tonnes/km}^2/\text{year}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students forget the stoichiometric conversion between S → SO₂ → H₂SO₄ and directly equate S to acid. Always show the three-step conversion explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Air Pollution Impact Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EMISSION SOURCES] --> B[Atmospheric Dispersion and Transformation]
    B --> C[Transport via Wind, Diffusion, Advection]
    C --> D[EXPOSURE ZONE Concentration C, Duration t]
    D --> E1[Receptor Group 1 Humans]
    D --> E2[Receptor Group 2 Vegetation]
    D --> E3[Receptor Group 3 Environment]
    E1 --> F1[Health Endpoints Respiratory, Cardiac, Neurological]
    E2 --> F2[Ecological Endpoints Yield Loss, Chlorosis, Biodiversity Shift]
    E3 --> F3[Environmental Endpoints Acid Rain, Visibility, Climate, Materials]
    F1 --> G[Impact Significance Evaluation against NAAQS and WHO]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H{Threshold Exceeded?}
    H --> I[YES Mitigation Required]
    H --> J[NO Project Approved with Monitoring]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pollutant × Receptor × Effect Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[SO2] --> R1H[Humans Bronchitis]
    P1 --> R1V[Vegetation Chlorosis]
    P1 --> R1E[Acid Rain Taj Marble Damage]
    P2[NO2] --> R2H[Humans Lung Infection]
    P2 --> R2V[Vegetation Reduced Photosynthesis]
    P2 --> R2E[Ozone Smog Formation]
    P3[CO] --> R3H[Humans COHb Binding]
    P3 --> R3V[Vegetation Indirect]
    P3 --> R3E[Climate Trace Effect]
    P4[PM25] --> R4H[Humans Cardiopulmonary Mortality]
    P4 --> R4V[Vegetation Stomatal Blockage]
    P4 --> R4E[Visibility Reduction Soiling]
    P5[O3] --> R5H[Humans Chest Pain]
    P5 --> R5V[Vegetation Foliar Necrosis]
    P5 --> R5E[Material Cracking Rubber]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — EIA Decision Logic for Air Quality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Proposed Project] --> Q1[Identify Emission Sources]
    Q1 --> Q2[Estimate Emission Inventory E in tonnes per year]
    Q2 --> Q3[Apply Dispersion Model Gaussian Plume]
    Q3 --> Q4[Predict Ground Level Concentration at Receptors]
    Q4 --> Q5[Compare with NAAQS and WHO]
    Q5 --> Q6{Exceeds Standard?}
    Q6 --> Q7[NO Acceptable Proceed]
    Q6 --> Q8[YES Evaluate Sensitive Receptors]
    Q8 --> Q9[Quantify Dose Response for Humans and Vegetation]
    Q9 --> Q10{Impact Significant?}
    Q10 --> Q11[NO Add Emission Controls]
    Q10 --> Q12[YES Design Mitigation Bag Filter, ESP, Scrubbers]
    Q12 --> Q13[Reassess Residual Impact]
    Q13 --> Q14[Decision Project Approved Rejected or Modified]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Human Respiratory System and PM Deposition

| Region | Diameter Threshold | Particles Trapped | Health Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nasopharynx | > 10 µm | PM₁₀⁺ | Sneezing, irritation |
| Trachea/Bronchi | 2.5–10 µm | PM₂.₅–PM₁₀ | Bronchitis, asthma |
| Alveoli | < 2.5 µm | PM₂.₅ | COPD, lung cancer |
| Bloodstream | < 0.1 µm | Ultrafine | Cardiovascular mortality |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** This size-dependent deposition is the reason why regulators shifted focus from TSP (Total Suspended Particulates) to **PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅** — the smaller the particle, the deeper it penetrates, and the greater the mortality correlation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Differentiate between primary and secondary air pollutants. Give two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Primary Pollutants:** Substances directly emitted into the atmosphere from identifiable sources in a form harmful to receptors.

Examples:
1. **Sulphur dioxide (SO₂)** — emitted from coal-fired power plants
2. **Carbon monoxide (CO)** — emitted from vehicular exhausts

**Secondary Pollutants:** Substances formed in the atmosphere through chemical reactions between primary pollutants and natural atmospheric constituents.

Examples:
1. **Ground-level ozone (O₃)** — formed by photolysis of NO₂ in presence of VOCs
2. **Sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄)** — formed by oxidation of SO₂ to SO₃ and reaction with water

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation Pattern (3 marks):** Definition: 1 mark, Primary example with source: 1 mark, Secondary example with formation pathway: 1 mark.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Explain the mechanism of CO toxicity in humans with reference to carboxyhemoglobin.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Carbon monoxide (CO) binds reversibly to the iron (Fe²⁺) centre of hemoglobin (Hb) to form **carboxyhemoglobin (COHb)**. The affinity of CO for Hb is approximately **200–250 times** greater than that of oxygen (O₂). This causes:

1. **Competitive displacement** of O₂ from Hb, reducing blood oxygen-carrying capacity
2. **Left-shift of the oxygen dissociation curve**, impairing O₂ release to tissues
3. **Tissue hypoxia** even at normal partial pressures of O₂

Symptoms scale with %COHb: 5 % causes slight headache, 20 % causes severe headache and confusion, >50 % is potentially fatal.

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Discuss in detail the impacts of air pollutants on human health. Explain with reference to SO₂, NO₂, CO, and PM₂.₅, citing WHO and NAAQS standards.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Human Health Impacts of SO₂ and NO₂

**Model Answer:**

**Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂):**
- A water-soluble, irritant gas primarily affecting the upper respiratory tract
- On inhalation, SO₂ dissolves in the moisture of nasal and bronchial mucosa, forming **sulphurous acid (H₂SO₃)** and **sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄)**
- Causes **bronchoconstriction**, especially in asthmatics, even at low concentrations (~400 µg/m³)
- At chronic exposure: aggravates chronic bronchitis, reduces lung function (FEV₁ decline), increases hospitalization rates
- **NAAQS (24-hr):** 80 µg/m³ ; **WHO 24-hr:** 40 µg/m³
- **Sensitive groups:** Children, elderly, COPD patients, outdoor workers

**Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂):**
- A reddish-brown gas, less water-soluble but more oxidative than SO₂
- Reaches deeper into the lungs — affects the **alveolar region**
- Causes **oxidative stress** via formation of reactive nitrogen species (NO₃⁻, peroxynitrite)
- Converts oxyhemoglobin to **methemoglobin** (cannot bind O₂)
- Increases susceptibility to respiratory infections (especially in children: reduced NK-cell activity)
- **NAAQS (24-hr):** 80 µg/m³ ; **WHO 24-hr:** 25 µg/m³
- Major secondary source: tropospheric ozone formation

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Health Impacts of CO and PM₂.₅

**Carbon Monoxide (CO):**
- Colorless, odorless — undetectable without instrumentation
- Forms **carboxyhemoglobin (COHb)**; affinity ~210× that of O₂
- Equation: 
$$
HbO_2 + CO \rightarrow HbCO + O_2
$$
- At 50 ppm ambient: %COHb ≈ 4.8 % (mild symptoms)
- At 100 ppm: %COHb ≈ 9 % (headache, fatigue)
- Effects on **fetal hemoglobin** are even more severe (fetal Hb binds CO with even higher affinity)
- NAAQS (8-hr): 4 mg/m³

**Particulate Matter (PM₂.₅):**
- Fine particles (< 2.5 µm) penetrate deep into alveoli
- **IARC Classified as Group 1 carcinogen** (lung cancer)
- Triggers systemic inflammation, oxidative stress
- Crosses into **bloodstream** (PM₀.₁ ultrafine fraction), causing:
  - Atherosclerosis
  - Myocardial infarction
  - Stroke
- WHO estimate: ~4.2 million premature deaths/year globally from ambient PM
- NAAQS (24-hr): 60 µg/m³ ; **WHO 24-hr:** 15 µg/m³

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Writing "CO is poisonous" without specifying the **biochemical mechanism (COHb formation)** → loses 2 marks
> 2. Citing NAAQS values without mentioning **WHO guidelines** → loses 1 mark
> 3. Omitting mention of **sensitive sub-populations** (children, asthmatics, elderly) → loses 1 mark
> 4. Not distinguishing PM₁₀ vs PM₂.₅ health effects → loses 1 mark

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Explain the impacts of air pollutants on vegetation and the broader environment. Discuss acid rain, photochemical smog, and ozone damage to crops.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Impacts on Vegetation

**Model Answer:**

Vegetation damage occurs through three pathways:
1. **Stomatal uptake** — gases diffuse through leaf stomata
2. **Foliar deposition** — particulates settle on leaf surface
3. **Soil-mediated uptake** — root absorption of acidified species

**Pollutant-specific vegetation effects:**

| Pollutant | Vegetation Effect | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| SO₂ | Chlorosis, necrosis | Disrupts chlorophyll synthesis, acidifies cell sap |
| NO₂ | Reduced photosynthesis | Replaces CO₂ in Calvin cycle reactions |
| O₃ | Fleck, stipple, bronzing | Oxidizes cell membrane lipids |
| HF | Tip burn, leaf margin necrosis | Accumulates at leaf margins via transpiration |
| PAN (peroxyacetyl nitrate) | Silvering, bronzing of lower leaf surface | Damages chloroplasts |
| Acid rain | Leaching of Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ from foliage | Nutrient depletion, soil acidification |

**Yield Loss Estimates (Global):**
- Wheat: 5–15 % yield reduction due to O₃ (AOT40-based)
- Soybean: 10–20 % yield loss in high-O₃ zones
- Rice: 6–12 % yield reduction
- Total economic loss to global agriculture from tropospheric O₃: ~USD 11–26 billion/year (UNECE estimates)

**Critical Levels for Vegetation (UNECE):**
- SO₂: 20 µg/m³ (annual mean)
- NOₓ: 30 µg/m³ (annual mean)
- O₃ AOT40: 3000 ppb·h (May–July daylight hours)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Broader Environmental Impacts — Acid Rain, Smog, Climate

**Acid Rain:**
- Normal unpolluted rain has pH ≈ 5.6 (dissolved CO₂ → carbonic acid)
- Acid rain pH < 5.6
- Formation pathway: SO₂ → SO₃ → H₂SO₄ ; NOₓ → HNO₃
- **Impacts:**
  - Lake acidification: pH drops to < 5, kills fish (especially trout)
  - Soil acidification: leaches Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, K⁺ → nutrient deficiency in forests
  - **Monument damage:** corrosion of marble (CaCO₃) and limestone
  - Famous example: **Taj Mahal** marble turning yellow due to SO₂ + particulate soiling (Mathura refinery case)

**Photochemical Smog:**
- Formed in summer by reaction of NOₓ + VOCs under sunlight
- Components: O₃ (90 %), PAN, formaldehyde, acrolein
- Reduces visibility, irritates eyes, damages crops
- Classic example: **Los Angeles smog**; in India — Delhi winter smog (now also primary PM-driven)

**Global Climate Effects:**
- CO₂: largest contributor to radiative forcing (≈ 1.68 W/m²)
- CH₄: GWP = 28 (over 100 years)
- Black carbon (PM): positive forcing (~ +1.1 W/m²)
- Tropospheric O₃: short-lived climate forcer

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. Writing "acid rain damages environment" without giving **specific pH thresholds** → loses 2 marks
> 2. Not mentioning the **Taj Mahal case study** (highly valued by Kerala examiners as local context) → loses 1 mark
> 3. Confusing stratospheric ozone (protective) with tropospheric ozone (pollutant) → loses 2 marks
> 4. Failing to give **numerical yield loss data** for crops → loses 1 mark

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize These 15 High-Yield Points Before the Exam:**

1. **Haldane Coefficient** $M \approx 210$: CO binds Hb 210× more tightly than O₂
2. **NAAQS 24-hour:** SO₂ = 80 µg/m³, NO₂ = 80 µg/m³, PM₁₀ = 100 µg/m³, PM₂.₅ = 60 µg/m³
3. **PM Size Hierarchy:** PM₁₀ (bronchi) > PM₂.₅ (alveoli) > PM₀.₁ (bloodstream)
4. **Photochemical smog** requires NOₓ + VOCs + sunlight; major component is O₃
5. **Taj Mahal** is a classic KTU case study for SO₂-induced marble corrosion
6. **Plant critical levels** are LOWER than human NAAQS — EIA must assess both
7. **Acid rain** pH < 5.6; formed by H₂SO₄ and HNO₃
8. **%COHb** clinical threshold: 5 % = headache, 20 % = confusion, >50 % = fatal
9. **O₃ in troposphere = pollutant**; O₃ in stratosphere = protective
10. **PAN** damages lower leaf surface of vegetation (silvering, bronzing)
11. **AOT40** = Accumulated exposure Over Threshold of 40 ppb O₃ — used for vegetation impact
12. **HF** is the most phytotoxic gas per unit mass — tip-burn damage
13. **IARC** classifies PM₂.₅ as **Group 1 carcinogen** (lung cancer)
14. **Methemoglobinemia** is caused by NO₂ binding; reduces O₂ carrying capacity
15. **Lead** (Pb) is a neurotoxin; affects IQ in children; banned in Indian petrol since 2000

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy (14-mark questions):**
> Always structure your answer using the **R–P–P–E framework**:
> - **Receptor** (who is affected?)
> - **Pollutant** (what substance?)
> - **Pathway** (how does it reach?)
> - **Endpoint** (what is the damage?)
> Add a concluding line comparing **NAAQS / WHO standards** to demonstrate board-level compliance awareness. This structure alone will secure 80 %+ marks even with partial recall.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ambient Air Quality Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ambient Air Quality Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ambient Air Quality Standards (AAQS)** are legally enforceable, prescribed limits on the concentration of specific air pollutants (termed *criteria pollutants*) in the outdoor (ambient) atmosphere, established by regulatory authorities to protect public health, vegetation, ecosystems, and materials from the adverse effects of air pollution.

In the Indian regulatory framework, the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** under the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981** notifies the **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)**. Internationally, benchmark standards are provided by the **World Health Organization (WHO)** and the **United States Environmental Protection Agency (US-EPA)**.

| Parameter | Standard Reference Body | Governing Framework |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| NAAQS (India) | CPCB / MoEFCC | Air Act 1981, EPA 1986 |
| Global Air Quality Guidelines | WHO | WHO 2021 Update |
| NAAQS (USA) | US-EPA | Clean Air Act 1970 |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the atmosphere as a giant, invisible **soup** in which we all breathe. Just as the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) sets a maximum limit on how much salt or sugar a packaged food can contain (e.g., $\le 5 \text{ g sugar per } 100 \text{ g}$), AAQS sets a maximum allowable concentration of pollutants in the air we inhale (e.g., $\le 60 \text{ }\mu g/m^3$ of $PM_{2.5}$ per 24 hours).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy**: Think of AAQS as the "**legal speed limit**" for pollutants. Cars (industries/vehicles) release exhaust (pollutants), and just as a speed limit ($60 \text{ km/h}$) prevents accidents on a highway, AAQS prevents "pollution accidents" in the lungs of the population.

## 1.3 Why AAQS Exist — The Need

1. **Health Protection**: Fine particulate matter ($PM_{2.5}$) penetrates deep into alveoli, causing asthma, bronchitis, and cardiovascular diseases.
2. **Vegetation & Ecosystem Safety**: $SO_2$ and $O_3$ cause chlorosis and crop yield loss.
3. **Material Damage**: Acidic pollutants ($SO_2$, $NO_x$) corrode metals, marble (Taj Mahal effect), and damage painted surfaces.
4. **Visibility**: Particulates cause haze, reducing scenic and aviation visibility.
5. **Legal Basis**: Required to prosecute violators and mandate mitigative actions in EIAs.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Key Takeaway**: AAQS is the *baseline benchmark* used in EIA studies. Every EIA report **must** compare predicted post-project pollutant concentrations against these standards.

## 1.4 Classification of Areas for AAQS (Indian Context)

The CPCB applies a tiered system based on land-use:

- **Industrial Area**: Slightly relaxed limits (proximity to point sources).
- **Residential, Rural & Other Areas**: Stricter limits (high population exposure).
- **Sensitive Area** (hospitals, schools, biodiversity zones, heritage sites): **Strictest** limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensitive Areas** include eco-fragile zones such as the Taj Trapezium, sanctuaries, and national parks — where any exceedance triggers mandatory mitigation under EIA Notification, 2006.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Criteria Pollutants (Conceptual Hierarchy)

The NAAQS framework targets **12 criteria pollutants** under Indian law (CPCB, 2009) and **6 classical criteria pollutants** under US-EPA:

### 2.1.1 The Classical Six (US-EPA "Big Six")

1. **Particulate Matter** ($PM_{10}$, $PM_{2.5}$)
2. **Sulphur Dioxide** ($SO_2$)
3. **Nitrogen Dioxide** ($NO_2$)
4. **Carbon Monoxide** ($CO$)
5. **Ozone** ($O_3$ — ground-level tropospheric)
6. **Lead** ($Pb$)

### 2.1.2 Additional Indian Criteria Pollutants

7. **Ammonia** ($NH_3$)
8. **Benzene** ($C_6H_6$)
9. **Benzo(a)Pyrene** (BaP — PAH marker)
10. **Arsenic** ($As$)
11. **Nickel** ($Ni$)
12. **Carbon Disulfide** ($CS_2$ — in some industrial zones)

## 2.2 Units of Measurement — Critical for KTU

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite**: Questions on unit conversion between **ppm** and **$\mu g/m^3$** are extremely common (often a 7-mark sub-part).

- **ppm** = parts per million = $1$ part in $10^6$ parts of air (volume basis).
- **$\mu g/m^3$** = micrograms per cubic meter of air (mass basis).
- **$mg/m^3$** = milligrams per cubic meter.

| Pollutant Type | Typical Unit | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Gases ($SO_2$, $NO_2$, $CO$) | $\mu g/m^3$ (Indian) or ppm (US) | Both used |
| Particulates ($PM$) | $\mu g/m^3$ only | Mass-based |
| Heavy metals ($Pb$, $As$) | $\mu g/m^3$ or $ng/m^3$ | Trace concentration |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the table below as your single-source revision for numerical problems.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Constants |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | ppm to $\mu g/m^3$ at 25 °C, 1 atm | $C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW}{24.45} \times 10^3$ | $MW$ = molecular weight |
| 2 | ppm to $mg/m^3$ at 25 °C, 1 atm | $C_{mg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW}{24.45}$ | $MW$ = molecular weight |
| 3 | Molar volume at NTP (0 °C, 1 atm) | $V_m = 22.4 \text{ L/mol}$ | Ideal gas |
| 4 | Molar volume at STP (25 °C, 1 atm) | $V_m = 24.45 \text{ L/mol}$ | Used in NAAQS conversions |
| 5 | Isothermal conversion | $\dfrac{C_1}{C_2} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$ (at constant P) | Corrects for temperature |
| 6 | Pressure correction | $C \propto P$ (at constant T) | Corrects for pressure |
| 7 | AQI (Indian CPCB sub-index) | $I_p = \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \times (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$ | Piecewise linear interpolation |
| 8 | Air Quality Index (US-EPA) | Same form, but breakpoints differ | $0 \text{ to } 500$ scale |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipeline Caveat**: When using the conversion formula, **NEVER** confuse the gas molar volume ($22.4$ at STP / $24.45$ at 25 °C) with the molecular weight of the pollutant. These are independent parameters.

## 2.4 Indian NAAQS — CPCB 2009 Standards (Comprehensive Table)

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Mnemonic**: "*Sunrise Over Noisy Clouds, Often Pleasing Bright As Nice*" → $SO_2, O_3, NO_2, CO, O_3, Pb, BaP, As, Ni$ — practice this for KTU exams.

| Pollutant | Time-Weighted Avg | Industrial | Residential / Rural / Other | Sensitive | Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $SO_2$ | Annual | $50$ | $50$ | $20$ | Improved West-Gaeke |
| $SO_2$ | 24 hr | $80$ | $80$ | $30$ | Improved West-Gaeke |
| $NO_2$ | Annual | $40$ | $40$ | $30$ | Jacob & Hochheiser |
| $NO_2$ | 24 hr | $80$ | $80$ | $30$ | Jacob & Hochheiser |
| $PM_{10}$ | Annual | $60$ | $60$ | $60$ | Gravimetric |
| $PM_{10}$ | 24 hr | $100$ | $100$ | $75$ | Gravimetric |
| $PM_{2.5}$ | Annual | $40$ | $40$ | $40$ | Gravimetric |
| $PM_{2.5}$ | 24 hr | $60$ | $60$ | $60$ | Gravimetric |
| $O_3$ | 8 hr | $120$ | $120$ | $100$ | UV Photometric |
| $O_3$ | 1 hr | $180$ | $180$ | $180$ | UV Photometric |
| $CO$ (mg/m³) | 8 hr | $2.0$ | $2.0$ | $2.0$ | NDIR |
| $CO$ (mg/m³) | 1 hr | $4.0$ | $4.0$ | $4.0$ | NDIR |
| $Pb$ ($\mu g/m^3$) | Annual | $0.5$ | $0.5$ | $0.5$ | AAS |
| $Pb$ ($\mu g/m^3$) | 24 hr | $1.0$ | $1.0$ | $1.0$ | AAS |
| $NH_3$ ($\mu g/m^3$) | Annual | $100$ | $100$ | $100$ | Indophenol Blue |
| $NH_3$ ($\mu g/m^3$) | 24 hr | $400$ | $400$ | $400$ | Indophenol Blue |
| Benzene ($\mu g/m^3$) | Annual | $5$ | $5$ | $5$ | GC |
| BaP ($ng/m^3$) | Annual | $1$ | $1$ | $1$ | HPLC |
| As ($ng/m^3$) | Annual | $6$ | $6$ | $6$ | AAS |
| Ni ($ng/m^3$) | Annual | $20$ | $20$ | $20$ | AAS |

*All values in $\mu g/m^3$ unless specified otherwise.*

## 2.5 WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines (2021 Update)

The 2021 WHO guidelines are **stricter than NAAQS 2009**, reflecting updated epidemiological evidence:

| Pollutant | Averaging Time | WHO 2021 (AQG) | WHO 2021 (Interim Target 1) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $PM_{2.5}$ | Annual | $5$ | $35$ |
| $PM_{2.5}$ | 24 hr | $15$ | $75$ |
| $PM_{10}$ | Annual | $15$ | $70$ |
| $PM_{10}$ | 24 hr | $45$ | $150$ |
| $O_3$ | 8 hr (peak season) | $100$ | $160$ |
| $NO_2$ | Annual | $10$ | $40$ |
| $NO_2$ | 24 hr | $25$ | $120$ |
| $SO_2$ | 24 hr | $40$ | $125$ |
| $CO$ ($mg/m^3$) | 24 hr | $4$ | $7$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight**: WHO's 2021 annual $PM_{2.5}$ guideline ($5 \text{ }\mu g/m^3$) is **8 times stricter** than the Indian NAAQS annual limit ($40 \text{ }\mu g/m^3$). This gap drives continuous regulatory revision.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in EIA

- **Baseline Data Collection**: EIA consultants measure ambient concentrations for the *existing scenario* before project construction.
- **Prediction & Assessment**: Models (AERMOD, CALPUFF, ISCST3) predict *future* pollutant levels — these are checked against AAQS.
- **Mitigation Planning**: Exceedance triggers design changes (tall stacks, bag filters, ESPs).
- **Monitoring**: Post-project, Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Stations (CAAQMS) verify compliance.
- **Public Health Studies**: AAQS exceedances correlate with hospital admissions (e.g., Delhi smog 2016, 2017).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From ppm to $\mu g/m^3$ (The KTU Classic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the single most-tested numerical concept in AAQS — practice it for at least 7-mark Part B questions.

### 3.1.1 Starting Premise

Concentration in **ppm (by volume)** means $1 \text{ cm}^3$ of pollutant per $10^6 \text{ cm}^3$ of air mixture (assuming ideal gas behaviour and equal molar distribution).

$$
C_{ppm} = \dfrac{V_{pollutant}}{V_{air}} \times 10^6
$$

### 3.1.2 Converting Volume to Moles

Using the **molar volume** ($V_m$) of an ideal gas at the reference state:

$$
n = \dfrac{V}{V_m}
$$

### 3.1.3 Converting Moles to Mass

$$
m = n \times MW = \dfrac{V}{V_m} \times MW
$$

### 3.1.4 Concentration per Cubic Meter

$$
C_{\mu g/m^3} = \dfrac{m}{V_{air} \text{ in } m^3} = \dfrac{V_{pollutant} \times MW}{V_{air} \times V_m}
$$

### 3.1.5 Final Algebraic Form

Substituting $C_{ppm} = V_{pollutant} \times 10^6 / V_{air}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\mu g/m^3} &= \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{C_{ppm}}{10^6} \times V_{air}\right) \times MW}{V_{air} \times V_m \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{L}} \\
&= C_{ppm} \times MW \times \dfrac{10^3}{V_m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.6 Final Master Equation

$$
\boxed{\,C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW}{V_m} \times 10^3\,}
$$

Where $V_m = 24.45 \text{ L/mol}$ at $25 \text{ °C}$ and $1 \text{ atm}$.

### 3.1.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem**: The 8-hour average $CO$ concentration near a roadway is measured as $9 \text{ ppm}$. Convert this to $\mu g/m^3$ at $25 \text{ °C}$, $1 \text{ atm}$, and verify whether it exceeds the NAAQS 8-hr standard of $2000 \text{ }\mu g/m^3$. Take $MW_{CO} = 28 \text{ g/mol}$.

**Step 1**: Identify constants.

$$
C_{ppm} = 9, \quad MW = 28 \text{ g/mol}, \quad V_m = 24.45 \text{ L/mol}
$$

**Step 2**: Apply the conversion formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\mu g/m^3} &= 9 \times \dfrac{28}{24.45} \times 10^3 \\
&= 9 \times 1.1452 \times 10^3 \\
&= 9 \times 1145.2 \\
&= 10306.8 \text{ }\mu g/m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: Compare with NAAQS.

$$
10306.8 \text{ }\mu g/m^3 \;\gg\; 2000 \text{ }\mu g/m^3 \quad (\text{NAAQS 8-hr for CO})
$$

**Conclusion**: The concentration **exceeds** the standard by approximately **5.15 times** — immediate mitigation required.

## 3.2 Derivation: Temperature & Pressure Correction Factor

When ambient conditions deviate from $25 \text{ °C}, 1 \text{ atm}$, apply:

$$
\dfrac{C_1}{C_2} = \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} \times \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}
$$

Where $T$ is in Kelvin.

### 3.2.1 Worked Example

> **Problem**: $SO_2$ is measured as $0.08 \text{ ppm}$ at $35 \text{ °C}$ and $1 \text{ atm}$. Convert to $\mu g/m^3$ at the same conditions. Take $MW_{SO_2} = 64$.

**Step 1**: Correct molar volume to local temperature.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_m^{local} &= 22.4 \times \dfrac{(273 + 35)}{273} \\
&= 22.4 \times \dfrac{308}{273} \\
&= 22.4 \times 1.1282 \\
&= 25.27 \text{ L/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: Apply conversion.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\mu g/m^3} &= 0.08 \times \dfrac{64}{25.27} \times 10^3 \\
&= 0.08 \times 2532.6 \\
&= 202.6 \text{ }\mu g/m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: Compare with NAAQS 24-hr ($80 \text{ }\mu g/m^3$).

$$
202.6 \text{ }\mu g/m^3 \;\gg\; 80 \text{ }\mu g/m^3 \quad \Rightarrow \text{**EXCEEDANCE**}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Air Quality Index (AQI) Sub-Index

The AQI follows a **piecewise linear interpolation** model:

$$
I_p = \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \times (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}
$$

Where:

- $I_p$ = sub-index for pollutant $p$
- $C_p$ = measured concentration of pollutant $p$
- $BP_{Hi}, BP_{Lo}$ = breakpoint concentrations bounding $C_p$
- $I_{Hi}, I_{Lo}$ = AQI values corresponding to $BP_{Hi}, BP_{Lo}$

The overall **AQI** = $\max\{I_p\}$ across all pollutants.

### 3.3.1 Worked Example

> **Problem**: $PM_{2.5}$ 24-hr concentration is $90 \text{ }\mu g/m^3$. Compute its AQI sub-index using the CPCB breakpoint table.

CPCB Breakpoints (truncated):

| $BP_{Lo}$ | $BP_{Hi}$ | $I_{Lo}$ | $I_{Hi}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $61$ | $90$ | $101$ | $200$ |

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{PM_{2.5}} &= \dfrac{200 - 101}{90 - 61} \times (90 - 61) + 101 \\
&= \dfrac{99}{29} \times 29 + 101 \\
&= 99 + 101 \\
&= 200
\end{aligned}
$$

**AQI Sub-Index** = $200$ → *"Moderate"* category.

## 3.4 Conversion for Common Pollutants (KTU Quick Reference)

| Pollutant | $MW$ (g/mol) | $1 \text{ ppm} = ? \text{ }\mu g/m^3$ (at 25 °C) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $SO_2$ | $64$ | $2620$ |
| $NO_2$ | $46$ | $1880$ |
| $NO$ | $30$ | $1230$ |
| $CO$ | $28$ | $1145$ |
| $O_3$ | $48$ | $1960$ |
| $NH_3$ | $17$ | $695$ |
| $H_2S$ | $34$ | $1390$ |
| $Cl_2$ | $71$ | $2904$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor**: $SO_2 \approx 2.6 \times 10^3 \text{ }\mu g/m^3$ per ppm. For quick mental math, *double the MW × 40* — that's your conversion factor.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: AAQS Framework

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the regulatory architecture governing Ambient Air Quality Standards, mapped for the Indian EIA context. This block-level architecture shows how scientific knowledge flows from international bodies (WHO, US-EPA) into national legislation (CPCB NAAQS) and finally into ground-level EIA applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[International Scientific Bodies] --> A1[WHO 2021 Global Air Quality Guidelines]
    A --> A2[US EPA NAAQS Criteria Pollutants]
    A --> A3[European Union AAQD 2016 2008 50 EC]

    B[National Regulatory Framework] --> B1[MoEFCC Govt of India]
    B --> B2[CPCB Central Pollution Control Board]
    B --> B3[SPCB State Pollution Control Boards]

    C[Legal Foundation] --> C1[Air Prevention and Control of Pollution Act 1981]
    C --> C2[Water Cess Act 1977]
    C --> C3[EIA Notification 2006 Amended 2020]

    D[CPCB NAAQS 2009] --> D1[12 Criteria Pollutants]
    D1 --> D2[PM10 PM2.5 SO2 NO2 CO O3 Pb]
    D1 --> D3[NH3 Benzene BaP As Ni]

    E[Area Classification] --> E1[Industrial Area]
    E --> E2[Residential Rural and Other]
    E --> E3[Sensitive Area Eco Fragile Zones]

    F[EIA Application] --> F1[Baseline AAQM at 3 to 5 stations]
    F --> F2[Dispersion Modelling AERMOD CALPUFF]
    F --> F3[Predicted vs NAAQS Comparison]
    F --> F4[Design Mitigation Bag Filter ESP Tall Stack]
    F --> F5[CAAQMS Continuous Monitoring]

    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B2
    C1 --> B2
    C3 --> F
    B2 --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F3 --> G[Final EIA Report and EMP]
    F5 --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram**: Trace the flow from **International Science** (top) → **National Law** (middle) → **Ground-level EIA Practice** (bottom). The AAQS is the **bridge** between scientific evidence and engineering action.

## 4.2 Decision Tree: Predict → Compare → Mitigate (KTU Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Project Activity Identified] --> P2[Emission Inventory Stack and Fugitive]
    P2 --> P3[Dispersion Model Run Gaussian Plume]
    P3 --> Q1{Predicted Concentration GLC}
    Q1 -->|Exceeds NAAQS| R1[Trigger Mitigation Measures]
    Q1 -->|Within NAAQS| R2[Compliance Statement]
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> S1[Continuous Monitoring via CAAQMS]
    S1 --> S2[Annual Compliance Report to SPCB]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the core EIA decision loop** — examiners may ask students to identify the *trigger point* (the Q1 diamond) where AAQS becomes the deciding criterion.

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Pollutants by Health Priority

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Highest Risk PM2.5] --> H2[High Risk PM10]
    H2 --> H3[High Risk NO2]
    H3 --> H4[Moderate Risk SO2]
    H4 --> H5[Moderate Risk O3]
    H5 --> H6[Lower Risk CO Acute only]
    H6 --> H7[Trace Risk Pb As Ni Carcinogenic]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Ambient Air Quality Standards. List any four criteria pollutants monitored under the Indian NAAQS.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Definition (1 Mark)**: Ambient Air Quality Standards (AAQS) are the prescribed maximum allowable concentrations of specific air pollutants in the outdoor atmosphere, set by regulatory authorities to safeguard human health, vegetation, and materials from pollution impacts. In India, these are notified by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** under the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**.
>
> **Four Criteria Pollutants (2 Marks)**:
> 1. **Particulate Matter** ($PM_{10}$ and $PM_{2.5}$)
> 2. **Sulphur Dioxide** ($SO_2$)
> 3. **Nitrogen Dioxide** ($NO_2$)
> 4. **Carbon Monoxide** ($CO$)
>
> *(Any four from the 12 NAAQS criteria pollutants are acceptable.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Differentiate between primary and secondary air quality standards with suitable examples.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Primary Standard | Secondary Standard |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **Objective (1 M)** | Protect **human health** | Protect **welfare** — vegetation, soil, water, visibility, materials, climate |
> | **Strictness (1 M)** | Stricter | More relaxed or equal |
> | **Example (1 M)** | $PM_{2.5}$ annual: $12 \text{ }\mu g/m^3$ (US-EPA primary) | $SO_2$ 3-hr: $1300 \text{ }\mu g/m^3$ (US-EPA secondary, vegetation damage prevention) |
>
> **Key Point**: Primary standards are set to be safe even for sensitive sub-populations (children, asthmatics, elderly). Secondary standards protect the broader environment.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** of India as notified by CPCB. Discuss the classification of monitoring areas and provide a comparative table for **PM10, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2** under Industrial, Residential, and Sensitive area categories. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The ambient air near a thermal power plant boundary shows **$SO_2$ concentration of 0.075 ppm** at **40 °C and 1 atm**. Convert this to **$\mu g/m^3$** and compare with the NAAQS 24-hour limit of $80 \text{ }\mu g/m^3$. Molecular weight of $SO_2 = 64 \text{ g/mol}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Solution (a) — [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — NAAQS Definition (2 Marks)**: The NAAQS are a set of legally binding pollutant concentration limits notified by the CPCB under Section 16 of the Air Act, 1981. They cover **12 criteria pollutants** and apply uniformly across India, but with **tiered limits** based on land-use classification.

**Step 2 — Area Classification (2 Marks)**:

- **Industrial Areas**: Heavy industries, power plants — slightly relaxed limits.
- **Residential, Rural & Other Areas**: Default urban/agricultural ambient — moderate strictness.
- **Sensitive Areas**: Hospitals, schools, eco-fragile zones (Taj Trapezium, national parks) — **strictest** limits.

**Step 3 — Comparative Table (3 Marks)**:

| Pollutant | Avg Time | Industrial | Residential | Sensitive |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $PM_{10}$ ($\mu g/m^3$) | 24 hr | $100$ | $100$ | $75$ |
| $PM_{10}$ ($\mu g/m^3$) | Annual | $60$ | $60$ | $60$ |
| $PM_{2.5}$ ($\mu g/m^3$) | 24 hr | $60$ | $60$ | $60$ |
| $PM_{2.5}$ ($\mu g/m^3$) | Annual | $40$ | $40$ | $40$ |
| $SO_2$ ($\mu g/m^3$) | 24 hr | $80$ | $80$ | $30$ |
| $SO_2$ ($\mu g/m^3$) | Annual | $50$ | $50$ | $20$ |
| $NO_2$ ($\mu g/m^3$) | 24 hr | $80$ | $80$ | $30$ |
| $NO_2$ ($\mu g/m^3$) | Annual | $40$ | $40$ | $30$ |

---

### Solution (b) — [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify Inputs** (1 Mark):

$C_{ppm} = 0.075, \quad MW = 64 \text{ g/mol}, \quad T = 40 \text{ °C} = 313 \text{ K}, \quad P = 1 \text{ atm}$

**Step 2 — Correct Molar Volume for Local Temperature** (2 Marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_m^{40°C} &= 22.4 \times \dfrac{T_{local}}{T_{STP}} \\
&= 22.4 \times \dfrac{313}{273} \\
&= 22.4 \times 1.1465 \\
&= 25.68 \text{ L/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Conversion Formula** (2 Marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\mu g/m^3} &= C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW}{V_m} \times 10^3 \\
&= 0.075 \times \dfrac{64}{25.68} \times 10^3 \\
&= 0.075 \times 2492.2 \\
&= 186.9 \text{ }\mu g/m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with NAAQS** (2 Marks):

$$
186.9 \text{ }\mu g/m^3 \;\; \text{vs.}\;\; \text{NAAQS limit} = 80 \text{ }\mu g/m^3
$$

**Exceedance Factor**: $\dfrac{186.9}{80} = 2.34 \times$

**Conclusion**: The concentration **exceeds** the 24-hr NAAQS limit by a factor of **2.34**. Mandatory mitigative actions required (e.g., Flue Gas Desulphurization, taller stack, alternate fuel).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the **WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines (2021)**. Compare the Indian NAAQS limits with WHO guidelines for **PM2.5 and NO2** and explain the engineering implications of the gap. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **formula to convert ppm to $\mu g/m^3$** at 25 °C and 1 atm. Use it to determine the $SO_2$ concentration in $\mu g/m^3$ given $C_{SO_2} = 0.05 \text{ ppm}$. Take $MW = 64 \text{ g/mol}$ and NAAQS 24-hr limit = $80 \text{ }\mu g/m^3$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Solution (a) — [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — WHO 2021 Context (2 Marks)**: The World Health Organization revised its Global Air Quality Guidelines in September 2021, drastically reducing acceptable pollutant concentrations based on updated epidemiological evidence linking air pollution to mortality and morbidity. The guidelines provide an **Air Quality Guideline (AQG)** value (target) plus four **Interim Targets (IT-1 to IT-4)** for gradual reduction.

**Step 2 — Comparison Table (3 Marks)**:

| Pollutant | Averaging | India NAAQS | WHO 2021 AQG | WHO IT-1 | Gap Multiplier |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $PM_{2.5}$ ($\mu g/m^3$) | Annual | $40$ | $5$ | $35$ | $8\times$ stricter (WHO) |
| $PM_{2.5}$ ($\mu g/m^3$) | 24 hr | $60$ | $15$ | $75$ | $4\times$ stricter (WHO) |
| $NO_2$ ($\mu g/m^3$) | Annual | $40$ | $10$ | $40$ | $4\times$ stricter (WHO) |
| $NO_2$ ($\mu g/m^3$) | 24 hr | $80$ | $25$ | $120$ | $3.2\times$ stricter (WHO) |

**Step 3 — Engineering Implications (2 Marks)**:

- **Tighter control technologies** needed (HEPA filters, ESPs, SCR/SNCR for $NO_x$).
- **Renewable energy transition** to meet $PM_{2.5}$ annual AQG.
- **Urban planning changes**: buffer zones, green corridors, EV mandates.
- **CPCB's National Clean Air Programme (NCAP)** aims to bridge this gap by 40% by 2025–26.

---

### Solution (b) — [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Derivation Setup (3 Marks)**: Starting from the definition of ppm:

$$
C_{ppm} = \dfrac{V_{pollutant}}{V_{air}} \times 10^6
$$
