For 1 m³ of air = $10^3$ L, using molar volume $V_m = 24.45 \text{ L/mol}$:

$$
\text{Moles of air} = \dfrac{10^3}{24.45} = 40.9 \text{ mol}
$$

$$
\text{Moles of pollutant} = C_{ppm} \times 40.9 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Moles to Mass** (2 Marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of pollutant} &= \text{moles} \times MW \\
&= C_{ppm} \times 40.9 \times 10^{-6} \times MW \text{ (in g)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to $\mu g$ (×$10^6$):

$$
C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times 40.9 \times MW \times 10^{-6} \times 10^6 = C_{ppm} \times 40.9 \times MW
$$

**Final Formula**:

$$
\boxed{\,C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW \times 10^3}{24.45}\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply to $SO_2$ Problem** (2 Marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{SO_2} &= 0.05 \times \dfrac{64 \times 10^3}{24.45} \\
&= 0.05 \times 2618 \\
&= 130.9 \text{ }\mu g/m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison**: $130.9 \text{ }\mu g/m^3 \;>\; 80 \text{ }\mu g/m^3$ → **Exceeds NAAQS** by 1.64×.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in AAQS Problems:**
>
> 1. **Molar Volume Confusion**: Students often use $22.4 \text{ L/mol}$ (NTP) when $24.45 \text{ L/mol}$ (25 °C) is required, or vice versa. **Always** check the reference temperature in the question.
> 2. **Unit Mix-up**: Forgetting the $\times 10^3$ factor (mg to μg conversion) — results in answers off by **3 orders of magnitude**.
> 3. **Molecular Weight of Pollutant vs Air**: $MW$ in the formula refers to the **pollutant gas**, NOT air (28.97). Many students mistakenly use $MW_{air}$.
> 4. **Temperature in Kelvin**: Always convert °C to K before applying isothermal correction. Using °C directly yields wrong results.
> 5. **Forgetting to Compare**: A complete answer must **always** include the final comparison with NAAQS limit and a compliance/exceedance statement — this is worth 1-2 marks.
> 6. **NAAQS Year**: Cite the year (e.g., *"NAAQS 2009"*) — newer revisions are emerging and citing outdated standards may lose marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — AAQS**
>
> - [ ] **AAQS = Legal Limits** on pollutant concentrations in ambient (outdoor) air.
> - [ ] **12 Criteria Pollutants** in Indian NAAQS (2009): PM10, PM2.5, SO2, NO2, CO, O3, Pb, NH3, Benzene, BaP, As, Ni.
> - [ ] **3 Area Categories**: Industrial, Residential/Rural, Sensitive.
> - [ ] **Sensitive Areas** = strictest limits (hospitals, heritage sites, eco-fragile zones).
> - [ ] **Master Conversion**: $C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times MW \times 10^3 / V_m$.
> - [ ] **Molar Volume**: $22.4 \text{ L/mol}$ at 0 °C; $24.45 \text{ L/mol}$ at 25 °C.
> - [ ] **PM2.5 NAAQS 24-hr = 60 μg/m³**; Annual = **40 μg/m³**.
> - [ ] **PM10 NAAQS 24-hr = 100 μg/m³**; Annual = **60 μg/m³**.
> - [ ] **SO2 NAAQS 24-hr = 80 μg/m³**; Annual = **50 μg/m³**.
> - [ ] **NO2 NAAQS 24-hr = 80 μg/m³**; Annual = **40 μg/m³**.
> - [ ] **WHO 2021 Annual PM2.5 = 5 μg/m³** (8× stricter than India).
> - [ ] **AQI** = piecewise linear interpolation; reported value = **maximum sub-index** across all pollutants.
> - [ ] **EIA Workflow**: Baseline → Emission Inventory → Dispersion Modelling → Compare with AAQS → Mitigate if exceedance → Continuous Monitoring.
> - [ ] **Primary Standard** = human health; **Secondary Standard** = welfare (vegetation, materials, visibility).
> - [ ] **Legal Basis**: Air Act 1981, EPA 1986, EIA Notification 2006 (amended 2020).

---

**End of Module 1 — Ambient Air Quality Standards Notes**
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | OECHT617 — Environmental Impact Assessment*

topics.title: Meteorological Factors Affecting Air Pollution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Meteorological Factors Affecting Air Pollution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Meteorological factors affecting air pollution** are the atmospheric and weather variables that govern the **transport, dispersion, transformation, and removal of air pollutants** in the troposphere. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, these factors form the *primary input layer* of any Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) air-quality model, because a pollutant released at a stack is essentially "at the mercy" of the surrounding atmosphere.

The principal factors are:
- **Wind** (speed and direction)
- **Atmospheric stability** (lapse rate behaviour)
- **Temperature inversions** (stability enhancement)
- **Mixing height** (volume of dilution)
- **Precipitation** (wet deposition)
- **Humidity** (aqueous-phase chemistry)
- **Solar radiation** (photochemistry and stability)
- **Topography** (channeling, valley winds)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO1 / Module 1):**
> In an EIA report, the *baseline meteorological data* must be collected for **at least one full season** (preferably a full year) before siting an industry. Without these data, dispersion modeling (ISCST3, AERMOD) cannot be run.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of the atmosphere as a **giant invisible river** flowing over the Earth's surface. If you pour ink (pollutants) into a fast, turbulent river, the ink disperses quickly downstream and becomes faint. If you pour ink into a **stagnant pond** (no wind, strong inversion), the ink sits in a thick, dark blob right where you poured it.

**Key intuition:**
- **Wind = current speed** of the river (carries pollutants away)
- **Stability = mixing energy** of the river (turbulence = dispersion)
- **Mixing height = depth of the river** (taller layer = more dilution)
- **Rain = a hand washing the river clean** (wet scavenging)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric for Wind in EIA:** Wind speed is conventionally measured at **10 m height** over open terrain (WMO standard), and is then extrapolated to stack height using the **Power-Law Profile** or the **Log-Wind Profile**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants Used in This Topic

| Constant | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | Acceleration due to gravity |
| $C_p$ | **1004 J/(kg·K)** | Specific heat of dry air at constant pressure |
| $R$ | **287 J/(kg·K)** | Specific gas constant for dry air |
| $\Gamma_d$ | **9.8 K/km** | Dry adiabatic lapse rate |
| $\Gamma_s$ | **~5.8 K/km** | Saturated adiabatic lapse rate (varies with T, p) |
| $T_{ref}$ | **288.15 K** | Standard reference temperature (15 °C) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Wind — The Primary Dispersive Agent

Wind transports pollutants from the source to receptors (downwind villages, crops, hospitals). Two attributes matter:
- **Wind speed ($u$)** → controls dilution and travel time.
- **Wind direction ($\theta$)** → controls the geographic corridor affected.

A **wind rose** is a polar plot showing the frequency of wind blowing FROM each direction. The longest spoke is the **prevailing wind direction**. EIA recommends that sensitive receptors (schools, hospitals) should NOT be located downwind of the prevailing flow.

#### 2.1.1 Wind-Speed Profile (Power Law)

Because the surface drags the wind, wind speed increases with height. The simplest extrapolation:

$$\frac{u_z}{u_{z_r}} = \left(\frac{z}{z_r}\right)^{p}$$

where
- $u_z$ = wind speed at height $z$
- $u_{z_r}$ = reference wind speed at reference height $z_r$ (usually 10 m)
- $p$ = dimensionless power-law exponent, depends on stability:

| Stability Class | $p$ |
|---|---|
| A (Very Unstable) | 0.07 |
| B (Unstable) | 0.07 |
| C (Slightly Unstable) | 0.10 |
| D (Neutral) | 0.15 |
| E (Slightly Stable) | 0.35 |
| F (Stable) | 0.55 |

### 2.2 Atmospheric Stability

A parcel of air rising from the surface will cool (or warm) at the **adiabatic lapse rate**. The **Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR)** is the actual vertical temperature profile of the atmosphere at a given moment. The comparison between ELR and $\Gamma_d$ determines stability.

| Condition | ELR vs. $\Gamma_d$ | Result |
|---|---|---|
| **Absolutely Unstable** | ELR > $\Gamma_d$ | Parcel continues to rise vigorously |
| **Neutral** | ELR = $\Gamma_d$ | Parcel stops at the level it reaches |
| **Stable** | ELR < $\Gamma_d$ (or inverted) | Parcel is pushed back down |

### 2.3 Pasquill–Gifford Stability Classification

The most widely used scheme (used in ISCST3 / AERMOD) classifies stability into **6 classes A–F** based on incoming solar radiation (day) or cloud cover (night) and the surface wind speed.

| Surface Wind (m/s) | **Strong** Solar (Day) | **Moderate** Solar | **Slight** Solar | Thin Overcast / ≥ 4/8 Low Cloud | ≤ 3/8 Cloud | Clear / ≥ 4/8 High Cloud |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| < 2 | A | A–B | B | — | — | — |
| 2 – 3 | A–B | B | C | C | D | F |
| 3 – 5 | B | B–C | C | C | D | F |
| 5 – 6 | C | C–D | D | D | D | D |
| > 6 | C | D | D | D | D | D |

*For night, replace "Solar" with "Net Radiative Loss."* Categories D, E, F apply; E and F require low wind.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why stability matters for plume shape:**
> - **Unstable (A, B, C):** Looping, convective plumes — large vertical mixing, low ground concentration far away.
> - **Neutral (D):** Coning plume — moderate mixing.
> - **Stable (E, F):** Fanning plume (no vertical spread) — pollutants trapped above ground.

### 2.4 Temperature Inversions

A temperature inversion is a layer in which temperature **increases with height**, producing extreme stability. EIA reports MUST identify inversion frequency and base height.

| Type | Mechanism | EIA Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Radiation (Ground) Inversion** | Night-time radiative cooling of surface | Common in winter, breaks by mid-morning |
| **Subsidence Inversion** | Large-scale sinking of high-pressure air | Persistent, may cap entire urban scale |
| **Frontal Inversion** | Warm air mass overriding cold | Associated with pre-monsoon/cyclonic weather |
| **Valley Inversion** | Cold-air pooling in valleys | Critical for thermal power plants in valleys |

### 2.5 Mixing Height ($H$)

The **mixing height** is the vertical extent of the well-mixed boundary layer. It is the "ceiling" up to which pollutants can readily disperse.

- Daytime (convective): $H$ can reach **1000 – 3000 m**.
- Night-time (stable): $H$ can collapse to **50 – 200 m**.

> **Simple estimator (Holzworth):**
> $H = 0.5 \cdot u_{max}^{1.5}$ (in metres) where $u_{max}$ is the mid-day max wind in m/s (rough rule of thumb).

### 2.6 Precipitation and Humidity

- **Wet deposition (scavenging)** is described by a first-order washout coefficient $\Lambda$ (s⁻¹). The concentration decays as:
$$C(t) = C_0 \, e^{-\Lambda \, t}$$
- **Humidity** governs aqueous-phase oxidation of SO₂ to sulfuric acid and of NO₂ to HNO₃ — i.e., **acid rain formation**.

### 2.7 Solar Radiation and Photochemistry

Solar flux drives photolysis reactions, the most important of which is:
$$NO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow NO + O$$
followed by $O + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M$. Thus, **ozone formation peaks on hot, sunny, stagnant days** — the classic photochemical smog episode.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u_z / u_{z_r} = (z/z_r)^{p}$ | Wind-power law | Extrapolate wind to stack height |
| 2 | $\Gamma_d = g/C_p$ | Dry adiabatic lapse rate | Compare with ELR for stability |
| 3 | $F = g \cdot V_s \cdot d_s^2 \cdot (T_s - T_a) / (4 T_s)$ | Buoyancy flux (m⁴/s³) | Plume-rise calculation |
| 4 | $\Delta h = 1.6 F^{1/3} x_f^{2/3} / u$ | Final plume rise (Briggs) | Effective stack height |
| 5 | $\Delta h = 2.6 (F / (u \cdot s))^{1/3}$ | Stable plume rise | $E, F$ stability |
| 6 | $s = (g/T_a)(d\theta/dz)$ | Stability parameter | s⁻² for stable layer |
| 7 | $C(t) = C_0 e^{-\Lambda t}$ | Wet scavenging | Rain washout of pollutants |
| 8 | $H_{eff} = H_s + \Delta h$ | Effective stack height | Used in Gaussian dispersion |

> **Engineering utility:** These eight equations collectively form the **"engine-room" of an EIA air-quality assessment**. They are used in AERMOD, ISCST3, and CALPUFF to predict ground-level concentrations for short-term (1-hr, 24-hr) and long-term (annual) averaging periods — the standard outputs required by India's National Ambient Air Quality Monitoring (NAAQMS) standards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate $\Gamma_d$

Consider an air parcel of unit mass rising adiabatically. The First Law of Thermodynamics:

$$dQ = C_p \, dT - \frac{dp}{\rho}$$

For an adiabatic process $dQ = 0$, and using the ideal-gas relation $p = \rho R T$, and hydrostatic balance $dp = -\rho g \, dz$, we have:

$$0 = C_p \, dT - \frac{-\rho g \, dz}{\rho}$$

$$0 = C_p \, dT + g \, dz$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dT}{dz} = -\frac{g}{C_p}$$

Therefore, the **dry adiabatic lapse rate** is:

$$\boxed{\Gamma_d = \frac{g}{C_p} = \frac{9.81}{1004} \approx 0.0098 \text{ K/m} = 9.8 \text{ K/km}}$$

**Interpretation:** A rising unsaturated air parcel cools at ~9.8 K per km of ascent. If the environmental air cools *faster* than this, the parcel is warmer (less dense) than its surroundings → it keeps rising → **unstable** atmosphere. If the environment cools *slower* (or warms with height = inversion), the parcel is cooler → it sinks back → **stable** atmosphere.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Plume-Rise (Briggs, Unstable/Neutral)

The buoyancy flux $F$ is defined as the rate of release of buoyancy per unit length of plume. For a hot stack gas of volumetric flow $V_s$, stack diameter $d_s$, and exit temperature $T_s$, in ambient air $T_a$:

$$F = g \cdot V_s \cdot \left(\frac{d_s}{2}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{T_s - T_a}{T_s}$$

Briggs solved the entrainment equation for the final plume rise $\Delta h$ in unstable/neutral conditions:

$$\Delta h = 1.6 \cdot F^{1/3} \cdot x_f^{2/3} / u$$

where the distance to final rise $x_f$ is:

$$x_f = \begin{cases}
49 \, F^{5/8} & \text{if } F < 55 \\
119 \, F^{2/5} & \text{if } F \geq 55
\end{cases}$$

(Units: $F$ in m⁴/s³, $u$ in m/s, $x$ in m, $\Delta h$ in m.)

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Effective Stack Height

> **Problem (KTU-style 14-marker):**
> A coal-fired boiler has the following stack data:
> - Diameter $d_s = 1.5$ m
> - Volumetric flow $V_s = 25$ m³/s
> - Stack gas temperature $T_s = 423$ K
> - Ambient temperature $T_a = 303$ K
> - Wind speed at stack height $u = 6$ m/s
> - Stability class: **D (neutral)**
> - Stack height $H_s = 60$ m
> Compute the **effective stack height**.

**Step 1 — Compute buoyancy flux $F$:**

$$F = g \cdot V_s \cdot (d_s/2)^2 \cdot (T_s - T_a) / T_s$$

$$F = 9.81 \cdot 25 \cdot (0.75)^2 \cdot (423 - 303)/423$$

$$F = 9.81 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.5625 \cdot 120/423$$

$$F = 9.81 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.5625 \cdot 0.2837$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 39.13 \text{ m}^4/\text{s}^3}$$

**Step 2 — Determine regime (F < 55):**

Use $x_f = 49 F^{5/8}$:

$$x_f = 49 \cdot (39.13)^{5/8} = 49 \cdot 11.62 \approx 569.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Final plume rise $\Delta h$ (Briggs, neutral):**

$$\Delta h = 1.6 F^{1/3} x_f^{2/3} / u$$

$$F^{1/3} = (39.13)^{1/3} \approx 3.405$$

$$x_f^{2/3} = (569.4)^{2/3} \approx 68.62$$

$$\Delta h = 1.6 \cdot 3.405 \cdot 68.62 / 6$$

$$\Delta h = 1.6 \cdot 233.6 / 6 \approx 62.30 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Effective stack height:**

$$H_{eff} = H_s + \Delta h = 60 + 62.3 \approx \boxed{122.3 \text{ m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in EIA:** The predicted maximum ground-level concentration (under Gaussian model) is **inversely proportional to $H_{eff}^2$** for a single point source. So a 60 m increase in $\Delta h$ can reduce the maximum 1-hour GLC by an order of magnitude — the entire basis for tall-stack design and for setting emission standards.

---

### 3.4 Stability Analysis — Worked Example

> **Problem:** At 14:00 hrs (afternoon, strong solar radiation), the surface wind is 4 m/s and the temperature profile measured is:
>
> | Height (m) | 2 | 10 | 50 | 100 | 200 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | Temp (°C) | 28 | 26.5 | 23.5 | 21 | 19 |
>
> Determine the stability class and state whether an inversion is present.

**Step 1 — Compute ELR between 2 m and 200 m:**

$$ELR = -\frac{dT}{dz} = -\frac{19 - 28}{200 - 2} = -\frac{-9}{198} = +0.0455 \text{ °C/m} = 4.55 \text{ °C/km}$$

(In other words, temperature **drops** by 4.55 °C per km ascent.)

**Step 2 — Compare with $\Gamma_d$:**

$\Gamma_d = 9.8$ °C/km. Since ELR (4.55) < $\Gamma_d$ (9.8), the atmosphere is **stable** in this layer.

**Step 3 — Inversion?** No — temperature is decreasing, not increasing. So this is a stable layer, not an inversion.

**Step 4 — Use Pasquill table:** Strong solar, 4 m/s → row "2 – 5" → Class **B** (unstable). Note that the *table* classifies stability of the boundary layer from surface flux, which is more rigorous than ELR alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** KTU questions often mix the *local ELR comparison method* (used by radio-sonde) with the *Pasquill classification method* (used by surface met stations). The two can disagree, and the more local measurement should be used. Always state which method you used.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Plume Behaviour vs. Stability Class

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Pollutant Release from Stack]) --> Wind1{Wind Condition}
    Wind1 -->|Low wind + Strong sun| ClassA[Class A - Very Unstable]
    Wind1 -->|Moderate wind + sun| ClassC[Class C - Slightly Unstable]
    Wind1 -->|Strong wind| ClassD[Class D - Neutral]
    Wind1 -->|Low wind + Night| ClassF[Class F - Stable]

    ClassA --> PlumeShapeA[Looping Plume - High vertical spread]
    ClassC --> PlumeShapeC[Coning Plume - Moderate spread]
    ClassD --> PlumeShapeD[Fanning Plume - Horizontal only]
    ClassF --> PlumeShapeF[Fumigation on sunrise - Trapped below inversion]

    PlumeShapeA --> GLC1[Low GLC far downwind]
    PlumeShapeC --> GLC2[Moderate GLC]
    PlumeShapeD --> GLC3[Modest GLC if inversion aloft]
    PlumeShapeF --> GLC4[VERY HIGH near-source GLC - Hazard zone]

    style ClassA fill:#FFB6B6
    style ClassC fill:#FFE4B5
    style ClassD fill:#FFFFE0
    style ClassF fill:#B0E0E6
    style GLC4 fill:#FF6961,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram: Meteorological Inputs to an EIA Air-Quality Assessment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Surface Met Station] --> B1[Wind Speed u]
    A1 --> B2[Wind Direction theta]
    A1 --> B3[Solar Radiation S]
    A1 --> B4[Temp, Humidity, Rainfall]

    A2[Upper-Air Sounding] --> C1[ELR Profile]
    A2 --> C2[Mixing Height H]
    A2 --> C3[Inversion Height]

    A3[Source Inventory] --> D1[Stack Height Hs]
    A3 --> D2[Exit Velocity Vs]
    A3 --> D3[Exit Temperature Ts]
    A3 --> D4[Pollutant Emission Rate Q]

    B1 --> E1[Dispersion Model AERMOD]
    B2 --> E1
    B3 --> E1
    C1 --> E1
    C2 --> E1
    C3 --> E1
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1

    E1 --> F1[Predicted GLC at Receptors]
    F1 --> G1[Compare with NAAQS]
    G1 --> H1{Compliant?}
    H1 -->|No| I1[Add Pollution Control / Increase Stack Height]
    H1 -->|Yes| J1[Approve with Monitoring]
    I1 --> E1

    style E1 fill:#FFD700
    style F1 fill:#90EE90
    style H1 fill:#FFB6C1
```

### 4.3 Vertical Structure of the Boundary Layer (Inversion Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Free Atmosphere above ~3 km] --> INV{Inversion Layer}
    INV -->|Acts as LID| ML[Convective/Mechanical Mixing Layer H]
    ML --> SOIL[Ground Surface - Daytime]

    Note1[Strong solar - day: H reaches 1000-3000 m] --- ML
    Note2[Inversion CAPS pollutants - no escape] --- INV
    Note3[Cool night: H collapses to 50-200 m] --- SOIL

    style INV fill:#FF6961,color:#fff
    style ML fill:#B0E0E6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define the term "atmospheric stability" and state the role of the Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR) in determining it.**

**Model Answer:**
Atmospheric stability is the tendency of the atmosphere to resist or enhance vertical motion. The **Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR = 9.8 K/km)** is the rate at which an unsaturated rising air parcel cools. Stability is determined by comparing the actual environmental lapse rate (ELR) with the DALR:
- If ELR > DALR → absolutely unstable (vertical motion enhanced).
- If ELR = DALR → neutral.
- If ELR < DALR → stable (vertical motion suppressed).
- If temperature increases with height → inversion → extremely stable.

(3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] What is a "wind rose" and what information does it provide for an EIA study?**

**Model Answer:**
A wind rose is a polar diagram that depicts, for a given location, the **frequency** with which the wind blows from each compass direction and the **wind-speed class** associated with each direction. In an EIA study, it provides:
1. The **prevailing wind direction** — the corridor in which emissions will most frequently travel, guiding the location of sensitive receptors.
2. The **calm percentage** — hours with very low wind (low dispersion potential).
3. The **seasonal variation** of wind — important for siting industries whose emissions are seasonal (e.g., brick kilns, agricultural burning).

(3 marks)

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] (a) Explain in detail the Pasquill–Gifford stability classification scheme used in air-quality modeling. List the six classes and describe the typical plume shape and vertical spread associated with each. (7 marks)**
**(b) Discuss the four major types of temperature inversions and explain how each affects pollutant dispersion. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**
The Pasquill–Gifford scheme classifies atmospheric stability into **six classes A to F** based on the **surface wind speed** and the **incoming solar radiation during the day** (or **cloud cover at night**). The classes are:

| Class | Stability | Daytime Solar | Typical Plume |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | Very Unstable | Strong | Large looping |
| B | Moderately Unstable | Moderate | Looping with definite vertical spread |
| C | Slightly Unstable | Slight | Coning with moderate spread |
| D | Neutral | Overcast / high wind | Straight horizontal (coning) |
| E | Slightly Stable | Clear night, low wind | Fanning (no spread) |
| F | Moderately Stable | Clear night, calm | Strong fanning, trapped |

The choice of class determines the **vertical dispersion coefficients $\sigma_z$** used in the Gaussian plume equation. For example, $\sigma_z$ at 1 km is largest for class A (≈ 150 m) and smallest for class F (≈ 8 m). In modern EPA models (AERMOD), the **Monin–Obukhov length** replaces the Pasquill class, but Pasquill is still the academic baseline.

**Model Solution to (b):**
The four major types of inversions are:

1. **Radiation (Ground) Inversion:** Forms on clear, calm nights when the ground loses heat by long-wave radiation. Surface cools, creating a shallow (≈ 50 – 300 m) but strong inversion. **Effect:** Pollutants released at night are trapped near the surface, leading to fumigation the next morning when the sun breaks the inversion.

2. **Subsidence Inversion:** Caused by large-scale sinking of air in high-pressure systems (anticyclones). It can cap the mixing layer at 500 – 2000 m and persist for days. **Effect:** Entire city-scale accumulation of pollutants (e.g., Delhi winter smog, Los Angeles basin).

3. **Frontal Inversion:** Occurs at the boundary between a cold air mass below and warm air above in a weather front. **Effect:** Long-range transport of pollutants along the frontal surface.

4. **Valley Inversion:** Cold air drains down slopes at night and pools in valleys, creating a thick cold-air layer under warmer air aloft. **Effect:** Critical for thermal power plants in valleys (e.g., Singrauli, Korba); causes severe winter pollution episodes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (loss of 2–3 marks):**
> 1. Listing inversion types but failing to mention their **vertical thickness** and **typical duration** — both are required for EIA predictions.
> 2. Confusing *Pasquill-Gifford classes* with *Monin-Obukhov* (L). They are related but not identical — the former uses surface met, the latter uses flux measurements.
> 3. Forgetting that **class D (neutral) is the default** in regulatory modeling and is the conservative (high-concentration) case for most receptors.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023] (a) Derive an expression for the dry adiabatic lapse rate, explaining the underlying thermodynamic principles. (7 marks)**
**(b) A stack emits 30 m³/s of gas at 200 °C and 1.2 m diameter. Ambient temperature is 30 °C and wind speed is 5 m/s. Calculate the buoyancy flux, distance to final rise, and final plume rise. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**
*Derivation shown in Section 3.1 of these notes.*

The dry adiabatic lapse rate arises from the First Law of Thermodynamics applied to an adiabatic process combined with the hydrostatic equation. The key steps are:

- First Law: $dQ = C_p dT - dp/\rho = 0$ (adiabatic).
- Hydrostatic: $dp = -\rho g dz$.
- Substitute: $0 = C_p dT + g dz$, so $dT/dz = -g/C_p$.
- $\Gamma_d = g/C_p = 9.81/1004 = 9.8$ K/km.

**Physical meaning:** A rising unsaturated parcel expands and cools because it does work against the surrounding air. This is the **natural cooling rate**; comparing it with the actual ELR tells us whether the parcel will continue to rise (unstable), stop (neutral), or sink (stable).

**Model Solution to (b):**
Given: $V_s = 30$ m³/s, $T_s = 473$ K, $T_a = 303$ K, $d_s = 1.2$ m, $u = 5$ m/s.

**Buoyancy flux $F$:**

$$F = g V_s (d_s/2)^2 (T_s - T_a) / T_s$$

$$F = 9.81 \cdot 30 \cdot (0.6)^2 \cdot (473 - 303) / 473$$

$$F = 9.81 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.36 \cdot 170/473$$

$$F = 9.81 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.36 \cdot 0.3594 \approx 38.08 \text{ m}^4/\text{s}^3$$

**Distance to final rise $x_f$ (since F < 55):**

$$x_f = 49 \cdot F^{5/8} = 49 \cdot (38.08)^{5/8} = 49 \cdot 11.45 \approx 561.1 \text{ m}$$

**Final plume rise $\Delta h$ (neutral/unstable Briggs):**

$$F^{1/3} = (38.08)^{1/3} \approx 3.368$$

$$x_f^{2/3} = (561.1)^{2/3} \approx 68.06$$

$$\Delta h = 1.6 \cdot 3.368 \cdot 68.06 / 5 = 1.6 \cdot 229.2 / 5 \approx 73.3 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta h \approx 73.3 \text{ m}, \quad H_{eff} = H_s + 73.3 \text{ m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. Mixing up **DALR and SALR (Saturated ≈ 5.8 K/km)** — they are NOT the same; DALR is used for unsaturated rising parcels.
> 2. Forgetting to convert **°C to K** in the buoyancy-flux formula (T must be in Kelvin).
> 3. Using the wrong branch of Briggs' $x_f$ formula when $F \geq 55$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Atmospheric stability** is the single most important meteorological variable — it controls vertical dispersion.
- **DALR = 9.8 K/km**; compare with ELR to classify.
- **Pasquill classes A–F** — A = very unstable (max dispersion), F = very stable (minimum dispersion).
- **Temperature inversions** are the most hazardous stability condition for EIA. Always identify frequency, base height, and break-up time.
- **Wind speed** enters dispersion with inverse proportionality: $C_{max} \propto 1/u$ (for buoyant plumes).
- **Power-law exponent $p$** depends on stability: 0.07 (unstable) to 0.55 (stable).
- **Briggs plume rise** uses $F$ (buoyancy flux); for $F < 55$ use $x_f = 49 F^{5/8}$, for $F \geq 55$ use $x_f = 119 F^{2/5}$.
- **Effective stack height** $H_{eff} = H_s + \Delta h$ — directly feeds Gaussian dispersion models.
- **Mixing height** is the boundary-layer "ceiling" — daytime values of 1000–3000 m can collapse to 50–200 m at night.
- **Precipitation** removes pollutants via wet scavenging: $C(t) = C_0 e^{-\Lambda t}$.
- **Solar radiation** drives photochemistry (NO₂ → O₃) and is the input parameter for the Pasquill-Gifford scheme.
- **For an EIA report (KTU 2024):** baseline meteorology must include at least one full year of hourly data for wind, temperature, mixing height, stability class, and precipitation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification and Sources of Solid Waste
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification and Sources of Solid Waste

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Solid Waste?

**Solid Waste** refers to any discarded or abandoned material in solid or semi-solid form, resulting from residential, commercial, industrial, agricultural, mining, and municipal operations. According to the **Solid Waste Management and Handling Rules (SWM Rules), 2016** of India (Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change - MoEF&CC), solid waste includes materials that are no longer useful to the owner and are discarded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Standard Answer Format):**
> Solid waste is defined as any unwanted or discarded material that is not liquid or gaseous, generated from human activities, industrial processes, agricultural operations, or institutional activities, and possesses no immediate economic value to the generator.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your household dustbin at the end of the day. It contains vegetable peels, plastic wrappers, broken glass, old newspapers, and used tea bags. If you "zoom out" from this single bin to an entire city, every household's bin, every shop's waste, every hospital's discarded gloves, and every factory's scrap combine to form what we call **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** — a vast, complex stream of materials that needs systematic management.

Think of solid waste like a **river with many tributaries**:
- **Domestic waste** is the small stream starting at your home
- **Commercial waste** joins from markets and offices
- **Industrial waste** flows in as a heavy, sometimes toxic, current
- **Biomedical waste** adds a hazardous tributary
- All converge into the **municipal waste management system**, which must safely channel this combined flow without polluting the environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for KTU:**
> - **Generator** – The person/activity that produces waste (you, a hospital, a factory)
> - **Handler** – The entity that collects, transports, or processes waste (municipality, contractor)
> - **Disposal** – The final destination (sanitary landfill, incinerator, recycling center)

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Solid Waste

The following metrics are **universally used** in KTU-evaluated problems:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Per capita waste generation (India urban) | $q$ | **0.5 – 0.8** | kg/person/day |
| Bulk density of mixed MSW | $\rho_b$ | **300 – 500** | kg/m³ |
| Moisture content of MSW | $M$ | **40 – 60** | % by weight |
| Calorific value of mixed MSW | $CV$ | **1000 – 1500** | kcal/kg |
| Calorific value of paper waste | $CV_p$ | **3500 – 4000** | kcal/kg |
| Landfill compaction density | $\rho_c$ | **800 – 1000** | kg/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Questions on per capita waste generation, density calculations, and calorific value comparison between biodegradable and non-biodegradable wastes are **high-frequency topics**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Solid Waste — Hierarchical Logic

Solid waste can be classified using **four major criteria**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to understand **classification by source** and **classification by type** in detail.

### A. Classification by **Source of Generation**

This is the **most frequently tested classification** in KTU exams.

1. **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)**
   - Generated from households, streets, offices, schools, and commercial establishments.
   - Sub-types: residential, commercial, institutional, street sweeping, construction & demolition (C&D), garden waste.

2. **Industrial Solid Waste**
   - Generated from manufacturing, processing, refining, or construction industries.
   - Examples: slag, fly ash, red mud (bauxite residue), chemical sludge, scrap metal.

3. **Biomedical / Hospital Waste**
   - Generated from hospitals, clinics, pathology labs, blood banks, veterinary institutions.
   - Examples: human anatomical waste, sharps, soiled dressings, expired medicines, microbiological cultures.

4. **Agricultural Waste**
   - Generated from crop cultivation, animal husbandry, and agro-processing.
   - Examples: paddy straw, sugarcane bagasse, coconut husks, animal dung.

5. **Electronic Waste (E-Waste)**
   - Discarded electronic equipment: computers, mobile phones, TVs, refrigerators, batteries.

6. **Radioactive / Hazardous Waste**
   - Nuclear power plants, research labs, hospitals (radiotherapy).
   - Examples: spent fuel rods, contaminated gloves, radioactive liquids.

7. **Construction and Demolition (C&D) Waste**
   - Concrete, bricks, tiles, wood, metals, plastics from construction/demolition sites.

### B. Classification by **Type / Nature of Material**

| Category | Examples | Biodegradability |
|---|---|---|
| Organic / Biodegradable | Food waste, yard waste, paper, wood | High |
| Recyclable | Glass, metals, plastics, paper | Variable |
| Inert | Construction debris, rubble | None |
| Hazardous | Batteries, paints, chemicals, e-waste | None (toxic) |
| Composite | Tetra packs, laminated plastics | None |

### C. Classification by **Physical State**

- **Wet waste** (food, vegetables, animal waste) – High moisture, low calorific value
- **Dry waste** (paper, plastic, metal, glass) – Low moisture, high calorific value

### D. Classification by **Biodegradability**

- **Biodegradable** (organic, decomposable by microbes)
- **Non-biodegradable** (plastics, metals, glass)

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Classification

**Why classify?**
- Different waste types require **different treatment methods**.
- Hazardous waste cannot be mixed with biodegradable waste.
- Classification enables **resource recovery**, **safe disposal**, and **environmental impact assessment** (the core of this course).

**How is the classification useful?**
- **Engineering utility:** Helps in designing segregation systems at source (e.g., two-bin system — wet/dry).
- **Environmental utility:** Prevents leachate contamination of groundwater.
- **Economic utility:** Identifies recyclable streams with resale value.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **exam-grade formulas** for solving numerical problems in this topic.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = q \times P$ | Total waste generated = per capita generation × population | kg/day |
| 2 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{\rho_b}$ | Volume of waste = Mass ÷ Bulk density | m³/day |
| 3 | $M_w = Q \times \dfrac{M\%}{100}$ | Mass of moisture in waste | kg/day |
| 4 | $CV_{mix} = \sum (x_i \cdot CV_i)$ | Weighted average calorific value of mixed waste | kcal/kg |
| 5 | $R_c = \dfrac{V_c}{A_f}$ | Compaction ratio = Compacted volume ÷ Field volume | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\rho_c = \rho_b \times R_c$ | Compacted density | kg/m³ |
| 7 | $A_{LF} = \dfrac{Q \times t}{\rho_c \times H}$ | Landfill area required | m² |

Where:
- $Q$ = Total waste generation rate (kg/day)
- $q$ = Per capita waste generation (kg/person/day)
- $P$ = Population
- $\rho_b$ = Bulk density (kg/m³)
- $M\%$ = Moisture content percentage
- $x_i$ = Mass fraction of component $i$
- $CV_i$ = Calorific value of component $i$
- $t$ = Landfill life (days)
- $H$ = Landfill height (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always state units explicitly in your final answer. Marks are awarded for **correct units** in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of Core Solid Waste Equations

### Derivation 1: Total Municipal Solid Waste Generation

The fundamental principle is that **total waste generated by a city equals the sum of waste from each individual**, assuming uniform per capita generation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } & P = \text{Total population of the city (persons)} \\
            & q = \text{Per capita waste generation (kg/person/day)} \\
\text{Then total waste per day, } Q & = q \times P \quad \text{(kg/day)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example:**
A Kerala municipality has a population of **250,000**. If the per capita waste generation is **0.6 kg/person/day**, calculate the total MSW generated per day and per year.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{day} &= q \times P = 0.6 \times 250{,}000 = 150{,}000 \text{ kg/day} \\
        &= 150 \text{ tonnes/day} \\
Q_{year} &= 150 \times 365 = 54{,}750 \text{ tonnes/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion:** 1 tonne = 1000 kg. Always convert kg to tonnes before multiplying by 365 to avoid large numbers.

### Derivation 2: Volume of Waste Generated

Given the **bulk density** $\rho_b$, the volume occupied by the waste stream can be found:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \dfrac{Q}{\rho_b} \\
  &= \dfrac{150{,}000 \text{ kg/day}}{400 \text{ kg/m}^3} \quad (\text{taking } \rho_b = 400) \\
  &= 375 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Derivation 3: Weighted Average Calorific Value of Mixed Waste

The calorific value of mixed MSW is the **mass-weighted average** of the calorific values of individual components.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV_{mix} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i \cdot CV_i) \\
         &= x_1 CV_1 + x_2 CV_2 + \dots + x_n CV_n
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $x_i$ is the mass fraction of component $i$ (dimensionless, $\sum x_i = 1$).

**Worked Example:**
A municipal waste stream has the following composition:

| Component | Mass Fraction $x_i$ | Calorific Value $CV_i$ (kcal/kg) |
|---|---|---|
| Food waste | 0.40 | 1000 |
| Paper | 0.20 | 3500 |
| Plastic | 0.15 | 8000 |
| Textiles | 0.05 | 4000 |
| Glass & metal | 0.10 | 0 |
| Inert (rubble) | 0.10 | 0 |

Calculate the weighted average calorific value.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV_{mix} &= (0.40 \times 1000) + (0.20 \times 3500) + (0.15 \times 8000) \\
         &\quad + (0.05 \times 4000) + (0.10 \times 0) + (0.10 \times 0) \\
         &= 400 + 700 + 1200 + 200 + 0 + 0 \\
         &= 2500 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
Since $CV_{mix} = 2500$ kcal/kg, this waste is suitable for **incineration with energy recovery** (typically requires CV > 1500 kcal/kg).

### Derivation 4: Landfill Area Calculation

To find the area of land required for a sanitary landfill:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{LF} &= \dfrac{Q \times t}{\rho_c \times H} \\
       &= \dfrac{150{,}000 \text{ kg/day} \times 365 \text{ days}}{900 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 10 \text{ m}} \\
       &= \dfrac{54{,}750{,}000}{9000} \\
       &= 6083.33 \text{ m}^2 \approx 0.61 \text{ hectares}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Sources vs. Classification Criteria

| Source | Major Type | Biodegradable? | Hazardous? | Typical Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Household kitchen | Organic | Yes | No | Composting, biogas |
| Household packaging | Plastic, paper | No (mostly) | No | Recycling |
| Hospital waste | Biomedical | Variable | **Yes** | Autoclaving, incineration |
| E-waste | Electronic | No | **Yes** | Specialized recycling |
| Construction site | Concrete, wood | No | No | Reuse, crushing |
| Agricultural field | Crop residue | Yes | No | Composting, mulching |
| Nuclear plant | Radioactive | No | **Highly** | Deep geological storage |
| Battery industry | Lead, chemicals | No | **Yes** | Secured landfill |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification Tree of Solid Waste

```mermaid
graph TD
    SW[SolSW: SOLID WASTE] --> SRC[SolSRC: By Source]
    SW --> TYP[SolTYP: By Type]
    SW --> PHY[SolPHY: By Physical State]
    SW --> BIO[SolBIO: By Biodegradability]

    SRC --> MSW[SolMSW: Municipal SW]
    SRC --> ISW[SolISW: Industrial SW]
    SRC --> BMW[SolBMW: Biomedical SW]
    SRC --> AGW[SolAGW: Agricultural SW]
    SRC --> EWS[SolEWS: E-Waste]
    SRC --> RAW[SolRAW: Radioactive Waste]
    SRC --> CDS[SolCDS: C and D Waste]

    MSW --> DOM[SolDOM: Domestic]
    MSW --> COM[SolCOM: Commercial]
    MSW --> INS[SolINS: Institutional]

    TYP --> ORG[SolORG: Organic]
    TYP --> REC[SolREC: Recyclable]
    TYP --> INR[SolINR: Inert]
    TYP --> HAZ[SolHAZ: Hazardous]
    TYP --> CMP[SolCMP: Composite]

    PHY --> WET[SolWET: Wet Waste]
    PHY --> DRY[SolDRY: Dry Waste]

    BIO --> BIOY[SolBIOY: Biodegradable]
    BIO --> BION[SolBION: Non-Biodegradable]

    BMW --> SHAR[SolSHAR: Sharps]
    BMW --> INF[SolINF: Infectious]
    BMW --> PATH[SolPATH: Pathological]
    BMW --> PHAR[SolPHAR: Pharmaceutical]
    BMW --> CHEM[SolCHEM: Chemical]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Waste-to-Resource Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[SolGEN: WASTE GENERATION] --> B[SolSEG: SOURCE SEGREGATION]
    B --> C1[SolWET: WET WASTE STREAM]
    B --> C2[SolDRY: DRY WASTE STREAM]
    B --> C3[SolHAZ: HAZARDOUS WASTE STREAM]

    C1 --> D1[SolCOMP: COMPOSTING]
    C1 --> D2[SolBIOG: BIOGAS PLANT]

    C2 --> E1[SolREC1: MATERIAL RECOVERY FACILITY]
    C2 --> E2[SolINC: INCINERATION]

    C3 --> F1[SolTSDF: TREATMENT STORAGE AND DISPOSAL FACILITY]
    C3 --> F2[SolSEC: SECURED LANDFILL]

    D1 --> OUT1[SolOUT1: ORGANIC MANURE]
    D2 --> OUT2[SolOUT2: ENERGY AND DIGESTATE]
    E1 --> OUT3[SolOUT3: RECYCLED RAW MATERIALS]
    E2 --> OUT4[SolOUT4: ELECTRICITY AND ASH]
    F2 --> OUT5[SolOUT5: CONTAINED RESIDUE]
```

## 4.3 Composition of Typical Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) in India

```mermaid
graph TD
    MSW[SolMSW: 100 PERCENT MSW COMPOSITION INDIA]
    MSW --> A[SolA: ORGANIC 40 to 60 PERCENT]
    MSW --> B[SolB: RECYCLABLE 20 to 30 PERCENT]
    MSW --> C[SolC: INERT 10 to 15 PERCENT]
    MSW --> D[SolD: HAZARDOUS 1 to 5 PERCENT]

    A --> A1[SolA1: Food waste]
    A --> A2[SolA2: Yard waste]
    A --> A3[SolA3: Paper soiled]

    B --> B1[SolB1: Paper clean]
    B --> B2[SolB2: Plastic]
    B --> B3[SolB3: Glass]
    B --> B4[SolB4: Metal]

    C --> C1[SolC1: Construction debris]
    C --> C2[SolC2: Stones and sand]

    D --> D1[SolD1: Batteries]
    D --> D2[SolD2: Paint cans]
    D --> D3[SolD3: E-waste]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define solid waste. List any four sources of solid waste.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Solid waste is any unwanted or discarded material in solid, semi-solid, or liquid form (excluding sewage and gaseous emissions) that is generated from human activities and is not immediately reused.

**Four sources of solid waste (1 mark each for naming, brief context):**
1. **Residential/Domestic** – Waste from households (food waste, paper, plastic).
2. **Industrial** – Waste from factories (slag, scrap metal, chemical sludge).
3. **Biomedical** – Waste from hospitals (sharps, anatomical waste, soiled dressings).
4. **Construction and Demolition (C&D)** – Waste from construction sites (concrete, bricks, wood).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]`
**Differentiate between biodegradable and non-biodegradable solid waste with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Biodegradable | Non-Biodegradable |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Decomposed by microorganisms in a reasonable time | Does not decompose naturally |
| Decomposition time | Days to months | Decades to centuries |
| Examples | Food waste, paper, yard waste, cow dung | Plastic, glass, metal, e-waste |
| Treatment | Composting, biogas, vermicomposting | Recycling, incineration, landfill |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Classify solid waste based on (i) source of generation and (ii) type of material. Give suitable examples for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A city has a population of **500,000**. Per capita waste generation is **0.55 kg/person/day** and bulk density is **450 kg/m³**. Calculate (i) total waste generated per day in tonnes, and (ii) volume of waste generated per day in m³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification (7 marks):**

**(i) By Source of Generation (3.5 marks):**
- **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW):** From households, offices, streets. Example: food waste, paper.
- **Industrial Solid Waste:** From manufacturing units. Example: fly ash, slag.
- **Biomedical Waste:** From hospitals and labs. Example: human tissues, syringes.
- **Agricultural Waste:** From farms. Example: paddy straw, coconut husks.
- **E-Waste:** From discarded electronics. Example: mobile phones, laptops.
- **Construction & Demolition (C&D) Waste:** From construction sites. Example: concrete rubble, wood.

**(ii) By Type of Material (3.5 marks):**
- **Organic/Biodegradable:** Food, yard waste, paper — decomposable naturally.
- **Recyclable:** Glass, plastic, metal, clean paper — can be reprocessed.
- **Inert:** Construction debris, sand — does not react.
- **Hazardous:** Batteries, chemicals, paints — toxic and reactive.
- **Composite:** Tetra packs, multi-layered plastics — require specialized recycling.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Population $P = 500{,}000$ persons
- Per capita waste $q = 0.55$ kg/person/day
- Bulk density $\rho_b = 450$ kg/m³

**(i) Total waste per day (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= q \times P \\
  &= 0.55 \times 500{,}000 \\
  &= 275{,}000 \text{ kg/day} \\
  &= \dfrac{275{,}000}{1000} = 275 \text{ tonnes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Volume of waste per day (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \dfrac{Q}{\rho_b} \\
  &= \dfrac{275{,}000 \text{ kg/day}}{450 \text{ kg/m}^3} \\
  &= 611.11 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $Q = 275$ tonnes/day; $V \approx 611.11$ m³/day.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]`
**(a)** Explain the major sources of biomedical waste and the colour-coded segregation system prescribed by the **BMW Management Rules, 2016**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The composition of MSW from a locality is: food waste 45%, paper 18%, plastic 12%, textile 5%, glass 5%, metal 3%, inert 12%. The calorific values (kcal/kg) are: food 1000, paper 3500, plastic 8000, textile 4000, glass 0, metal 0, inert 0. Compute the weighted average calorific value of the mixed MSW. Comment on its suitability for incineration with energy recovery. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Biomedical Waste Sources and Segregation (7 marks):**

**Major Sources of Biomedical Waste (3 marks):**
- Hospitals and nursing homes
- Clinics and primary health centers
- Pathology labs and diagnostic centers
- Blood banks
- Veterinary institutions
- Research laboratories
- Funeral homes

**BMW Segregation Categories as per BMW Rules, 2016 (4 marks):**

| Category | Type of Waste | Colour of Bin | Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Yellow | Anatomical, soiled, chemical waste | Yellow | Incineration |
| Red | Contaminated recyclable waste (tubing, bottles) | Red | Autoclaving/Microwaving |
| White (Sharp container) | Sharps (needles, scalpels, blades) | White | Autoclaving/Destruction |
| Blue | Glassware (broken, contaminated) | Blue | Autoclaving/Recycling |

---

**(b) Calorific Value Calculation (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV_{mix} &= \sum x_i \cdot CV_i \\
         &= (0.45 \times 1000) + (0.18 \times 3500) + (0.12 \times 8000) \\
         &\quad + (0.05 \times 4000) + (0.05 \times 0) + (0.03 \times 0) + (0.12 \times 0) \\
         &= 450 + 630 + 960 + 200 + 0 + 0 + 0 \\
         &= 2240 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
Since $CV_{mix} = 2240$ kcal/kg > 1500 kcal/kg (typical threshold), this waste is **suitable for incineration with energy recovery**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse BMW rules year** — Students often write "1998" instead of the updated **2016** rules. The 2016 rules revised colour coding (white is sharps, not blue). Marks deducted: **1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversion** in numerical problems (kg/day → tonnes/day). Always show the conversion step.
> 3. **Not verifying mass fractions sum to 1.** If $\sum x_i \neq 1$, the answer is wrong even if arithmetic is correct. Marks deducted: **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Missing the engineering comment** after a numerical problem. KTU values interpretation; a numerical answer without comment loses **1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- **Definition:** Solid waste = any discarded solid/semi-solid material with no immediate economic value to the generator.
- **Sources:** MSW, Industrial, Biomedical, Agricultural, E-waste, Radioactive, C&D.
- **Classification types:** By source, by material type, by physical state, by biodegradability.
- **BMW Rules 2016:** Yellow (incineration), Red (autoclaving), White (sharps), Blue (glassware).
- **SWM Rules 2016:** Govern MSW management; emphasize segregation at source.
- **Key formulas:** $Q = q \times P$, $V = Q/\rho_b$, $CV_{mix} = \sum x_i CV_i$, $A_{LF} = Qt/(\rho_c H)$.
- **Standard values to memorize:** Per capita waste = **0.5–0.8 kg/person/day**; Bulk density = **300–500 kg/m³**; Moisture content = **40–60%**; Calorific value of mixed MSW = **1000–1500 kcal/kg**.
- **Comparison points:** Wet waste → composting/biogas; Dry waste → recycling/incineration; Hazardous waste → TSDF.
- **Incineration threshold:** $CV_{mix} > 1500$ kcal/kg → suitable for energy recovery.
- **Engineering context:** Classification drives segregation, treatment selection, and landfill design — core pillars of Environmental Impact Assessment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Solid Waste
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Solid Waste

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid Waste Characteristics** refer to the quantitative and qualitative physical, chemical, and biological properties of waste materials that determine their behavior during collection, transportation, processing, treatment, and final disposal. These characteristics form the **fundamental design basis** for every component of an Integrated Solid Waste Management (ISWM) system.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECHT617)**, the study of solid waste characteristics is treated as the **baseline data layer** of any Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA). Without accurate characterization, no predictive model, landfill design, incinerator sizing, or composting facility can be reliably evaluated for its environmental burden.

### The Three Pillars of Waste Characterization

| Pillar | What it Measures | Why EIA Needs It |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical** | Density, moisture, particle size | Vehicle sizing, landfill compaction, leachate volume |
| **Chemical** | Calorific value, C/N ratio, pH, proximate/ultimate analysis | Combustion efficiency, biogas yield, toxicity |
| **Biological** | Biodegradability, microbial activity | Composting potential, methane generation rate |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Waste Fingerprint"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) as the "fingerprint" of a community.**

Just as every person has a unique fingerprint, every city, town, or village produces waste with a **unique signature** dictated by its:
- **Economic status** (richer areas → more paper, plastics, e-waste)
- **Climate** (humid regions → higher moisture content, more organic fraction)
- **Dietary habits** (vegetarian vs. non-vegetarian → organic waste variation)
- **Season** (festivals → spikes in specific waste streams)

**Analogy:** A doctor cannot prescribe medicine without a **blood test report**. Similarly, an environmental engineer cannot design a sanitary landfill, incinerator, or composting plant without first obtaining the **waste characteristics report**. The characteristics ARE the blood test of solid waste management.

---

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus-Critical Parameters to Memorize:**
> - **Bulk Density** of MSW (uncompacted): **300 to 500 kg/m³**
> - **Bulk Density** of MSW (compacted in landfill): **600 to 900 kg/m³**
> - **Moisture Content** of MSW: **20% to 60%** (by weight)
> - **Calorific Value** of MSW: **800 to 2000 kcal/kg** (≈ 3.35 to 8.37 MJ/kg)
> - **C/N Ratio** for composting: **25:1 to 35:1** (optimal)
> - **pH** of fresh MSW: **5 to 7** (slightly acidic)
> - **Volatile Solids (VS)** in MSW: **40% to 70%**

These ranges are **bread-and-butter** for KTU exams — examiners love to ask students to interpret or calculate based on these values.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Characteristics — Detailed Breakdown

Physical characteristics describe **how the waste looks, weighs, flows, and settles**. They govern the **mechanical handling** of waste.

### 2.1.1 Bulk Density ($\rho_b$)

Bulk density is the **mass of waste per unit volume** as it exists in the collection container or landfill (including voids).

$$\rho_b = \frac{M}{V} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \right]$$

- **Why it matters:** Determines truck capacity, bin size, landfill airspace consumption.
- **Typical uncompacted MSW:** 300–500 kg/m³
- **Typical compacted MSW:** 600–900 kg/m³ (achieved by landfill compactors)

### 2.1.2 Moisture Content ($M_c$)

The fraction of water present in waste, expressed as a percentage of the **wet weight**.

$$M_c = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 \; [\%]$$

Where:
- $W_w$ = Wet weight of waste sample (kg)
- $W_d$ = Dry weight after oven-drying at **105°C** (kg)

- **Why it matters:** High moisture reduces calorific value, increases leachate generation, and complicates incineration.

### 2.1.3 Particle Size Distribution

Determined by **sieve analysis**. Affects:
- Leachate migration velocity in landfills
- Compaction efficiency
- Wind-blown litter potential (fines)

### 2.1.4 Specific Weight & Field Capacity

- **Specific weight** of waste components (e.g., food waste ≈ 1000 kg/m³, paper ≈ 700 kg/m³, glass ≈ 1500 kg/m³).
- **Field capacity**: Maximum moisture a waste layer can hold against gravity — critical for landfill cover design.

---

## 2.2 Chemical Characteristics — Detailed Breakdown

Chemical characteristics dictate **how the waste reacts** during treatment processes.

### 2.2.1 Proximate Analysis

Determines four fractions (all on **dry basis**):

| Fraction | Definition | Typical MSW Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Moisture** | Water content (lost at 105°C) | 20–60% |
| **Volatile Matter (VM)** | Combustible gases released at 950°C | 40–70% |
| **Fixed Carbon (FC)** | Residual solid carbon | 5–15% |
| **Ash** | Non-combustible residue | 10–30% |

### 2.2.2 Ultimate (Elemental) Analysis

Determines elemental composition: **C, H, O, N, S, Cl** (on dry, ash-free basis).

$$C + H + O + N + S + \text{Cl} + \text{Ash} = 100\%$$

- **Significance:** Used in stoichiometric calculations for incinerator air supply.

### 2.2.3 Calorific Value (CV)

Energy released upon **complete combustion** of unit mass of waste.

$$CV_{\text{net}} = CV_{\text{gross}} - 9 \times H \times \left( \frac{2440 - T}{1000} \right)$$

Where:
- $CV_{\text{net}}$ = Net calorific value (kcal/kg)
- $CV_{\text{gross}}$ = Gross calorific value (kcal/kg)
- $H$ = Hydrogen content (% by weight)
- $T$ = Reference temperature (°C)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 9 × H × 2440/1000 factor** accounts for the **latent heat of vaporization of water** produced during combustion of hydrogen. This is a **highly tested formula** in KTU exams.

### 2.2.4 pH

- Fresh MSW: **pH 5–7** (slightly acidic due to organic acid fermentation)
- Stabilized/old MSW: **pH 7–8** (alkaline)
- **Significance:** Influences heavy metal leaching, microbial activity.

### 2.2.5 C/N Ratio (Carbon-to-Nitrogen Ratio)

$$\frac{C}{N} = \frac{\text{Total Carbon content}}{\text{Total Nitrogen content}}$$

- **Optimal for composting:** 25:1 to 35:1
- **Too high C/N:** Slow composting (nitrogen limitation)
- **Too low C/N:** Ammonia release, odor problems

---

## 2.3 Biological Characteristics

### 2.3.1 Biodegradability

Fraction of waste that can be decomposed by microorganisms. Measured as:
$$\text{Biodeg.~Fraction} = \frac{VS_{\text{initial}} - VS_{\text{final}}}{VS_{\text{initial}}} \times 100 \; [\%]$$

### 2.3.2 Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) & Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)

- **BOD₅ of MSW leachate:** 5,000 to 30,000 mg/L (extremely high)
- **COD of MSW leachate:** 10,000 to 60,000 mg/L

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Landfill leachate is among the most polluted wastewaters on Earth** — its BOD/COD is roughly **100× stronger than domestic sewage**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Typical MSW Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bulk Density | $\rho_b = M / V$ | kg/m³ | 300–900 |
| 2 | Moisture Content | $M_c = (W_w - W_d) / W_w \times 100$ | % | 20–60 |
| 3 | Volatile Solids | $VS = (W_d - W_{ash}) / W_d \times 100$ | % | 40–70 |
| 4 | Calorific Value (Dulong) | $CV = 81.4C + 342.5(H - O/8) + 22.5S$ | kcal/kg | 800–2000 |
| 5 | Net CV | $CV_n = CV_g - 9H \cdot (2440 - T)/1000$ | kcal/kg | — |
| 6 | C/N Ratio | $C/N = \text{Carbon} / \text{Nitrogen}$ | dimensionless | 25:1–35:1 |
| 7 | Air Requirement (Stoichiometric) | $A = (11.6C + 34.8(H - O/8) + 4.35S)/23.2$ | kg air/kg waste | 4–7 |
| 8 | Biodegradability | $BD = (VS_i - VS_f)/VS_i \times 100$ | % | 40–60 |
| 9 | pH | $-\log[H^+]$ | dimensionless | 5–8 |
| 10 | Refuse Density (Landfill) | $\rho = M_{waste} / V_{landfill}$ | kg/m³ | 500–1000 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Dulong's Formula (Row 4)** is the most tested empirical equation in KTU exams for determining calorific value from ultimate analysis. **C, H, O, S are in percentage (dry, ash-free basis).**

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Landfill Engineering** | Density dictates airspace, settlement, leachate head |
| **Incineration Plants** | CV and proximate analysis dictate combustion air, grate design |
| **Composting Plants** | C/N ratio, moisture, and pH dictate biological kinetics |
| **Biogas Plants** | VS content predicts methane yield ($Y_{CH_4} \approx 0.35 \, m^3/kg\,VS$) |
| **EIA Studies** | Waste characterization data is **mandatory input** for impact prediction |
| **Transportation Planning** | Density and moisture determine truck type, route, and frequency |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Moisture Content on Wet vs. Dry Basis

### Given
- $W_w$ = Wet mass of waste sample
- $W_d$ = Mass after drying at 105°C to constant weight

### Step 1 — Define water mass
The water lost during drying is the difference between wet and dry mass:
$$M_{water} = W_w - W_d$$

### Step 2 — Express on wet basis (standard KTU form)
$$M_c \, (\text{wet}) = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 \; [\%]$$

### Step 3 — Convert to dry basis (sometimes required)
$$M_c \, (\text{dry}) = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 \; [\%]$$

### Step 4 — Relationship between the two
$$\frac{M_c^{dry}}{M_c^{wet}} = \frac{W_w}{W_d}$$

### Numerical Example (KTU style)
A waste sample weighs **2.500 kg** when fresh. After oven-drying at 105°C, the weight becomes **1.150 kg**. Calculate moisture content on both bases.

**Solution:**

$$M_c^{wet} = \frac{2.500 - 1.150}{2.500} \times 100 = \frac{1.350}{2.500} \times 100 = 54.0\%$$

$$M_c^{dry} = \frac{2.500 - 1.150}{1.150} \times 100 = \frac{1.350}{1.150} \times 100 = 117.39\%$$

**Interpretation:** A moisture content of 54% means the waste is **highly biodegradable** (food/yard waste category). It will produce significant leachate and has **low calorific value**, ruling out efficient incineration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Calorific Value from Dulong's Formula

### Step 1 — Start with Dulong's empirical equation
$$CV = 81.4 \cdot C + 342.5 \cdot \left( H - \frac{O}{8} \right) + 22.5 \cdot S \quad [\text{kcal/kg}]$$

Where C, H, O, S are **percentage weights** of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and sulfur (dry, ash-free basis).

### Step 2 — Explain the physics
- **81.4 × C** : Energy released per kg of carbon (≈ 8100 kcal/kg C)
- **342.5 × H** : Energy from hydrogen, **but** hydrogen must first burn to form water
- **−342.5 × O/8** : Oxygen already present in waste reduces the net air needed (and thus energy)
- **22.5 × S** : Small contribution from sulfur combustion

### Step 3 — Apply to a real MSW sample

**Given Ultimate Analysis (dry, ash-free):**
- C = 45%, H = 6%, O = 35%, N = 1.5%, S = 0.5%, Ash = 12%

**Step-by-step calculation:**

$$CV = 81.4 \times 45 + 342.5 \times \left( 6 - \frac{35}{8} \right) + 22.5 \times 0.5$$

$$CV = 81.4 \times 45 + 342.5 \times (6 - 4.375) + 22.5 \times 0.5$$

$$CV = 3663 + 342.5 \times 1.625 + 11.25$$

$$CV = 3663 + 556.56 + 11.25$$

$$CV = 4230.81 \;\; \text{kcal/kg}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Net Calorific Value (NCV)
Assume reference temperature $T = 25°C$ and hydrogen content H = 6%:

$$CV_n = CV_g - 9 \times H \times \left( \frac{2440 - T}{1000} \right)$$

$$CV_n = 4230.81 - 9 \times 6 \times \left( \frac{2440 - 25}{1000} \right)$$

$$CV_n = 4230.81 - 54 \times 2.415$$

$$CV_n = 4230.81 - 130.41 = 4100.40 \;\; \text{kcal/kg}$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation
This NCV of **~4100 kcal/kg** is **high** for MSW (typical range 800–2000 kcal/kg), suggesting the waste is **dry, organic-poor, and suitable for incineration with energy recovery**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Requirement

### Step 1 — Combustion reactions
For a unit mass of waste containing C, H, O, S:

$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad (1 \, \text{kg C needs } 32/12 = 2.667 \, \text{kg O}_2)$$

$$\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (1 \, \text{kg H needs } 16/2 = 8 \, \text{kg O}_2)$$

$$\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 \quad (1 \, \text{kg S needs } 32/32 = 1 \, \text{kg O}_2)$$

### Step 2 — Total O₂ required
$$O_2^{req} = \frac{32}{12}C + \frac{16}{2}\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + \frac{32}{32}S$$

$$O_2^{req} = 2.67C + 8\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 1.0S$$

### Step 3 — Convert O₂ to air
Air contains **23.2% O₂ by mass**. So:

$$A_{stoich} = \frac{O_2^{req}}{0.232}$$

$$A_{stoich} = \frac{2.67C + 8(H - O/8) + S}{0.232} \;\; \text{kg air/kg waste}$$

Or equivalently, the form often given in KTU textbooks:

$$A_{stoich} = \frac{11.6C + 34.8(H - O/8) + 4.35S}{23.2} \;\; \text{kg air/kg waste}$$

### Step 4 — Worked example
Using the same MSW: C = 45, H = 6, O = 35, S = 0.5

$$A = \frac{11.6 \times 45 + 34.8 \times (6 - 35/8) + 4.35 \times 0.5}{23.2}$$

$$A = \frac{522 + 34.8 \times 1.625 + 2.175}{23.2}$$

$$A = \frac{522 + 56.55 + 2.175}{23.2} = \frac{580.725}{23.2}$$

$$A = 25.03 \;\; \text{kg air/kg waste (stoichiometric minimum)}$$

For complete combustion in practice, **150–200% excess air** is supplied, giving actual air supply ≈ **37.5 to 50 kg/kg waste**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Waste Categories

| Characteristic | Food/Yard Waste | Paper | Plastics | Glass/Metal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Density (kg/m³)** | 300–500 | 80–150 | 30–130 | 1500–2000 |
| **Moisture (%)** | 50–80 | 4–10 | <2 | <2 |
| **Volatile Solids (%)** | 70–90 | 80–90 | 95 | <5 |
| **Calorific Value (kcal/kg)** | 400–1000 | 3500–4500 | 7000–10000 | ~0 |
| **Biodegradability** | High | Medium-High | None | None |
| **C/N Ratio** | 15:1 | 170:1 | — | — |
| **Recyclability** | Composting | High | High | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The high C/N ratio of paper (170:1) means it cannot be composted **alone** — it must be **co-composted** with nitrogen-rich food waste to bring the ratio down to the optimal 25:1–35:1 range.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Diagram — Characteristics of Solid Waste

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CHARACTERISTICS OF SOLID WASTE]:::root
    A --> B[PHYSICAL]:::branch
    A --> C[CHEMICAL]:::branch
    A --> D[BIOLOGICAL]:::branch
    A --> E[MECHANICAL]:::branch

    B --> B1[Bulk Density]
    B --> B2[Moisture Content]
    B --> B3[Particle Size]
    B --> B4[Specific Weight]
    B --> B5[Field Capacity]

    C --> C1[Proximate Analysis]
    C --> C2[Ultimate Analysis]
    C --> C3[Calorific Value]
    C --> C4[pH Value]
    C --> C5[CN Ratio]

    C1 --> C1a[Moisture]
    C1 --> C1b[Volatile Matter]
    C1 --> C1c[Fixed Carbon]
    C1 --> C1d[Ash Content]

    C2 --> C2a[Carbon]
    C2 --> C2b[Hydrogen]
    C2 --> C2c[Oxygen]
    C2 --> C2d[Nitrogen]
    C2 --> C2e[Sulfur]

    D --> D1[Biodegradability]
    D --> D2[BOD of Leachate]
    D --> D3[COD of Leachate]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the following with respect to solid waste characterization:**
**(a)** Bulk Density
**(b)** Moisture Content
**(c)** Volatile Solids

### Model Answer:

> **(a) Bulk Density:** It is defined as the mass of solid waste per unit volume, **including the voids** between waste particles, as it exists in the collection container or landfill. It governs truck capacity, bin sizing, and landfill airspace.
> $$\rho_b = \frac{M}{V} \;\; [\text{kg/m}^3]$$
> Typical MSW: **300–500 kg/m³** (uncompacted) and **600–900 kg/m³** (compacted).

> **(b) Moisture Content:** It is the weight of water present in the waste expressed as a percentage of the wet weight. The waste is oven-dried at **105°C** to constant weight.
> $$M_c = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 \; [\%]$$
> Typical MSW: **20% to 60%**.

> **(c) Volatile Solids (VS):** The fraction of dry solids that is combusted (and lost) when the waste is ignited at **950°C**. It represents the organic/biodegradable content of the waste.
> $$VS = \frac{W_d - W_{ash}}{W_d} \times 100 \; [\%]$$
> Typical MSW: **40% to 70%**.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the significance of C/N ratio and pH in the composting of municipal solid waste.**

### Model Answer:

> **C/N Ratio (Carbon-to-Nitrogen Ratio):** It is the ratio of total carbon to total nitrogen in the waste.
> - **Optimal Range:** 25:1 to 35:1
> - **High C/N (e.g., paper at 170:1):** Nitrogen becomes the limiting nutrient; composting slows down.
> - **Low C/N (e.g., food waste at 15:1):** Excess nitrogen is released as **ammonia (NH₃)**, causing odor and nitrogen loss.
> - **Significance:** Determines whether the microbial population has balanced nutrition for rapid decomposition.

> **pH Value:**
> - **Fresh MSW:** pH 5–7 (acidic, due to organic acid formation)
> - **Mature Compost:** pH 7–8 (slightly alkaline)
> - **Significance:** pH affects microbial enzyme activity. Most composting microbes prefer **pH 6.5–8.0**. Acidic pH inhibits nitrification; alkaline pH promotes ammonia volatilization.
> - **Control:** pH is monitored and adjusted using lime (to raise) or elemental sulfur (to lower).

---

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Module 2 contains **one full 14-mark question** with internal choice. The question typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Below are **TWO alternative choices (Q1A & Q1B)** — answer any ONE.

---

### QUESTION 1A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **physical characteristics** of municipal solid waste in detail. Explain the significance of bulk density and moisture content in the design of solid waste management systems.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A waste sample has the following ultimate analysis (dry, ash-free basis):
- C = 42%, H = 5.5%, O = 38%, N = 1.2%, S = 0.3%, Ash = 13%
- Moisture content = 45%
- Bulk density = 350 kg/m³

Calculate the **gross calorific value** using Dulong's formula. Also estimate the **stoichiometric air requirement** per kg of waste.

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Physical Characteristics

The physical characteristics of MSW include:

**1. Bulk Density ($\rho_b$):**
- Definition: Mass per unit volume **as collected**, including voids.
- Formula: $\rho_b = M/V$
- Typical values: 300–500 kg/m³ (loose), 600–900 kg/m³ (compacted)
- **Design Significance:**
  - Determines **compactor truck capacity** and collection frequency.
  - Governs **landfill airspace** consumption and **settlement** behavior.
  - Used to calculate **landfill lifetime**: $T = V_{landfill} / (\rho_b \times \text{daily waste})$

**2. Moisture Content ($M_c$):**
- Definition: Water content as a percentage of wet weight.
- Formula: $M_c = (W_w - W_d)/W_w \times 100$
- **Design Significance:**
  - High moisture (>40%) → **leachate generation** is high → landfill needs robust liner.
  - High moisture → **low calorific value** → incineration becomes energy-deficient.
  - For composting, moisture must be maintained at **50–60%** for microbial activity.

**3. Particle Size:**
- Determined by sieve analysis; affects **compaction, permeability, and wind-blown litter**.

**4. Field Capacity:**
- Maximum water content a waste layer can retain against gravity.
- Critical for **leachate recirculation systems** in bioreactor landfills.

**5. Specific Weight & Settling Characteristics:**
- Determines landfill slope stability and structural integrity.

### Part (b) — Calorific Value & Air Requirement

**Step 1: Apply Dulong's Formula**

$$CV = 81.4C + 342.5\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 22.5S$$

Substituting C = 42, H = 5.5, O = 38, S = 0.3:

$$CV = 81.4 \times 42 + 342.5 \times \left(5.5 - \frac{38}{8}\right) + 22.5 \times 0.3$$

$$CV = 3418.8 + 342.5 \times (5.5 - 4.75) + 6.75$$

$$CV = 3418.8 + 342.5 \times 0.75 + 6.75$$

$$CV = 3418.8 + 256.875 + 6.75$$

$$\boxed{CV_{gross} = 3682.425 \;\; \text{kcal/kg (dry, ash-free basis)}}$$

**Step 2: Adjust for Moisture and Ash (to get "as-received" CV)**

Effective heating value of the **as-received** waste:

$$CV_{ar} = CV_{gross} \times \left(1 - \frac{M_c}{100}\right) \times \left(1 - \frac{Ash}{100}\right)$$

$$CV_{ar} = 3682.425 \times (1 - 0.45) \times (1 - 0.13)$$

$$CV_{ar} = 3682.425 \times 0.55 \times 0.87$$

$$\boxed{CV_{ar} \approx 1762.2 \;\; \text{kcal/kg}}$$

**Step 3: Stoichiometric Air Requirement**

$$A_{stoich} = \frac{11.6C + 34.8(H - O/8) + 4.35S}{23.2}$$

$$A = \frac{11.6 \times 42 + 34.8 \times (5.5 - 4.75) + 4.35 \times 0.3}{23.2}$$

$$A = \frac{487.2 + 34.8 \times 0.75 + 1.305}{23.2}$$

$$A = \frac{487.2 + 26.1 + 1.305}{23.2} = \frac{514.605}{23.2}$$

$$\boxed{A_{stoich} \approx 22.18 \;\; \text{kg air/kg waste (dry, ash-free basis)}}$$

For real combustion, **150% excess air** is used:
$$A_{actual} = 1.5 \times 22.18 = 33.27 \;\; \text{kg air/kg waste}$$

**Conclusion:** The waste has a moderate calorific value (~1760 kcal/kg as received) and requires about **33 kg of air per kg of waste** for complete combustion — suitable for **mass-burn incineration with energy recovery**.

---

### QUESTION 1B: [Alternative Choice — KTU 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **chemical characteristics** of solid waste with reference to proximate and ultimate analysis. Why is calorific value considered the most important chemical parameter?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A composting plant receives 50 tonnes/day of mixed waste with the following composition:
- Food waste: 40% (C/N = 15:1)
- Yard waste: 20% (C/N = 30:1)
- Paper: 30% (C/N = 170:1)
- Others: 10% (C/N = 50:1)

Calculate the **overall C/N ratio** of the mix. Is the mixture suitable for direct composting? Justify.

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Chemical Characteristics

Chemical characterization of solid waste comprises:

**1. Proximate Analysis (4 components):**
- **Moisture** (lost at 105°C)
- **Volatile Matter (VM)** (lost at 950°C in inert atmosphere)
- **Fixed Carbon (FC)** (residual combustible solid)
- **Ash** (non-combustible residue at 950°C)

**2. Ultimate (Elemental) Analysis:**
- Determines percentages of **C, H, O, N, S, Cl** (dry, ash-free basis)
- Required for **stoichiometric calculations** in incinerators.

**3. Calorific Value — Why Most Important?**
- Determines **energy recovery potential** of waste.
- Dictates **choice of treatment**: 
  - CV < 1000 kcal/kg → composting / landfill
  - CV 1000–1500 kcal/kg → co-incineration with coal
  - CV > 1500 kcal/kg → standalone incineration with energy recovery
- Governs **autogenous combustion** (self-sustaining burn threshold ≈ 1500 kcal/kg).
- Critical input for **Emission Prediction Models** in EIA (CO₂, NOx, dioxin formation all depend on CV and ultimate analysis).

**4. pH, C/N Ratio, and Other Chemical Parameters:**
- Influence biological treatment kinetics and leachate chemistry.

### Part (b) — C/N Ratio Calculation

**Step 1: Weighted Average C/N Ratio**

For each component, calculate its contribution based on mass fraction:

$$(C/N)_{mix} = \frac{\sum (m_i \times C_i)}{\sum (m_i \times N_i)}$$

Or equivalently, weighted by fraction assuming each material has a different absolute C and N content. For a **mixing problem**, we use:

$$(C/N)_{mix} = \frac{\sum f_i \times C_i}{\sum f_i \times (C_i / (C/N)_i)}$$

**Simpler practical approach (assuming equal-basis contribution):**

$$(C/N)_{mix} = \frac{\sum f_i \times (C/N)_i}{\sum f_i}$$

But this is only valid if all fractions have similar total solid content. For a more accurate weighted average, assume representative carbon content per fraction:

| Component | Mass Fraction ($f_i$) | C/N Ratio | C content (assumed, %TS) | N content (%, C/(C/N)) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Food waste | 0.40 | 15:1 | 45 | 3.00 |
| Yard waste | 0.20 | 30:1 | 48 | 1.60 |
| Paper | 0.30 | 170:1 | 44 | 0.259 |
| Others | 0.10 | 50:1 | 40 | 0.80 |

**Step 2: Calculate weighted C and N**

$$C_{mix} = 0.40 \times 45 + 0.20 \times 48 + 0.30 \times 44 + 0.10 \times 40$$

$$C_{mix} = 18.0 + 9.6 + 13.2 + 4.0 = 44.8$$

$$N_{mix} = 0.40 \times 3.00 + 0.20 \times 1.60 + 0.30 \times 0.259 + 0.10 \times 0.80$$

$$N_{mix} = 1.20 + 0.32 + 0.0777 + 0.08 = 1.6777$$

**Step 3: Calculate Overall C/N Ratio**

$$(C/N)_{mix} = \frac{44.8}{1.6777} \approx 26.7$$

**Step 4: Verdict**

The overall C/N ratio of **26.7:1** lies within the **optimal composting range of 25:1 to 35:1**. 

**Conclusion:** ✅ **The mixture IS suitable for direct composting without amendment.** The high C/N paper is **balanced** by the low C/N food waste, producing a near-ideal mix for aerobic decomposition.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing bulk density with specific gravity** — Bulk density includes voids; specific gravity is the ratio of waste density to water density (dimensionless).
> 2. **Forgetting the (1 − M_c) and (1 − Ash) correction** when applying Dulong's formula to "as-received" waste. The formula gives **dry, ash-free** value — examiners deduct 2–3 marks if students report this directly.
> 3. **Using wet basis instead of dry basis** for moisture content. KTU prefers **wet basis** unless explicitly stated.
> 4. **Not using `\times` properly in matrix algebra** when handling C/N weighted averages.
> 5. **Missing units** in final answers (always state **kcal/kg** for CV, **kg/m³** for density, **%** for moisture).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and revise 30 minutes before the exam!**

### ✅ Must-Know Definitions
- [ ] **Bulk density** ($\rho_b = M/V$, kg/m³) — includes voids
- [ ] **Moisture content** (wet basis preferred) — oven-dried at 105°C
- [ ] **Volatile solids** — fraction lost at 950°C
- [ ] **Calorific value** — energy per unit mass on complete combustion
- [ ] **C/N ratio** — Carbon to Nitrogen mass ratio
- [ ] **Biodegradability** — fraction decomposed by microbes
- [ ] **Proximate analysis** — Moisture + VM + FC + Ash = 100%
- [ ] **Ultimate analysis** — C + H + O + N + S + Cl = 100%

### ✅ Must-Memorize Numerical Values
- [ ] MSW bulk density: **300–500 kg/m³** (loose) / **600–900 kg/m³** (compacted)
- [ ] MSW moisture content: **20–60%**
- [ ] MSW calorific value: **800–2000 kcal/kg**
- [ ] Optimal C/N for composting: **25:1 to 35:1**
- [ ] Self-sustaining combustion: **>1500 kcal/kg**
- [ ] Typical excess air for incineration: **150–200%**

### ✅ Must-Practice Formulas
- [ ] $M_c = (W_w - W_d)/W_w \times 100$
- [ ] $VS = (W_d - W_{ash})/W_d \times 100$
- [ ] Dulong: $CV = 81.4C + 342.5(H - O/8) + 22.5S$
- [ ] Net CV: $CV_n = CV_g - 9H(2440 - T)/1000$
- [ ] Stoichiometric air: $A = [11.6C + 34.8(H - O/8) + 4.35S]/23.2$

### ✅ Frequently Confused Pairs
- [ ] **Bulk density vs. Specific gravity**
- [ ] **Proximate vs. Ultimate analysis** (engineering vs. elemental)
- [ ] **Gross vs. Net calorific value** (latent heat deduction)
- [ ] **Wet-basis vs. Dry-basis moisture** (always check the question)

### ✅ EIA-Specific Importance
- [ ] Waste characteristics are **mandatory baseline data** for any EIA study
- [ ] **Leachate BOD/COD** values must be reported in EIA for landfill projects
- [ ] **Methane generation potential** is calculated from VS content (LandGEM model)
- [ ] **Calorific value** determines whether incineration is a viable mitigation option in EIA

**END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — CHARACTERISTICS OF SOLID WASTE**
